1.4: vesomach yado al rosh haolah veratzon lo lekapper alav: 'He shall lay his hand on the head of the burnt offering, and it shall be accepted for him to make atonement for him.' The semikah (laying of hands) is the key act of self-identification with the sacrifice: the offerer transfers his identity to the animal, which then represents him before YHWH. The term kopher (ransom/atonement, verb kipper) has its root either in 'to cover' or 'to ransom/substitute' (the latter supported by the Semitic cognate). The NT takes both senses: Christ's blood covers/forgives sin (Col 2:13-14) and he gives his life as a ransom (lytron) for many (Mark 10:45).
1.1: Vayiqra el-Moshe (vayiqra): 'And he called to Moses.' The book's Hebrew title is Vayiqra from this opening word (Greek Leuitikon = 'Levitical things'). The divine calling from the tent of meeting (ohel mo'ed) is not a new revelation but a continuation of Sinai: YHWH now speaks from within the tabernacle just completed. The shift from Sinai's mountain to the tent marks the portability of the covenant — the holy space moves with the people.
1.2: adam ki yaqriv mikem qorban la-YHWH (qorban, H7133): 'When any person among you brings a qorban to YHWH.' The word qorban derives from qarav (to draw near, to approach) — an offering is fundamentally an act of approach, not appeasement. Adam (a person — the generic human term, from adamah = ground) signals the universal scope: any person, not just a priest or official, may bring an offering. The democratization of access to YHWH is built into the vocabulary of sacrifice.
1.3: 'olah (H5930): 'burnt offering.' From alah (to go up, to ascend): the 'olah is the entirely ascending sacrifice — every part goes up in smoke to YHWH, nothing returned to the offerer. Tamim (H8549): 'without defect, blameless' — the same term used of Noah (Gen 6:9 'Noah was tamim') and Abraham (Gen 17:1 'walk before me and be tamim'). Ratzon (H7522): acceptance, favor, goodwill — the sacrifice is presented 'so that it may be accepted for him' (lirtsono). The 'olah is the offering of total dedication: the whole self, symbolized by the whole animal, going up to God.
1.5: shachat (H7819) 'to slaughter' + zaraq (H2236) 'to sprinkle/toss': the two key sacrificial actions distinct from one another — killing and blood-application are separate ritual acts performed by separate parties (the offerer kills; the priests handle the blood). Dam (H1818) 'blood': its application around the altar (al ha-mizbeach saviv) will be explained theologically in Lev 17:11 — 'the life (nefesh) of the flesh is in the blood, and I have given it to you on the altar to make atonement for your souls.'
1.6: hifshit et ha-'olah venitteach otah linetachehah: 'He skins the burnt offering and cuts it into pieces.' Nittach (H5408): to cut into specified pieces — the animal is not burned whole but dismembered into portions. This contrasts with the bird offering (vv. 14-17) which is not cut apart. The skinning and portioning is the offerer's work; the arrangement and burning belong to the priests. The labor division reflects the collaborative nature of the sacrifice: offerer and priest each have required roles.
1.7: venatan bene Aharon ha-kohen esh al ha-mizbeach: Aaron's sons arrange fire on the altar and wood upon the fire. The altar fire is divinely kindled (Lev 9:24 fire came out from before YHWH) and must never go out (Lev 6:13 'esh tamid' = perpetual fire). The priests' role in arranging wood ensures the fire is maintained through human stewardship of what God initiated. The combination of divine origin and human maintenance is characteristic of covenant institutions.
1.8: rosh (H7218) 'head' + peder (H6309) 'the fat pieces/suet': the head and suet are arranged on the burning wood in a specific order (ve'archu = 'they arrange in order'). The technical precision of the ritual ordering reflects its communicative function: a properly arranged sacrifice speaks; an improperly arranged one does not. The priests as arranged-order specialists is what makes the sacrificial system communicative rather than merely transactional.
1.9: qerev (H7130) 'inner parts/entrails' and karaim (H3767) 'legs': washed with water before burning. The washing of the internal organs and legs addresses the parts most visibly soiled — the contents of the gut and the dirt of travel. Even in sacrifice, ritual purity is addressed before the offering ascends. Reah nihoah (H7381+H5207): 'soothing/pleasing aroma' — a metaphorical idiom for what satisfies the divine response. The term nihoah derives from nuach (to rest, settle) — the aroma is what causes YHWH's response to settle/rest from potential judgment. Used first of Noah (Gen 8:21), where the same phrase appears after the flood.
1.10: tzon (H6629): 'flock' — sheep or goats. The graded sacrificial system moves from large animals (herd, v.2) to small animals (flock, v.10) to birds (v.14), calibrated to the offerer's means. This graduated access ensures that no one is excluded from the 'olah by economic constraint. The same animal standard applies regardless of socioeconomic class: tamim zachar (a blameless male) across all categories.
1.11: ve'shachat oto al yerekh ha-mizbeach tzafonah: slaughter on the north side of the altar. The north-side specification for the 'olah and later the chattat (Lev 4:24, 6:25) is never explicitly explained in the text. Rabbinic tradition associated the north with divine judgment (cf. Jer 1:14 'from the north disaster will be poured out'); this may encode the direction of sacrificial atonement. The spatial precision of the ritual — specific sides, specific movements — maps the tabernacle as a geography of meaning.
1.12: linetiachehah: into its pieces — repeated from v.6 to confirm the cutting procedure applies to flock animals as well as cattle. The portions specified: head, fat (peder), and remaining pieces (netachim). The arrangement is identical regardless of the animal's size — the ritual form is stable even as the material scales down. Consistency of form across economic categories reinforces that the significance is in the act, not the magnitude of the gift.
1.13: qerev vekaraim (inner organs and legs) again washed with water — repeated from v.9 for flock animals. The refrain of washing before burning and the formula reah nihoah la-YHWH marks the conclusion of each variant: the outcome is the same regardless of the animal used. The liturgical repetition is not redundancy but reinforcement: the system of access is genuinely available at all levels.
1.14: 'olah min ha-'of ('of, H5775): 'burnt offering from birds (turtledove or young pigeon).' Tor (H8449, turtledove) and ben-yonah (H1469, young pigeon) are the most economical sacrifice possible — the provision for the very poor. Lev 12:8 allows birds as an alternative to a lamb for new mothers who cannot afford a sheep; Luke 2:24 records that Mary and Joseph brought 'a pair of turtledoves or two young pigeons' — the offering of those without means, identifying Jesus with the economically poor from birth.
1.15: malaph et rosho (malaph, H4454): 'to wring off the head' — a specific technique for bird sacrifice distinct from the knife-slaughter used for mammals. The blood is drained against the side of the altar (himatzer). Birds are not skinned or portioned but prepared whole. The unique method for bird sacrifice — itself a unique provision for those who cannot afford animals — shows that the system is adapted to the offerer's condition at every level, not applied uniformly regardless of context.
1.16: muria (H4239): the crop/craw (digestive organ of the bird); notzato (H5133): feathers or wing-tips. These are removed and thrown by the altar at the ash heap on the east side (miqom ha-deshen). The bird's digestive contents (which would be unclean) are discarded before the offering ascends. The separation of the digestible interior from the ascent portion maintains the purity of what goes up to YHWH even in the least expensive offering category.
1.17: shissa oto vikhnafav: 'He splits it at its wings without dividing it completely.' Unlike animal offerings, the bird is not cut apart — it is torn open at the wings and spread flat for burning. The result is a flattened, opened bird laid on the altar wood. Reah nihoah la-YHWH closes the chapter: regardless of whether the offering is a bull or a pigeon, the formula is the same. The theological outcome of the sacrifice — YHWH's soothing response — is not indexed to the economic value of the offering.

16.2: Yom Kippur (Day of Atonement) is the annual reset of Israel's covenant relationship: the accumulated sin of the year is dealt with by the high priest's double ritual — (1) the bull's blood for his own sin, (2) one goat killed for the people's sin (the sacrificed goat), (3) one goat bearing the sins symbolically into the wilderness (the scapegoat, azazel). The two-goat ritual together communicates the complete theology of atonement: blood-payment (the dead goat) + removal (the scapegoat). Christ fulfills both aspects: his blood makes propitiation and his bearing of sin removes it ('as far as the east is from the west, so far does he remove our transgressions from us', Ps 103:12).
16.1: The Yom Kippur legislation is grounded in the death of Nadab and Abihu (Lev 10:1-2), who entered YHWH's presence without authorization. The historical reference (aḥarei mot shney bnei Aharon, 'after the death of the two sons of Aaron') sets the chapter in the aftermath of a catastrophic priestly failure. Yom Kippur is the divinely-given solution: an annual, authorized, highly regulated pathway into the divine presence, structured to prevent the death that comes from unauthorized approach.
16.3: bezot yavo Aharon el-haqqodesh: 'In this way Aaron shall enter the holy place.' The emphatic demonstrative bezot (in this / by this) marks the entry protocol as the sole legitimate pathway. The two animals: par ben-baqar (young bull) for the sin offering (ḥatta't) for Aaron's own sin, and a ram for the burnt offering (olah). The priest must address his own impurity before mediating for the people — a point Hebrews exploits to contrast Christ, who had no sin of his own (Heb 7:27).
16.4: ketonet-bad qodesh yilbash: 'He shall put on the holy linen tunic.' On Yom Kippur the high priest exchanges his normal elaborate golden vestments (Exod 28) for simple white linen — bad (linen) repeated four times in this verse. The linen is white (lavan), associated with purity. The high priest enters the holy of holies not in his symbols of status but in symbols of purity — a reversal of normal priestly display. The four linen garments all belong to YHWH (bigdei qodesh, holy garments).
16.5: From the congregation: two male goats (se'irei izzim) for the sin offering and one ram for the burnt offering. The two goats are a single sin offering taken together — they function as a paired unit representing the two aspects of atonement: blood-payment (the slaughtered goat) and removal (the scapegoat). One offering, two mechanisms. This two-part structure will be explained through vv. 15-22.
16.6: vehiqriv Aharon et-par haḥatta't asher-lo vekhipper ba'ado uvead beito: 'Aaron shall offer the bull of his sin offering and make atonement for himself and his household.' The sequential structure requires the priest's own atonement first — he cannot mediate for others until he himself has been cleansed. Bead beito (for his household) includes his wife and all dependent family members. The necessity of priestly self-atonement is the main contrast point of Heb 7:27: 'He has no need, like those high priests, to offer sacrifices daily, first for his own sins and then for those of the people.'
16.7: The two goats are presented together at the sanctuary entrance. The paired presentation before YHWH is necessary before lot-casting — both goats belong to the ritual simultaneously; they are not yet differentiated. The structural parallel with the two birds in the leper's cleansing (Lev 14:4-7) is noted by later interpreters: one is killed, one released; both together represent cleansing.
16.8: venatan Aharon al-shnei hase'irim goralot: 'Aaron shall cast lots over the two goats.' Goralot (lots) — the method is not described precisely; later tradition (Mishnah Yoma) used two inscribed gold tablets. The critical term: azazel. This is the most debated term in Leviticus. Three main interpretations: (1) a place name ('land of removal/desolation'); (2) a compound meaning 'goat of removal' or 'complete removal' (ez-azal); (3) a supernatural entity — a wilderness demon or fallen angel to whom the goat is dispatched (supported by 1 Enoch, Dead Sea Scrolls, and the parallel with goat-demons in 17:7). The MKT renders azazel as a proper name, leaving the ambiguity intact.
16.9: The goat assigned to YHWH by lot becomes the sin offering — it is slaughtered, its blood brought inside the veil. This is the propitiatory goat: its blood atones in the sanctuary, cleansing the holy place from accumulated Israelite impurity. The lot procedure ensures that neither goat is pre-selected by human choice — the assignment is YHWH's own determination.
16.10: veha-sa'ir asher ala alav hagoral la'aza'zel ya'amod-ḥai lifnei YHWH lekapper alav: 'The goat on which the lot for Azazel fell shall be presented alive before YHWH to make atonement over it.' The live goat's function is also atonement (kipper) — but its mechanism is different: not blood-sprinkling but confession and removal. It is first presented before YHWH (legitimizing the ritual), then used for confession, then released. The word ḥai (alive) emphasizes that this goat does not die in the ritual; its death outside the camp (or at the cliff, per later tradition) is incidental to its function.
16.11: Aaron slaughters his bull first — before the people's goat — demonstrating the sequential logic: self-atonement must precede intercession. Vekhipper ba'ado uvead beito (and he shall make atonement for himself and his household) is repeated from v. 6, reinforcing the priority of priestly cleansing. The blood of Aaron's bull will be used inside the holy of holies (v. 14).
16.12: veleqaḥ melo-hammaḥtah gaḥalei-esh me'al hamizbeaḥ: 'He shall take a censer full of burning coals from the altar.' The incense censer is the high priest's shield for entering the holy of holies: he carries coals from the bronze altar and two handfuls of finely ground fragrant incense (qetoret sammim daqah). The incense cloud (anan heqetoret) will cover the kapporet, protecting the priest from seeing YHWH directly. The smoke is protective, not merely ceremonial — velo yamut (and he shall not die) is the goal.
16.13: venatan et-haqetoret al-ha'esh lifnei YHWH vekissa anan-haqetoret et-hakapporet: 'He shall put the incense on the fire before YHWH so that the cloud of incense covers the atonement cover.' The incense cloud functions as a veil between the priest's sight and the divine presence localized at the kapporet. The theology: YHWH's presence is genuinely located here, but direct exposure is lethal. The incense mediates access. Later tradition (Mishnah Yoma 5:1) preserves Pharisaic debate over whether the incense should be lit before or after entering the veil — a halakhic dispute reflecting the verse's precise sequence.
16.14: velakaḥ middam hapar vehizza beetzba'o al-pnei hakapporet qedmah: 'He shall take some of the bull's blood and sprinkle it with his finger on the front of the atonement cover eastward.' The blood is applied to the kapporet (the divine throne) directly. The direction: qedmah (eastward/on the east face) — once on the face of the kapporet itself, then seven times before it on the floor. Seven is the completion number (Gen 1). The blood-sprinkling on and before the kapporet is the central act of the entire Yom Kippur ritual — it is where atonement happens at its most concentrated point.
16.15: The same blood-sprinkling procedure is repeated with the people's goat blood inside the holy of holies. Aaron sprinkles bull blood (for his own sin) and goat blood (for the people's sin) on and before the kapporet in the same sequence. The doubled application — priest's blood then people's blood — makes Yom Kippur atonement comprehensive: both priesthood and congregation are cleansed from sin accumulated during the year.
16.16: vekhipper al-haqqodesh mitum'ot bnei Yisrael umippish'eihem lekhol-ḥatotam: 'He shall make atonement for the holy place because of the uncleannesses of the Israelites and because of their transgressions, for all their sins.' Three categories are distinguished: tumot (ritual uncleannesses — the accumulated impurity from birth, death, bodily flows), psha'im (willful transgressions/rebellions), ḥatot (sins generally). The holy place itself has been contaminated by Israel's sins even though they never entered it — the sanctuary absorbs impurity from the camp. Yom Kippur cleanses the sanctuary as much as the people.
16.17: vekhol-adam lo-yihyeh be'ohel mo'ed bevo'o lekapper baqqodesh: 'No person shall be in the tent of meeting when he enters to make atonement in the holy place.' Total isolation of the high priest during the inner-sanctuary work. This is the moment of greatest solemnity — the entire community waits outside while the single mediator stands before YHWH. The exclusion of 'all persons' (kol-adam) reinforces the uniqueness of the high-priestly role. The NT will use this structure: Christ enters the true holy of holies alone, as the sole mediator (1 Tim 2:5; Heb 9:7, 11-12).
16.18: veyatsa el-hamizbeaḥ asher lifnei YHWH vekhipper alav: 'He shall come out to the altar that is before YHWH and make atonement for it.' Having cleansed the inner sanctuary, the high priest moves outward: first the altar of incense (implied), then the bronze altar. The outward movement is spatially significant — atonement radiates from the most holy place outward through the entire sanctuary complex. Blood from both animals is mixed and applied to the four horns of the altar.
16.19: vehizza alav min-hadam beetzba'o sheva pe'amim: 'He shall sprinkle some of the blood on it with his finger seven times.' The sevenfold sprinkling (also at v. 14) is the completion number — seven applications ensure comprehensive cleansing. Veteherhu veqiddesho mitum'ot bnei Yisrael (cleansing it and consecrating it from the uncleannesses of the Israelites): the altar's dual state change — from impure to clean, from ordinary to consecrated — accomplished by blood application.
16.20: The transition from sanctuary-cleansing to scapegoat: the live goat is now summoned. The sequencing is deliberate — the sanctuary must be cleansed first; only then is the confession ritual performed over the live goat. The two mechanisms address different aspects: the blood deals with impurity in the sanctuary; the scapegoat deals with iniquity symbolically removed from the community.
16.21: vesomakh Aharon et-shetei yadav al-rosh hasa'ir haḥai vehitvaddah alav: 'Aaron shall lay both his hands on the head of the live goat and confess over it all the iniquities of the Israelites.' The semikah here uses both hands (shetei yadav) — the intensive form, reserving full weight and identification for this act. The verbal confession (viddui) spoken over the goat is the mechanism of transfer: the named iniquities of Israel are laid onto the goat. Kol-avonot bnei Yisrael (all the iniquities of the Israelites) + kol-pisha'eihem lekhol-ḥatotam (all their transgressions, all their sins) — the triple formula covers everything.
16.22: venasa hasa'ir alav et-kol-avonotam el-erets gezerah: 'The goat shall carry all their iniquities to a land cut off.' Nasa (to carry/bear) is the same verb used for bearing sin throughout the OT and in Isa 53:12 (vehu ḥet-rabbim nasa, 'he bore the sins of many'). Erets gezerah (land cut off / desolate/isolated land) — the goat goes to a place of complete separation. The removal is spatial and irreversible: the community's sins go away, carried by a living being, to a place from which there is no return.
16.23: Aaron re-enters the tent of meeting to remove the Yom Kippur linen garments and leave them inside — they are too holy for ordinary use and cannot re-enter the camp. He then bathes and puts on his regular high-priestly vestments before offering the burnt offerings. The change of garments marks the transition from the extraordinary (holy of holies entry) back to the ordinary high-priestly ministry.
16.24: Aaron bathes in the holy place, dresses in his regular garments, and goes out to offer his own and the people's burnt offerings (olot). The burnt offerings (one ram each for Aaron and the congregation) complete the Yom Kippur complex — the sin offerings cleanse; the burnt offerings restore the relationship and express total dedication. The sequence: cleansing → restoration.
16.25: The fat (ḥelev) of the sin-offering animals is burned on the altar — the portion belonging to YHWH. Even on Yom Kippur, the standard rule that fat belongs to YHWH is maintained (Lev 3:16-17). The burning of fat is a reiaḥ niḥoaḥ (pleasing aroma) offering even when the main blood-work has been unusual.
16.26: The man who led the scapegoat away (meshalaḥ hasa'ir la'aza'zel) becomes ritually impure through the act — he has been the vehicle by which Israel's sins were conveyed outside the camp. He must wash his clothes and bathe before returning. The impurity is not moral guilt but ritual contamination acquired by handling the sin-laden goat.
16.27: The carcasses of the bull and the goat (whose blood was brought into the holy place) must be taken outside the camp and burned — skin, flesh, and dung. The fire outside the camp (miḥuts lammaḥaneh) is the disposal mechanism for the most holy sin-offering animals whose blood entered the inner sanctuary. This rule is cited by Heb 13:11-12 as the type of Christ suffering 'outside the gate.'
16.28: The man who burns the carcasses also becomes unclean and must wash and bathe. Three people acquire impurity through Yom Kippur rituals: (1) the scapegoat handler (v. 26), (2) the carcass-burner (v. 28), and (3) implicitly Aaron himself requires bathing (v. 24). The entire ritual generates contagion in the participants — the mediation of sin is not cost-free even for those facilitating atonement.
16.29: vehayetah lachem leḥuqqat olam baḥodesh hashvei'i be'asor laḥodesh te'annu et-nafshoteikhem: 'This shall be a statute forever for you: on the tenth day of the seventh month you shall practice self-denial.' Inui nefesh (affliction/self-denial of soul) is the biblical prescription for Yom Kippur fasting — the noun innui (affliction) from the verb anah (to afflict/humble). Rabbinic tradition identified five deprivations: no eating, no drinking, no bathing, no anointing, no marital relations. Leḥuqqat olam (a statute forever) marks this as a permanent covenant obligation.
16.30: ki vayom hazeh yekhapper aleikhem letaher etkhem mikol ḥatoteikhem lifnei YHWH titharu: 'For on this day atonement shall be made for you to cleanse you; from all your sins before YHWH you shall be clean.' The climactic statement of Yom Kippur's purpose: comprehensive cleansing (taher) from all sins. Lifnei YHWH (before YHWH) — the cleansing is not merely social or psychological but covenantal and relational, occurring in YHWH's presence. The passive titharu (you shall be clean) emphasizes that cleansing is something done to Israel, not accomplished by Israel's effort.
16.31: Shabbat Shabbaton hi lachem: 'It is a Shabbat of complete rest for you.' Shabbat Shabbaton is the superlative construction (Sabbath of Sabbaths = the ultimate/complete Sabbath) — the highest intensity of the Sabbath principle. Yom Kippur is the only day in the Torah calendar given this designation, shared only with the seventh-year land sabbath (Lev 25:4) and one other observance. The phrase combines the annual atonement calendar with the weekly rest rhythm, marking it as a peak moment in Israel's entire temporal structure.
16.32: The law applies to any legitimately anointed and ordained high priest, not only to Aaron personally — asher-yimshaḥ oto vaasher yemale yadav (who is anointed and whose hand is filled) = the full ordination formula. This forward-looking clause establishes Yom Kippur as an ongoing priestly institution, not a one-time event tied to Aaron's personal ministry.
16.33: The comprehensive scope of Yom Kippur atonement: the holy sanctuary (miqdash haqqodesh), the tent of meeting, the altar, the priests, and all the congregation of Israel (kol-am haqqahal). Atonement radiates outward from the holy of holies through every layer of the sanctuary and community. No part of Israel's covenant structure is excluded from the annual cleansing.
16.34: lekhapper al-bnei Yisrael mikol-ḥatotam aḥat bashanah: 'to make atonement for Israel from all their sins once a year.' Aḥat bashanah (once a year) — the limitation is the key theological datum exploited by Hebrews: the annual repetition proves that Yom Kippur could not achieve permanent removal of sin (Heb 10:1-3). If the sacrifice had accomplished its goal, the worshippers would have been cleansed once for all and the annual ritual would have been unnecessary. The repetition is the system's built-in indicator that it was provisional, designed to point forward to a once-for-all (ephapax) sacrifice.

17.11: ki nefesh habasar badam hi vaani netatihu lachem al hamizbeach lekapper al nafshoteichem ki hadam hu bakafra banefesh: 'For the life [nefesh] of the flesh is in the blood, and I have given it for you on the altar to make atonement for your souls [nefesh], for it is the blood that makes atonement by the life.' The blood-for-life exchange is the theological axiom underlying all sacrifice: blood (= life) offered for life (= the offerer's forfeit life). The principle is stated as divine appointment — 'I have given it for you on the altar' — sacrifice is not a human invention but a divinely ordained mechanism of atonement. Hebrews 9:22's 'without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins' is a direct application of Lev 17:11 to Christ's death.
17.1: Standard address formula. Chapter 17 opens the Holiness Code's initial layer — the blood laws that regulate all animal slaughter and consumption. The broad addressee (Aaron, sons, all Israel) signals a universal obligation extending beyond the sanctuary to all Israelite life.
17.2: The law extends to 'all the people of Israel' (kol-bnei Yisrael) — not merely the priests — and specifically names the act of slaughtering (yishḥat) livestock. The law governs not just ritual sacrifice but ordinary butchering: in the wilderness period, no animal may be slaughtered outside the sanctuary.
17.3: ish ish mibbeit Yisrael asher yishḥat: 'any man of Israel who slaughters.' The repeated ish ish (man, man = anyone/whoever) is the legal universalizing formula in Leviticus. The three animals: shor (ox/bull), keves (lamb), ez (goat) — the domesticated animals that can be offered sacrificially. The rule applies regardless of location: inside or outside the camp.
17.4: vedam yiḥasheiv laish hahu dam shafakh: 'blood shall be reckoned to that man — he has shed blood.' Anyone who slaughters without bringing the animal to the sanctuary entrance is guilty of shefikh dam (bloodshed) — treated like murder. This is because unauthorized slaughter severs the connection between killing and atonement that the sanctuary system maintains. Venikh'rat haish hahu miqqerev ammo: 'that man shall be cut off from among his people' — the karet penalty, which represents the most severe divine punishment short of execution.
17.5: The purpose of centralizing slaughter at the sanctuary: lema'an asher yavi'u bnei Yisrael et-ziḥeihem (so that the Israelites may bring their sacrifices). The word ziḥeihem (their sacrifices, from zevaḥ) refers to what they were previously offering in the open field — slaughter-meals that had a quasi-sacrificial character. By requiring all such slaughter to come to the sanctuary, YHWH absorbs and Torahizes the universal human impulse to mark killing with ritual.
17.6: The priest throws (zaraq) the blood against the altar — the verb is distinctive, suggesting a vigorous throwing action rather than careful application. The fat is burned as a reiaḥ niḥoaḥ (pleasing aroma). By requiring all slaughter to flow through this mechanism, every meat meal in Israel becomes a form of communion with YHWH.
17.7: velo-yizbeḥu od et-ziḥeihem lasse'irim asher hem zonim aḥarei-hem: 'They shall no longer sacrifice their offerings to the goat-demons (se'irim) after whom they have been whoring.' Se'irim (goat-demons, sg. sa'ir) — the term for the semi-divine wilderness beings associated with arid places and disorder (Isa 13:21, 34:14). The use of zonim (whoring/prostituting) for idolatry is the standard prophetic language for covenant unfaithfulness. The law addresses a real syncretistic practice in Israel's past.
17.8: The law extends to resident aliens (ger) — the foreigner living among Israel is subject to the same slaughter centralization rule. This is a recurring pattern in the Holiness Code: the resident alien is included in Israel's covenant obligations, not just Israel's covenant privileges. Ger haggur betokham (the alien who sojourns among them) cannot maintain a parallel pagan sacrificial system.
17.9: The penalty for unauthorized sacrifice (even of permitted animals) is karet — being cut off from the people. This applies equally to native Israelite and resident alien. The equivalence of native and alien under the blood laws reflects the Holiness Code's consistent expansion of covenant obligations beyond ethnic Israel.
17.10: veish ish mibbeit Yisrael umin-hager hagar betokham asher yokhal kol-dam: 'any man of Israel or resident alien who eats any blood.' The blood prohibition is broader than the slaughter law: it applies to any blood in any form. The penalty: unatati fanai banaefesh ha'okhelat et-hadam (I will set my face against the person who eats blood and cut him off) — YHWH's own personal attention is the enforcement mechanism. This is the most direct form of divine sanction in Leviticus, reserved for fundamental violations.
17.12: Summary restatement of the blood prohibition with the same universalizing ish ish formula. The double prohibition (no native, no alien) emphasizes that the blood taboo transcends ethnic and covenant boundaries — it reflects something about blood's intrinsic association with life that makes consumption an offense against the life-principle itself.
17.13: asher yatsud tseid ḥayyah o-oph asher ye'akhel: 'who hunts a game animal or bird that may be eaten.' Game animals (hunted rather than domesticated) are subject to a special blood rule: since their blood cannot be brought to the altar (they are not slaughter animals), it must be poured out and covered with earth (ve'ikissahu be'afar). Shafakh dam (poured out) + kissa (covered) — the blood is given back to the earth, acknowledging that it belongs to YHWH even when it cannot go to the altar.
17.14: ki nefesh kol-basar damo venafshoto hu: 'the life of every creature — its blood is its life.' Restatement of the v. 11 principle applied to game animals: the blood-prohibition follows from the blood-life equation. The phrase kol-asher yokhlenu yikkareth (whoever eats it shall be cut off) applies the karet penalty uniformly across all animal categories.
17.15: vekhol-nefesh asher tokhal nevelah uterefa ba'eziret uvagger: 'Every person — native or alien — who eats an animal that died naturally or was torn.' Nevelah (naturally dead) and terefa (torn by wild animals) — the two categories of non-slaughtered death. Eating them generates ritual impurity requiring clothes-washing and bathing; the person remains unclean until evening. This is a graduated penalty compared to the karet of v. 14 — eating from natural death is less severe than eating blood, but still requires purification.
17.16: ve'im lo yekhabbeis veguffo lo yirḥats venasa avono: 'if he does not wash his clothes and does not bathe his body, he shall bear his iniquity.' The responsibility for purification lies with the individual: failure to perform the ritual washing does not remove the impurity but makes the person culpable for remaining in an impure state. Venasa avono (he shall bear his iniquity) — the language of personal moral accountability applied to a ritual-purity context.

19.18: veahavta lereiacha kamocha ani YHWH: 'You shall love your neighbor as yourself: I am YHWH.' The neighbor-love command concludes with the divine self-identification formula (ani YHWH) — the obligation is grounded not in a social contract but in YHWH's own character and authority. The scope of rea (neighbor) in Leviticus 19 extends throughout the chapter to include resident aliens (v. 34: 'love him as yourself, for you were aliens in Egypt'). Jesus's parable of the Good Samaritan expands the neighbor-category to include the unexpected outsider, but the Levitical warrant is already broader than its surface reading.
19.1: YHWH spoke to Moses — the revelation formula opens the Holiness Code's ethical summit, ch 19, which gathers the widest range of social, ritual, and agricultural legislation under a single theological principle.
19.2: Qedoshim tihyu ki qadosh ani YHWH Eloheichem — be holy (qedoshim) for I, YHWH your God, am holy. This is the foundational axiom of Lev 17–26: holiness is not primarily ritual separation but participation in the divine character. The plural qedoshim addresses the entire congregation (edah) of Israel, not only the priests; holiness is a communal, not merely sacerdotal, vocation.
19.3: Each of you shall revere (tira'u) his mother and his father, and you shall keep my Sabbaths — the two primary relational loyalties (family and divine rhythm) are placed at the head of the chapter's legislation. The reversal of the Decalogue's order (mother before father, Exod 20:12: father first) is deliberate, noting the mother's authority deserves explicit acknowledgment.
19.4: Do not turn to idols (elilim) and do not make for yourselves cast gods — elilim is a wordplay on el (God/strength): idols are non-gods, nothings. The plural command echoes the Decalogue's first two commandments (Exod 20:3-4), which the chapter's structure deliberately recapitulates.
19.5: When you offer a peace offering (zevach shelamim) to YHWH, you shall offer it so that you may be accepted — the peace offering legislation (vv.5-8) enforces the time limit from Lev 7:15-18: the fellowship offering must be eaten within two days; eating on the third day renders it an abomination (pigul).
19.6: It shall be eaten on the day you offer it or on the day after, and anything left over until the third day shall be burned up with fire — the two-day window (day of offering + next day) applies to the toda (thanksgiving) variant (Lev 7:15) and the neder/nadavah (vow/freewill) variant (Lev 7:16); burning the remainder prevents fermentation and ritual contamination.
19.7: If it is eaten at all on the third day, it is tainted (pigul); it will not be accepted — pigul (foul, abomination of ritual impurity) is a technical term for an offering rendered void by improper timing or wrong intention; it cannot generate atonement for the offerer even if consumed.
19.8: And everyone who eats it shall bear his iniquity (avono yissa), because he has profaned what is holy to YHWH, and that person shall be cut off (venikhreta) from his people — eating pigul activates the karet penalty, which applies to high-handed violations of sacred boundaries throughout Leviticus. The eating of a ritually void sacrifice extends its defilement to the eater.
19.9: When you harvest the harvest of your land, you shall not reap your field right up to its edge (pe'ah sadekha), nor shall you gather the gleanings after your harvest — the pe'ah (corner/edge) law requires leaving unharvested strips at field edges; the leket (gleaning) law prohibits picking up stalks that fall during reaping. Both provisions create a legal agricultural safety net for the poor and sojourner.
19.10: And you shall not strip your vineyard bare (lo te'olel), neither shall you gather the fallen grapes of your vineyard. You shall leave them for the poor and for the sojourner — the vineyard parallel to the field law (v.9): olel (small clusters missed in harvest) and peret (individual fallen grapes) must be left. Ruth 2 exemplifies the gleaning system working as intended: Boaz's field follows these laws.
19.11: You shall not steal (lo tignovu). You shall not deal falsely (lo techachedu). You shall not lie (lo teshakru) to one another — three verbs form a progression from action (theft) to relational act (dealing falsely) to speech (lying). The placement after the gleaning laws and before the oath laws connects economic, relational, and verbal integrity as a single ethical domain.
19.12: You shall not swear by my name falsely (lo tishave'u bishmi lashaqer), and so profane the name of your God — invoking YHWH's name in a false oath treats the divine name as a guarantee for a lie, profaning (cillel) the Name's character. This is the Decalogue's third commandment (Exod 20:7: do not take the name of YHWH in vain) restated in oath-context form.
19.13: You shall not oppress your neighbor (lo ta'ashoq) or rob him. The wages of a hired worker shall not remain with you all night until the morning — withholding wages overnight exploits the day-laborer who depends on daily payment for daily food (cf. Deut 24:14-15; Jas 5:4). The prohibition on oshek (oppression) and gazal (robbery) frames wage theft as economic violence against neighbors.
19.14: You shall not curse the deaf (lo teqallel cheresh) or put a stumbling block before the blind (ve-lifney iver lo titten mikhshol), but you shall fear your God — exploiting vulnerability that the victim cannot perceive (the deaf cannot hear the curse; the blind cannot see the obstacle) is precisely what fear of God prohibits. The two examples represent all forms of taking advantage of those who cannot self-defend.
19.15: You shall do no injustice (lo ta'asu avel) in judgment. You shall not be partial to the poor (lo tissa feney-dal) or defer to the great (lo tehdar feney gadol), but in righteousness shall you judge your neighbor — judicial impartiality is required in both directions: not showing pity to the poor (which distorts justice) and not deferring to the powerful (which sells justice). Tzedek (righteousness) requires consistent standard regardless of social status.
19.16: You shall not go around as a slanderer (lo telech rachil) among your people, and you shall not stand up against the life (dam) of your neighbor — rachil (slanderer/gossip, literally 'trader/peddler') suggests someone who traffics in others' reputations. The second clause (lo ta'amod al-dam re'ekha, do not stand against your neighbor's blood) prohibits either false accusation that could result in execution or failure to act when a neighbor's life is in danger.
19.17: You shall not hate your brother in your heart (lo-tisna et-achikha bilvavekha). You shall reason frankly with your neighbor (hokheach tokhiach et-amitekha), lest you incur sin because of him — the internal dimension of community ethics: the heart-hatred that never erupts in overt conflict but festers. The antidote is direct, honest rebuke (tokhach) rather than suppressed resentment — a theme central to both Matt 18:15-17 and rabbinic ethics (Avot 2:10).
19.19: You shall keep my statutes (chuqqotai tishmoru). You shall not let your cattle breed with a different kind (kil'ayim). You shall not sow your field with two kinds of seed (sha'atnez), nor shall you wear a garment of cloth made of two kinds of material — the kil'ayim (two-kinds, hybrid) prohibitions: animal crossbreeding, mixed-seed planting, and mixed-fiber weaving. These laws enforce created categories (Gen 1: each after its kind), treating the blurring of boundaries as a violation of creation order rather than a mere ritual taboo.
19.20: If a man lies sexually with a woman who is a slave, assigned to another man but not yet ransomed or given her freedom, a distinction shall be made — the slave-concubine case: she is betrothed or designated but not yet legally free. The case is treated as a crime requiring biqqoret (inquiry/investigation), with a guilt offering but not death, because her slave status means she does not have the full legal capacity to be held to the capital standard of Lev 20:10.
19.21: He shall bring his guilt offering (asham) to YHWH, to the entrance of the tent of meeting — the asham is the reparation offering for violations involving sacred property or rights (Lev 5:14-6:7). The man has violated the woman's assigned betrothal-right; the guilt offering covers this reparable violation.
19.22: And the priest shall make atonement for him with the ram of the guilt offering before YHWH for his sin that he has committed, and he shall be forgiven for the sin that he has committed — the standard atonement-forgiveness formula; despite the severity of the violation (sexual exploitation of a protected woman), atonement is available through the asham, unlike the cases in Lev 20 that carry the death penalty.
19.23: When you come into the land and plant any kind of tree for food, you shall regard its fruit as forbidden (areltem orlato). Three years it shall be forbidden to you; it must not be eaten — orlah (foreskin/uncircumcision) is the term for the fruit of years 1–3; treating it as uncircumcised (forbidden, consecrated away) allows the tree to establish root structure without premature harvesting. The agricultural wisdom is embedded in purity language.
19.24: And in the fourth year all its fruit shall be holy (qodesh hillulim), an offering of praise to YHWH — the fourth year's fruit is entirely consecrated to YHWH, brought to Jerusalem (cf. Deut 14:23) as first-fruit acknowledgment. Hillulim (praise, celebration) links the fourth-year offering to rejoicing rather than mere obligation.
19.25: But in the fifth year you may eat of its fruit, to increase its yield for you — the five-year cycle (1-3 forbidden, 4th consecrated, 5th for eating) creates a development sequence: YHWH receives first; then the farmer benefits. The promise lehosif lachem tevuato (to increase its yield for you) grounds the delayed gratification in covenantal blessing.
19.26: You shall not eat any flesh with the blood in it (al ha-dam). You shall not interpret omens (lo tenacheshu) or tell fortunes (lo te'onenu) — blood-eating prohibition (cf. Lev 17:10-14; Deut 12:16) precedes the divination prohibitions; both treat illegitimate access to life-force (blood) and forbidden knowledge (omens) as violations of divine sovereignty over life.
19.27: You shall not round off the hair on your temples (lo taqifu pe'at roshechem) or mar the edges of your beard (ve-lo tashchit et pe'at zeqanekha) — mourning rites involving specific beard/hair cutting are prohibited (cf. Lev 21:5; Deut 14:1); these were associated with pagan mortuary practices. The prohibition preserves Israelite distinctiveness from Canaanite grief rites.
19.28: You shall not make any cuts on your body for the dead (seret la-nefesh) or tattoo yourselves (uktovat qa'aqa lo titenu bachem); I am YHWH — gashing the body for the dead (cf. 1 Kgs 18:28 for the prophets of Baal) and permanent body marking both associated with pagan mourning/devotion practices. The divine self-identification formula (ani YHWH) terminates the clause, grounding the prohibition in covenant identity.
19.29: Do not profane your daughter by making her a prostitute (lo techallel et-bitkha lehaznotah), lest the land fall into prostitution and the land become full of depravity (zimah) — sacred or cultic prostitution (common in Canaanite fertility religion) would have been the proximate reference; but the prohibition extends to any exploitation of one's daughter for sexual gain. The connection to the land becoming full of zimah links sexual ethics to territorial holiness (cf. Lev 18:24-28).
19.30: You shall keep my Sabbaths (et-shabtotai tishmoru) and reverence my sanctuary (miqdashi tira'u); I am YHWH — Sabbath observance and sanctuary reverence form a paired refrain (cf. Lev 26:2) that frames the ethical legislation of the Holiness Code; time (Sabbath) and space (sanctuary) are the two axes of holy order around which all ethical life is organized.
19.31: Do not turn to mediums (ha-ovot) or necromancers (ha-yidde'onim); do not seek them out to be defiled by them — ov (medium/spirit of the dead) and yidde'oni (familiar spirit/wizard) are the two terms used in Lev 20:27 (capital offense) and 1 Sam 28:7-8 (Saul's consultation at En-dor). Consulting the dead usurps YHWH's monopoly on revelation and knowledge.
19.32: You shall stand up before the gray head (mipenei seva taqum) and honor the face of an old man (ve-hadarta peney zaqen), and you shall fear your God — respect for the elderly is grounded in fear of God, not merely social convention; the same divine self-identification formula (ani YHWH) that grounds the prohibition on idolatry also grounds respect for the aged. The community's attitude toward its most vulnerable members reflects its theological character.
19.33: When a stranger (ger) sojourns with you in your land, you shall not do him wrong (lo tonu oto) — the ger (resident alien, sojourner) lacks clan protection; the prohibition on oppression (yanh) is the first of a paired command, with the positive counterpart in v.34. Israel's own sojourn in Egypt is the experiential ground for sojourner ethics throughout the Torah.
19.34: You shall treat the stranger who sojourns with you as the native among you, and you shall love him as yourself (ve-ahavta lo kamocha), for you were strangers in the land of Egypt — the sojourner-love command uses the identical formulation as the neighbor-love command of v.18 (ve-ahavta le-re'akha kamocha). The application of the universal principle to the specific case of the outsider expands the covenant community's ethical radius.
19.35: You shall do no wrong (lo ta'asu avel) in judgment, in measures of length or weight or quantity — the just-weights legislation (vv.35-36) applies the anti-injustice principle of v.15 (judicial integrity) to commercial transactions; avel (injustice) covers both legal and commercial fraud, treating dishonest scales as a form of perversion of justice.
19.36: You shall have just balances (mozney tzedeq), just weights (avney tzedeq), a just ephah (eifah tzedek), and a just hin (hin tzedeq); I am YHWH your God who brought you out of the land of Egypt — four instruments of honest commerce (balance, stone weight, dry measure, liquid measure) named explicitly; the exodus formula grounds commercial ethics in the same redemptive identity that grounds every other covenantal obligation. Dishonest trade is not merely illegal but a repudiation of the redeemer God.
19.37: And you shall observe all my statutes (kol-chuqqotai) and all my rules (kol-mishpatai) and do them; I am YHWH — the Holiness Code chapter closes with a summary command to keep all the statutes and rules, followed by the foundational self-identification. The pairing chuqqim (statutes, non-transparent commands) and mishpatim (judgments, rationally transparent civil laws) encompasses the full range of Levitical legislation.

11.1: YHWH speaks to both Moses and Aaron — unusual in Leviticus, where most instructions go to Moses alone. The double addressee signals that this law concerns the priestly community as enforcers: Aaron and his sons will adjudicate purity cases. The phrase vayedabber YHWH el-Moshe ve'el-Aharon (YHWH spoke to Moses and to Aaron) marks laws that require priestly implementation.
11.2: zot haḥayyah asher tokhelu: 'This is the living thing that you may eat.' The term ḥayyah (living creature) is the broad category; the subsequent verses subdivide it into land animals, aquatic creatures, birds, and flying insects. The dietary laws assume that Israel's relationship to creation is ordered — not every creature is equally appropriate for consumption, and the distinctions encode a theology of discernment (lehabdil, v. 47).
11.3: maphresat parsa ushosaat shesa umaalet gerah: 'splits the hoof, completely divided, and chews the cud.' Two criteria must both be met. Parsa (hoof, pl. parsot) refers to the horny covering; shosaat shesa ('completely divided') requires full bifurcation; gerah (cud) refers to partially digested food brought back for re-chewing. The double requirement — external (hoof) and internal (digestive) — creates a taxonomic system that cannot be met by appearance alone; it requires observation of behavior. The pig fails the second test; the camel fails the first.
11.4: et-hagamal: The camel (gamal) chews the cud but lacks a split hoof. The camel was the primary long-distance transport animal in the ancient Near East; its exclusion from the diet marks its role as a working animal distinct from food animals. tameh hu lachem: 'it is unclean to you' — the personal pronoun lachem (to you) signals that uncleanness is relational and covenantal, not an intrinsic property of the animal itself.
11.5: hashaphan: The rock badger or hyrax (Procavia capensis), a small mammal that appears to chew its jaw laterally (a form of coprophagy or caecotrophy), thus appearing to chew cud but lacking the ruminant digestive system. Its front feet are not truly split-hoofed. The identification is uncertain; some render it as 'coney.' Like the camel, it fails the split-hoof criterion.
11.6: ha'arnevet: The hare. Hares perform cecotrophy (re-ingestion of soft fecal pellets) which gives the appearance of cud-chewing; they also lack split hooves. The MKT renders arnevet as 'hare' following the most common identification. The law makes no claim about the hare's actual digestive biology — it describes observed behavior (chewing motion) as the criterion.
11.7: veet-haḥazir: The pig (ḥazir). The pig is the paradigmatic forbidden food in the OT and later Jewish tradition. It has fully split hooves but does not chew cud — it fails the internal/behavioral criterion. By the 2nd century BCE, refusal to eat pork had become a marker of Jewish identity (1 Macc 1:62-63; 2 Macc 6:18-31). The pig's exclusion made Israel's diet visibly distinct from surrounding cultures where pork was common.
11.8: mivsaram lo tokhelu uveniglatam lo tiga'u: 'their flesh you shall not eat, and their carcasses you shall not touch.' Double prohibition: consumption and contact. The term nivelah (carcass/corpse of an animal) marks a distinct impurity category — the dead body of an animal generates ritual contagion. The prohibition on touching carcasses extends beyond diet to behavior in any context.
11.9: snapir veqasqeset: 'fins and scales.' Two criteria for aquatic life, paralleling the two criteria for land animals. Snapir (fins) enables directed swimming; qasqeset (scales) covers the body. Creatures with both — most familiar fish — are clean. The Hebrew taxonomy groups all water life into those with and without these features, making no distinction between saltwater and freshwater beyond 'seas and streams.'
11.10: sheqets: 'detestable/abomination.' This is a stronger term than the standard tameh (unclean) — sheqets (from the root shqts) denotes something not merely ritually impure but fundamentally unfit, an object of revulsion. The term is reserved here for aquatic creatures without fins and scales, and for flying insects (v. 20, 23). The distinction matters: some unclean things (like animals that die naturally) can become clean through washing; sheqets creatures are categorically rejected, not merely temporarily impure.
11.11: sheqets yihyu lachem: 'they shall be detestable to you.' The personal address (lachem) continues — the classification is addressed to Israel as a covenant community, not as a universal biological taxonomy. The repetition of sheqets (vv. 10, 11, 12) emphasizes the categorical nature of the aquatic prohibition.
11.12: Summary restatement of the aquatic rule: fins and scales = clean; no fins and scales = detestable. The threefold repetition (vv. 10, 11, 12) is characteristic of Levitical legal style — stating a rule, applying it, then restating the principle — which ensures no ambiguity through what might appear redundant repetition.
11.13: veet-elleh teshaqqesu min-ha'oph: 'These you shall detest among the birds.' The bird list (vv. 13-19) uses the verb shaqats (to detest/make abominable), the denominative of sheqets. No criteria are given for forbidden birds, unlike the land-animal and aquatic rules — the list is simply enumerated. Most are birds of prey and carrion eaters: their exclusion may relate to their contact with blood and death, which are primary impurity sources in Leviticus. ha'nesher: the eagle or griffon vulture — the largest and most imposing bird, listed first.
11.14: hada'ah: The red kite; ha'ayyah: the black kite. Raptors that hunt live prey. The phrase 'of any kind' (leminatah) appears throughout the bird list, extending each prohibition to the full species category. The Hebrew bird taxonomy uses broad grouping terms rather than precise ornithological distinctions.
11.15: oreḇ leminahu: 'the raven of any kind.' Ravens are corvids — intelligent scavengers and omnivores. The raven was famously sent out by Noah from the ark (Gen 8:7) and fed Elijah (1 Kgs 17:6); its exclusion from the diet does not affect its symbolic or narrative roles.
11.16: The list continues with owl species and the sea gull (haśhaḥaph). The Hebrew terms for owl species (bat-ha'anah, taḥmas) are among the most uncertain identifications in the OT; the exact species cannot be determined with confidence. The MKT follows the most commonly proposed identifications while noting that certainty is impossible for many of these terms.
11.17: Additional owl species: hakos (little owl), hashalaḵ (cormorant or fisher-owl), hayanshuph (great owl or short-eared owl). The Hebrew term shalaḵ (plunging) describes a diving bird — likely a pelican or gannet rather than a cormorant. These are all birds that hunt fish or live near water and death.
11.18: Further owl/vulture species: hatinshemet (barn owl or ibis), haqa'at (tawny owl or pelican), haraḥam (Egyptian vulture/carrion vulture). The last term, raḥam, is related to the root rḥm (womb/compassion) — perhaps a folk etymology for the bird's appearance. These are primarily carrion-eaters, reinforcing the pattern of death-contact as the underlying logic of the list.
11.19: haḥasidah: the stork (from ḥesed, steadfast love — named for its faithfulness to its mate or offspring); ha'anaphah: the heron; hadukhiphath: the hoopoe; ha'ataleph: the bat. The bat is not a bird but is classified here with flying creatures — the Hebrew taxonomy groups by mode of locomotion, not by biological class. This is important for interpretation: the dietary law does not reflect modern taxonomy but ancient phenomenological classification.
11.20: kol-sheretz ha'oph haholekh al-arba': 'all flying swarmers that walk on four legs.' Sheretz (swarming creature) is a category distinct from birds — it covers flying insects and other small crawling creatures. Ha'oph (flying creature) is the qualifier. The criterion 'walks on four legs' appears to describe insects generally (ignoring that insects have six legs — the text counts the walking legs). These are categorically detestable (sheqets).
11.21: asher lo-khra'ayim mima'al leraglav: 'that have jointed legs above their feet for hopping on the ground.' The exception clause permits flying insects with kera'ayim (jointed/bent legs) designed for jumping. This is a morphological criterion parallel to the hoofed-animal criteria. The permitted insects are locusts — species that hop rather than merely crawl.
11.22: Four permitted locust species: arbeh (migratory locust, the most common term), sola'am (bald locust), ḥargol (cricket/long-horned locust), ḥagab (grasshopper). The precise species are uncertain; all four are jumping insects in the locust/grasshopper family. John the Baptist ate locusts and wild honey (Matt 3:4), consistent with this permitted category. Locusts were a practical protein source in the ancient Near East.
11.23: Summary: all other sheretz ha'oph (flying swarmers) with four feet (i.e., that crawl rather than jump) are detestable (sheqets). The logic is consistent: the flying creature category has a positive criterion (jointed jumping legs) parallel to the land and water criteria.
11.24: veleh titamma'u: 'by these you shall become unclean.' The impurity transmission rules now follow the prohibition list. Tame' (become unclean) is the reflexive/factitive — touching the carcass is the mechanism. ad-ha'arev: 'until the evening' — the standard time-limit for minor impurity; evening resets the ritual day. This is the lightest impurity category: time-limited, resolved without sacrifice.
11.25: veḥobel nibletan yekabbes begadav: 'whoever picks up their carcass shall wash his clothes.' Contact by carrying (nasa) rather than merely touching is a more severe form of contamination — it requires laundering of clothes in addition to the person being unclean until evening. The escalating severity (touch → carry → eat) reflects a graduated contamination theology.
11.26: Animals with divided hooves that fail the double criterion (either incomplete division or lack of cud-chewing) also generate carcass impurity. This verse extends the impurity rules beyond the listed forbidden animals to any animal that partially meets the criteria — ensuring no gray-area exceptions.
11.27: kol-holekh al-kappav: 'everything that walks on its paws.' Carnivorous mammals — dogs, lions, bears, cats — that walk on pads rather than hooves. These are unclean, and their carcasses transmit impurity. The prohibition covers the full range of non-hoofed land animals not already covered by the listed exceptions.
11.28: Carrying the carcass of a paw-walker requires both clothes-washing and waiting until evening. The symmetry with v. 25 (carrying vs. touching) is maintained across all carcass categories — the graduated response is consistent regardless of which forbidden animal's carcass is involved.
11.29: sheretz hashorerets al-ha'arets: 'the swarming things that swarm on the ground.' A distinct sub-category of ground-swarming creatures, separate from the flying swarmers (v. 20) and from the general land animals. Eight species are listed: ḥoled (weasel), akbar (mouse/rat), tsav (great lizard/toad), and others. Sheretz (from shrs, to swarm/teem) is the collective term for small creatures that move in groups or swarms.
11.30: Continuation of the ground-swarmers list: ana qah (gecko), koaḥ (monitor lizard/land crocodile), leta'ah (wall lizard), ḥomet (skink/sand lizard), tinshemet (chameleon). The precise identification of each term is disputed. What is clear is the category: small reptiles and rodents that 'swarm' along the ground, live in the soil, and are particularly associated with death, decay, and the underworld in ancient Near Eastern thought.
11.31: tameim hem lachem: 'they are unclean to you.' The summary formula for the ground-swarmers. Contact with their dead bodies transmits impurity until evening. The phrase hem lachem (they are to you) again positions uncleanness as relational — a category defined by the covenant relationship, not a universal property.
11.32: Contamination spreads to objects: any wooden, cloth, leather, or sackcloth article on which a dead swarmer falls becomes unclean. It must be immersed in water and remains unclean until evening. This extends the impurity system beyond persons to domestic objects — the household, not just the individual, is the unit of purity concern. The mention of specific materials (wood, cloth, leather, sack) shows that the law addresses the practical household context of ancient Israel.
11.33: vekhol-keli-ḥeres asher-yippol mehem: 'any clay vessel into which any of them falls.' The clay vessel (keli ḥeres) becomes unclean and must be broken — not merely washed. This is because clay is porous and cannot be fully cleansed; it absorbs impurity in a way that wood or metal cannot. The rule about breaking clay pots is consistent across Leviticus and Numbers where persistent impurity is involved. The asymmetry between clay (break) and other materials (wash) reflects practical knowledge of material properties.
11.34: Any food or liquid in a contaminated vessel becomes unclean by contact. The contamination pathway: dead swarmer → vessel → water in vessel → food wet by that water. The rule about water as a conductor of impurity (mayim yutan, 'water has been put on') appears also in v. 38 (wet seed) — water activates impurity transfer in a way that dry contact does not for seeds and food.
11.35: vekol-asher-yippol minevilatam alav yitma: 'everything on which any part of their carcass falls is unclean.' Fixed cooking vessels — tanur (clay oven) and kirayim (cooking range/brazier with two openings) — must be torn down. They cannot be cleansed because their function involves food contact and fire-use that would spread contamination. The destruction of contaminated fixed installations reflects the seriousness of maintaining purity in food preparation.
11.36: akh ma'yan ubor miqveh-mayim yihyeh tahor: 'But a spring or a cistern, a gathering of water, shall be clean.' A positive exception: large natural water sources — springs, cisterns, *miqveh* (ritual gathering of water) — are not contaminated by carcass contact in the same way. They remain clean, though the person who touched the carcass while in them is still unclean. The miqveh (gathering of water) here is the term later used for the ritual immersion pool — the law implicitly grounds the purifying function of immersion in this verse.
11.37: vekhivnevelatam al-zera'-zeru'a asher yizarea tahor hu: 'if any part of their carcass falls on seed that is to be planted, it is clean.' Dry seed is not contaminated by carcass contact. The exception preserves agricultural productivity — if every contaminated seed had to be discarded, subsistence farming would be impossible. The rule draws a practical boundary around the impurity system: it is designed to govern food and personal purity, not to destroy the food supply.
11.38: Contrast: if water has been put on the seed first and a carcass falls on it, the wet seed is unclean. Water activates impurity transfer (see v. 34) — the distinction between dry and wet is a consistent principle throughout the Levitical purity system. Wet = susceptible to impurity; dry = resistant. This is an important exegetical observation for interpreting the broader system.
11.39: vekhiv-yamut min-habeheima asher-hi lachem leokhlah: 'if an animal that you are permitted to eat dies.' An animal that was clean for consumption but died naturally (not slaughtered) generates impurity through contact and consumption. The term nevel (carcass of a naturally-dead animal) is distinct from treifa (torn/killed by predators, v. 40) and from zavuaḥ (slaughtered). The clean/unclean distinction does not override the life/death distinction: even a clean animal becomes an impurity source at death.
11.40: Eating from a dead clean animal requires washing of clothes and renders the person unclean until evening. Carrying the carcass also requires clothes-washing. The parallel between eating and carrying maintains the graduated response — both are forms of intimate contact with death that require purification, even when the animal was intrinsically clean.
11.41: vekhol-hasheres hashorerets al-ha'arets sheqets hu lo ye'akhel: 'Every creature that swarms on the ground is detestable; it shall not be eaten.' Sheqets returns as the characterization of all ground-swarmers — they are categorically detestable, not merely ritually impure. The prohibition is absolute: there is no clean sub-category of ground-swarming creatures parallel to the locust exception among flying swarmers.
11.42: kol-holekh al-gahon: 'everything that goes on its belly' — snakes and worm-like creatures; kol-holekh al-arba': 'everything that goes on all fours' — crawling quadrupeds; kol-marbeh raglaim: 'everything that has many feet' — centipedes, millipedes. The three movement-types comprehensively cover all ground-locomotion categories, ensuring no creature that moves by crawling or multiple legs escapes the prohibition.
11.43: al-teshaqqesu et-nafshoteikhem: 'do not defile yourselves in your souls/selves.' The term nefesh (soul/self/life) here is the reflexive — eating forbidden things defiles the self, not merely the body. Shiqqutstem (make yourselves detestable) uses the root of sheqets reflexively: by eating detestable things, you become detestable. The dietary law is a guard against self-corruption — identity is shaped by what one consumes.
11.44: ki ani YHWH Eloheikhem vehitqaddashtem vihyitem qedoshim ki qadosh ani: 'For I am YHWH your God; consecrate yourselves and be holy, for I am holy.' The theological foundation of the entire dietary system is YHWH's own holiness (qedushah). Hitqaddashtem (consecrate yourselves) is the reflexive-intensive of qadash (to be holy/set apart); qedoshim tihyu (you shall be holy) is the imperative of the holiness program. The logic: YHWH is intrinsically holy (set apart, utterly other); Israel is YHWH's covenant people; therefore Israel must embody that separateness in every domain of life including diet. Peter quotes this verse directly in 1 Pet 1:16 to ground NT ethical holiness.
11.45: ki ani YHWH hama'aleh etchem me'erets mitsrayim lihyot lachem lElohim: 'For I am YHWH who brought you up out of the land of Egypt to be your God.' The historical grounding of the holiness demand: the Exodus event establishes both YHWH's identity as Israel's redeemer and Israel's identity as YHWH's redeemed people. The dietary laws are not arbitrary restrictions but the embodied practice of a people whose whole existence is defined by divine rescue. Vihyitem qedoshim ki qadosh ani: the holiness command is grounded in divine nature, not human effort — Israel is called to reflect what YHWH already is.
11.46: zot torat habeheima veha'oph: 'This is the law (torah) concerning animals and birds.' The summary heading (torah, instruction/law) introduces the closing summary. The term torah here is in its basic sense of authoritative instruction, before it acquired the canonical sense of 'the Law.' It applies to beheima (land animals), oph (flying creatures), nefesh ḥayyah hahoma-yim (living creatures in the waters), and nefesh hashoretset (swarming creatures).
11.47: lehabdil bein hatame uvein hatahor uvein haḥayyah hane'ekelet uvein haḥayyah asher lo te'akhel: 'to distinguish between the unclean and the clean, and between the living creatures that may be eaten and those that may not be eaten.' The climactic purpose statement of the entire chapter. Lehabdil (to separate/distinguish/discern) is the priestly verb — the same root used in Gen 1 for the divine acts of separation (light/darkness, waters above/below). The priest's function is to embody the Creator's act of ordering; the dietary laws extend that discernment into daily life. Israel as 'a kingdom of priests' (Exod 19:6) is a people whose everyday eating practices enact the distinction-making that characterizes both YHWH's creation and YHWH's holiness.

12.1: Standard Sinaitic address formula — YHWH speaks to Moses alone for the post-birth purification law. The transition from the dietary laws (addressed to Aaron and Moses jointly) to the birth-purity laws (Moses alone) reflects the scope: the dietary laws required priestly enforcement; the birth-purity law primarily affects the mother and is administered by the priest only at the offering stage (v. 6).
12.2: isha ki tazria veyaldah zakhar: 'When a woman bears seed and gives birth to a male.' The verb tazria (third-person feminine hiphil of zara', to sow/plant seed) is literally 'when she sows seed' or 'when she causes seed to sprout' — a striking agricultural metaphor applied to human conception. The chapter heading (ki tazria) is the Hebrew name for this Torah portion. The comparison keniddat devatah (as during her menstruation) indicates a seven-day impurity period — the birth itself generates the same impurity category as menstruation (niddah).
12.3: uvayom hashemini yimmol besar orlatah: 'On the eighth day the flesh of his foreskin shall be circumcised.' The eighth-day circumcision rule connects the birth-purity law to the Abrahamic covenant (Gen 17:12). The timing is significant: the mother's seven-day impurity period ends, and on the eighth day — the first day of her 33-day blood-purification period — the male child enters the covenant. The eighth day is theologically loaded in Leviticus and later tradition as a day of new beginning (Lev 9:1 — Aaron's inaugural offering; Lev 14:10 — leper's purification completion).
12.4: shloshet ushloshem yom teshev bidemei toharah: 'thirty-three days she shall remain in the blood of her purification.' Demei toharah (blood of purification) is a nuanced phrase — the blood is called 'blood of purification' even though it is still associated with ritual impurity. The mother is in an intermediate state: no longer in the severe impurity of niddah but not yet fully restored to holiness. She may not touch holy things or enter the sanctuary — the two primary locations of YHWH's presence. The total impurity period for a son: 7 + 33 = 40 days.
12.5: ve'im-neqevah teled vetame'a shnayim kishnuatah: 'if she gives birth to a girl, she shall be unclean two weeks, as in her menstruation.' The impurity period doubles for a daughter — 14 days of niddah-equivalent impurity followed by 66 days of blood-purification, totaling 80 days. The reason for the doubled period is not explained in the text and has generated extensive rabbinic discussion. It may reflect the understanding that a female child carries her own future reproductive capacity, or it may reflect the ritual weight of the female body's future role. The asymmetry is acknowledged but not rationalized in the text itself.
12.6: uvimlo'ot yemei toharatah levin o levat: 'when the days of her purification are complete, for a son or a daughter.' The purification offering consists of two animals: a year-old lamb (keves ben-shenato) for a burnt offering (olah) and a young dove or turtledove (ben-yonah or tor) for a sin offering (ḥatta't). The lamb is the standard offering of someone fully reintegrating into the covenant community; the accompanying sin offering addresses not moral sin but the ritual impurity state of childbirth. The two-offering structure (restoration + purity-cleansing) mirrors other purification rituals in Leviticus.
12.7: vekhipper aleiha hakohen vetahera: 'the priest shall make atonement for her and she will be clean.' Kipper (atonement) is the central verb of Levitical sacrifice — it covers, removes, or ransoms guilt and impurity. Here it applies to birth-impurity rather than moral sin, confirming that kipper operates across both sin and ritual-impurity categories. zot torat hayoledet: 'this is the law for the one who gives birth' — the summary formula (torah) paralleling v. 11:46. The law applies universally: every birth, regardless of sex, requires this purification ritual.
12.8: ve'im-lo timsah yadah dei she: 'if she cannot afford a lamb.' The poverty provision offers two turtledoves (tor) or two young pigeons (bnei yonah) as a substitution — one for the burnt offering and one for the sin offering. This provision is directly invoked in Luke 2:24: when Mary and Joseph bring Jesus to the temple for Mary's purification and Jesus's presentation, they offer 'a pair of turtledoves, or two young pigeons' (Luke 2:24, citing Lev 12:8) — signaling that Joseph and Mary were poor. The one who would become the ultimate offering was presented in the temple under the provision for the poor.

14.1: YHWH spoke to Moses (waydabber YHWH el-Moshe) — the standard revelation formula that opens priestly legislation, grounding every ritual requirement in divine speech rather than cultural convention.
14.2: Zot tihyeh torat ha-metzora — this shall be the law (torah) of the diseased person (metzora): the term metzora derives from tzara'at, a term covering a range of skin conditions, fungal infections of fabric and houses; it does not correspond precisely to modern clinical leprosy (Hansen's disease).
14.3: The priest (kohen) shall go out of the camp and examine (yivsodu) — the leper lived outside the camp (Lev 13:46); re-entry required priestly inspection. The priest goes out to the unclean person, initiating the cleansing rather than awaiting the healed person inside the sanctuary.
14.4: The priest shall command two living clean birds (tsipporim hayyot tehorot), cedar wood (etz erez), scarlet yarn (sheni tola'at), and hyssop (ezov) — four elements whose combination recurs in the red heifer purification (Num 19:6) and in the Passover blood-daubing (hyssop, Exod 12:22). The number two and the contrast between slaughter and release encode death-and-life symbolism.
14.5: One bird shall be slaughtered (shachat) over an earthen vessel (keli cheres) over running water (mayim hayyim) — living water (hayyim = flowing, living) is required for major purification rites (Lev 14:5,6,50-52; Num 19:17); stagnant water does not suffice. The earthen vessel catches the blood; the combination of blood and living water will recur in the second bird's release.
14.6: The priest shall take the live bird with the cedar wood, scarlet yarn, and hyssop and dip them together with the live bird in the blood of the bird slaughtered over the running water — all four elements are dipped together, uniting them in the sacrificial blood before the live bird's release. The ritual binds the two birds as a single symbolic unit: one dies, the other carries the cleansing blood away.
14.7: He shall sprinkle (hizza) seven times on the one being cleansed of the skin disease — seven sprinklings mark complete cleansing throughout Leviticus (cf. Lev 4:6; 14:27,51; 16:14,19; Num 19:4). The metzora who was declared unclean (Lev 13:3) is now declared clean (tihar) by the same priestly authority.
14.8: The one being cleansed shall wash (kibbes) his clothes, shave off all his hair (gillach et-kol-se'aro), and bathe in water (rachatz bamayim) — triple purification: laundering, shaving, immersion. The shaving is radical (kol = all his hair) to remove any residue of the affliction from hair follicles; he may then enter the camp but must remain outside his tent for seven days.
14.9: On the seventh day he shall shave all his hair again — head, beard, eyebrows, all hair (kol-se'aro): the repetition of full shaving on day 7 mirrors the seven-day isolation period. After the second shaving he washes his clothes and bathes; a new counting begins.
14.10: On the eighth day he shall take two male lambs without blemish (kesavim temimim) and one female lamb a year old (kivisah achat bat-shenata temima) and three tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil for a grain offering, and one log of oil — the eighth-day ceremony requires three animals plus grain and oil, one of the most elaborate re-entry offerings in Leviticus. The log is approximately 300ml.
14.11: The priest who cleanses (ha-metaher) shall set the man being cleansed and these offerings before YHWH at the entrance of the tent of meeting — the cleansed person appears before the sanctuary threshold. The priest who performs the cleansing is the same one who performed the diagnosis (ch 13), maintaining continuity of priestly authority throughout the process.
14.12: The priest shall take one of the male lambs and offer it as a guilt offering (asham) — the guilt offering precedes the sin offering (v.19) in this ritual, unusual because the metzora is not guilty of a specific violation; the asham here functions as reparation for the period of exclusion from sanctuary participation rather than for a definable sin.
14.13: He shall slaughter the lamb in the place where they slaughter the sin offering and the burnt offering, in the holy place (ha-makom ha-qadosh) — the guilt offering shares the slaughter location with the sin and burnt offerings (cf. Lev 6:25; 7:2), northeast of the altar (Lev 1:11). The blood handling will differ from the standard guilt offering.
14.14: The priest shall take some of the blood of the guilt offering, and the priest shall put it on the lobe of the right ear (tenuch ozen-yemino) of the one being cleansed, on the thumb of his right hand (bohen yado ha-yemanit), and on the big toe of his right foot (bohen raglo ha-yemanit) — the identical three-point anointing used for Aaron and his sons at ordination (Exod 29:20; Lev 8:23-24), applied here to a restored layperson. Ear, hand, foot = the person's hearing (obedience), action (work), and movement (walk) — the whole reoriented life consecrated by blood.
14.15: The priest shall then take some of the log of oil and pour it into the palm of his own left hand — the oil is poured into the priest's left palm and applied with the right finger; the separation of pouring and applying hands allows precise ritual control. The oil application follows the blood application on the same three points (vv.17-18), layering consecration over atonement.
14.16: The priest shall dip his right finger in the oil that is in his left hand and shall sprinkle some of the oil with his finger seven times before YHWH — seven oil sprinklings mirror the seven blood sprinklings of v.7; the completeness motif structures the entire cleansing. The sprinklings are directed toward YHWH (before the sanctuary) before the oil is applied to the person.
14.17: Some of the oil remaining in his hand the priest shall put on the lobe of the right ear of him who is being cleansed and on the thumb of his right hand and on the big toe of his right foot — the blood of the guilt offering was applied first (v.14); now oil is placed on the same three points. The sequence blood-then-oil at ear/thumb/toe is identical to the ordination ceremony of Lev 8:23-30, where blood was applied first and then ordination oil.
14.18: And the rest of the oil that is in the priest's hand he shall put on the head of him who is being cleansed — the oil remaining after the three-point application is poured over the head, an act of anointing (mashach). The restored leper receives the same anointing gesture as priests and kings, marking restoration to full covenant membership.
14.19: Then the priest shall offer the sin offering (asham → should be chatat) and make atonement (kipper) for the one being cleansed from his uncleanness — the sin offering follows the guilt offering, completing the two-phase atonement. The Hebrew kipper (to atone, cover, ransom) is the central theological term of Levitical sacrifice; here it specifically covers the uncleanness (tum'ato) of the skin disease.
14.20: And the priest shall offer the burnt offering (olah) and the grain offering (minchah) on the altar — the culminating burnt offering (complete consecration) and grain offering (dedication of produce/life) complete the re-entry sequence. After this the person is fully clean (taher) and restored to normal Israelite life.
14.21: But if he is poor (dal) and cannot afford so much, he shall take one male lamb as a guilt offering to be waved, to make atonement for him — the poverty provision reduces the offering from three animals to one lamb plus two birds (vv.21-22). The guilt offering lamb is non-negotiable; the sin and burnt offerings may be replaced by turtledoves or pigeons. The essential atoning mechanism is preserved while the financial burden is reduced.
14.22: And two turtledoves (shtei torim) or two pigeons (shtei vnei yona), such as he can afford — the poor person's two birds replace the two additional lambs of vv.10-20.
