Turtledoves and pigeons are the standard poor-person's offering throughout Leviticus (Lev 1:14; 5:7,11; 12:8); Luke 2:24 records that Mary and Joseph brought this offering at Jesus's presentation, indicating their modest means.
14.23: He shall bring them on the eighth day for his cleansing to the priest, to the entrance of the tent of meeting before YHWH — the eighth-day timing is identical for rich and poor; the difference is only in the animals brought. The threshold of the tent of meeting is the liminal space where human uncleanness meets divine holiness for priestly mediation.
14.24: The priest shall take the lamb of the guilt offering and the log of oil, and the priest shall wave them as a wave offering before YHWH — waving (tenupha) consecrates the offering by presenting it before YHWH and receiving it back as a priestly portion (Lev 7:30-34). The lamb is waved while still alive before slaughter.
14.25: He shall slaughter the lamb of the guilt offering, and the priest shall take some of the blood of the guilt offering and put it on the lobe of the right ear of him who is being cleansed and on the thumb of his right hand and on the big toe of his right foot — the three-point blood application is identical for rich and poor (cf. v.14), preserving the full symbolic significance regardless of economic status. The ritual form of restoration is equal even if the materials differ.
14.26: The priest shall pour some of the oil into the palm of his own left hand — the oil ceremony is identical to the wealthy person's ceremony (v.15); the poverty provision changes only the animal offerings, not the oil application. The layered symbolism of blood-at-three-points then oil-at-three-points is preserved in full.
14.27: And the priest shall sprinkle with his right finger some of the oil that is in his left hand seven times before YHWH — seven sprinklings for the poor as for the wealthy; the completeness of the cleansing is not diminished by economic circumstances. The equality of access to full atonement is built into the structure of Levitical provision.
14.28: And the priest shall put some of the oil that is in his hand on the lobe of the right ear of him who is being cleansed, on the thumb of his right hand and on the big toe of his right foot, in the place where the blood of the guilt offering was put — the phrase al-meqom ha-dam (in the place of the blood) indicates that oil is applied precisely over the blood points, sealing or layering consecration upon atonement.
14.29: And the rest of the oil that is in the priest's hand he shall put on the head of him who is being cleansed, to make atonement for him before YHWH — the head anointing concludes both the wealthy and the poor person's restoration ceremony identically. The formula lekhapper alav lifney YHWH (to make atonement for him before YHWH) confirms the legal and relational restoration.
14.30: He shall also offer one of the turtledoves or pigeons, whichever he can afford — the poor person's sin offering and burnt offering (vv.30-31) replace the lambs of vv.19-20; the two birds serve double duty as both sin offering and burnt offering, one for each.
14.31: Such as he can afford, the one as a sin offering and the other as a burnt offering, along with the grain offering — the grain offering is mentioned here (cf. v.21: three tenths of an ephah with oil); no reduction is specified for the poor person's grain offering, though the proportion to the reduced animal offering may implicitly differ.
14.32: This is the law for him in whom is a case of leprous disease, who cannot afford the offerings for his cleansing — the section closes with a summary formula (zot torat asher-bo nega tzara'at), marking the end of the metzora purification laws and the provision for the poor as an integrated unit of legislation.
14.33: YHWH spoke to Moses and Aaron — both Moses and Aaron are addressed (cf. Lev 11:1; 13:1) when legislation concerns matters in which Aaron's priestly role is distinct from Moses's prophetic role. The house-plague legislation requires both the prophetic word and the priestly action.
14.34: When you come into the land of Canaan, which I give to you for a possession, and I put a case of leprous disease (nega tzara'at) in a house in the land of your possession — the house-plague legislation is prospective, applying only after settlement in Canaan. The phrase ve-natati (I put) is theologically striking: YHWH is the agent who places the affliction in the house, making the house-cleansing a divine visitation requiring priestly diagnosis rather than a routine maintenance issue.
14.35: Then he who owns the house shall come and tell the priest, 'There seems to me to be some kind of disease in my house' — the homeowner uses the cautious ka-nega (like a disease), maintaining epistemic humility before the official priestly determination. The obligation to report to the priest, not to remedy the situation independently, reflects the priestly monopoly on purity determinations.
14.36: The priest shall command that they empty the house before the priest goes in to examine the disease — emptying the house before inspection protects household goods from the mass declaration of impurity that would result if the priest diagnosed the house as diseased while contents were still inside. The protection of the household's material goods reflects pastoral concern within the purity system.
14.37: And he shall examine the disease. And if the disease is in the walls of the house with greenish or reddish spots (yeraqqraqot o adamdammot), and if they appear to be deeper than the surface — the diagnostic criteria parallel the skin disease criteria of Lev 13: color abnormality (greenish-reddish) plus apparent depth. The repetition of the same diagnostic logic for houses as for persons reflects a holistic purity theology in which the land, houses, and persons share the same vulnerability to tzara'at.
14.38: Then the priest shall go out of the house to the door of the house and shut up the house seven days — the house is quarantined exactly as a person with an ambiguous skin condition (Lev 13:4,5,21,26,31), with a seven-day isolation period before reinspection. The structural parallelism between person-quarantine and house-quarantine shows that tzara'at is understood as a single category of affliction affecting different substrates.
14.39: And the priest shall come again on the seventh day and look — the identical re-examination protocol applies to houses as to persons; if the disease has spread (paroh ha-nega) in the walls, it is malignant (tzara'at memaheret in v.44, 'spreading tzara'at').
14.40: Then the priest shall command that they take out the stones in which is the disease and throw them into an unclean place outside the city — removal of diseased stones is the architectural equivalent of shaving hair from an afflicted person; isolating and disposing of the diseased material removes the source. The unclean place outside the city maintains spatial separation of impurity from the community.
14.41: He shall have the inside of the house scraped all around (ve-et-ha-bayit yiqtzeh misviv) — scraping the interior walls removes potentially infected plaster, just as shaving hair removes potentially infected tissue. The scraped material is disposed of in the unclean place outside the city along with the diseased stones.
14.42: And they shall take other stones and put them in the place of those stones, and he shall take other plaster and plaster the house — replacement of diseased stones and replastering gives the house a chance at restoration; the new materials provide a clean substrate for reinspection. If the disease returns in the new materials (v.43-44), the house is declared incurably diseased.
14.43: If the disease breaks out again in the house, after he has taken out the stones and scraped the house and plastered it — if the disease returns after remediation, the judgment is that it has entered the structure itself; the house is declared irremediably impure. The recurrence after treatment is the decisive diagnostic criterion, paralleling the skin disease recurrence criterion of Lev 13:7.
14.44: Then the priest shall come and look, and if the disease has spread in the house, it is a malignant leprous disease (tzara'at memaheret hi) — memaheret (spreading, persistent) is the term for active, spreading, incurable tzara'at; the house is declared unclean (tamei) with the same finality as an incurably diseased person (Lev 13:11).
14.45: He shall break down the house, its stones and timber and all the plaster of the house, and he shall carry them outside the city to an unclean place — complete demolition is the house's equivalent of the metzora's permanent exclusion from the camp (Lev 13:46). The materials (stones, wood, plaster) are all carried to the unclean place; nothing is salvaged from an incurably diseased house.
14.46: Moreover, whoever enters the house while it is shut up shall be unclean until the evening — even during the quarantine period (before any diagnosis), entering the sealed house transmits impurity until evening. The house's impurity is contagious during the ambiguous period, not only after diagnosis.
14.47: And whoever sleeps in the house shall wash his clothes, and whoever eats in the house shall wash his clothes — lying in the house (prolonged exposure) and eating in the house (intimate association) create a higher degree of contact requiring laundering, while simple entry requires only sunset purification. The degree of impurity correlates with degree of contact.
14.48: But if the priest comes and looks, and if the disease has not spread in the house after the house was plastered, the priest shall pronounce the house clean, for the disease is healed (nirpa ha-nega) — the same verb rapha (heal, repair) is used for the house as for persons; the house 'healed' is language that treats the house as a living entity susceptible to healing as well as affliction.
14.49: For the cleansing of the house he shall take two small birds, cedar wood, scarlet yarn, and hyssop — the same four-element combination used for the human metzora's cleansing ceremony (v.4); the structural parallelism is complete: the same ritual cleanses both afflicted persons and afflicted houses, affirming the underlying unity of the tzara'at category.
14.50: He shall kill one of the birds in an earthen vessel over running water — the two-bird ceremony proceeds identically for the house as for the person (cf. vv.5-7): one bird slaughtered over living water in an earthen vessel, one bird dipped in the blood and released.
14.51: He shall take the cedar wood and the hyssop and the scarlet yarn, along with the live bird, and dip them in the blood of the bird that was killed and in the running water and sprinkle the house seven times — seven sprinklings for the house (as for the person, v.7). The live bird will carry the blood-mixture away when released, symbolically transferring the impurity out of the community.
14.52: Thus he shall cleanse the house with the blood of the bird and with the running water and with the live bird and with the cedar wood and hyssop and scarlet yarn — the summary names all five elements of the cleansing medium: blood, water, live bird, cedar, hyssop + scarlet. The combination of blood and water recurs throughout Leviticus and NT typology as the complete cleansing medium.
14.53: He shall let the live bird go out of the city into the open country (el-mul panei ha-sadeh). So he shall make atonement for the house, and it shall be clean — the bird's release into the open field (as in v.7 for the metzora) carries the cleansed impurity away from the community permanently. The language of atonement (kipper) is applied to the house, not just its inhabitants.
14.54: This is the law for every case of leprous disease (nega tzara'at): for an itch (netek) — netek is the scalp/beard condition of Lev 13:29-37, included here in the summary list.
14.55: For leprous disease in a garment or in a house — the summary (vv.54-57) explicitly names garment-tzara'at (Lev 13:47-59) and house-tzara'at (14:33-53) as subcategories under the single torah ha-tzara'at, confirming that all three substrates (person, fabric, architecture) are governed by a unified purity theology.
14.56: And for a swelling (se'et) and a scab (sapachat) and a shiny spot (baheret) — three surface anomalies first defined in Lev 13:2 that serve as the diagnostic criteria for human tzara'at; their inclusion in the closing summary ties chs 13–14 together as a coherent legal unit.
14.57: To teach when something is unclean and when it is clean (lehorot beyom hatamei uveyon hatahor) — the explicit pedagogical function of the entire tzara'at legislation: to teach the distinction between tamei and tahor, unclean and clean. This closing formula articulates what Leviticus 10:10 states as the priest's fundamental duty: to distinguish holy from common and unclean from clean.

15.1: YHWH spoke to Moses and Aaron — both Moses and Aaron are addressed because the discharge legislation (Lev 15) involves priestly oversight of offerings (vv.14-15,29-30) and priestly protection from defilement (v.31); it is both prophetic legislation and priestly practice.
15.2: Speak to the people of Israel and say to them, when any man has a discharge from his body (zov mibsaro), his discharge is unclean — zov (flow, discharge) is the root term for the entire chapter; the zav (man with chronic discharge) and zavah (woman with irregular discharge) are named after it. The discharge renders the man (zav) himself impure, not merely the discharge substance.
15.3: And this is the law of his uncleanness for a discharge: whether his body runs with his discharge, or his body is blocked up by his discharge, it is his uncleanness — the distinction between a running (chronic, active) discharge and a blocked one suggests both acute and chronic presentations of the condition; both create the same degree of impurity in the person himself.
15.4: Every bed on which the one with the discharge lies shall be unclean, and everything on which he sits shall be unclean — contact impurity spreads from the zav to surfaces he lies or sits on, creating a secondary level of impurity that contaminates anyone who subsequently contacts those surfaces (v.5). The impurity radiates outward from the person through physical contact points.
15.5: And anyone who touches his bed shall wash his clothes and bathe himself in water and be unclean until the evening — the standard purification formula: laundering + immersion + sunset. The erev (evening) boundary marks the natural daily reset point in the Israelite purity system; all contact impurities of ordinary persons resolve by sunset after purification.
15.6: And whoever sits on anything on which the one with the discharge has sat shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and be unclean until the evening — secondary contact (sitting where the zav sat) creates the same temporary impurity as direct contact. The transmission chain: zav → surface → person → until-evening impurity.
15.7: And whoever touches the body of the one with the discharge shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and be unclean until the evening — direct body contact with the zav also creates until-evening impurity with standard purification. The categories of contact in vv.4-12 systematically cover: his bed, his seat, his body, his spittle, anything he rides, anything he touches, anything under him.
15.8: And if the man with the discharge spits on someone who is clean, then he shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and be unclean until the evening — saliva from the zav transmits impurity; the specific mention of spitting indicates that any bodily emission from the zav can create contact impurity, not only the discharge itself.
15.9: And any saddle on which the one with the discharge rides shall be unclean — the saddle (or riding apparatus) is treated like a sitting surface (v.6); a riding animal's saddle contacts both the animal and the rider in extended proximity, warranting specific inclusion alongside beds and seats.
15.10: And whoever touches anything that was under him shall be unclean until the evening. And whoever carries such things shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and be unclean until the evening — carrying objects that were under the zav creates a higher degree of contact than merely touching them, warranting laundering in addition to bathing; the distinction between touching and carrying reflects graduated impurity transmission based on degree of contact.
15.11: Anyone whom the one with the discharge touches without having rinsed his hands in water shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and be unclean until the evening — if the zav has not washed his hands, touching another person transmits impurity; if he has washed his hands, touching does not transmit impurity. Hand-washing provides a practical limiter on the zav's social interaction, enabling some normal social life during the period of discharge.
15.12: And an earthen vessel that the one with the discharge touches shall be broken, and every vessel of wood shall be rinsed with water — earthenware cannot be purified (Lev 11:33; 6:28; 15:12) and must be destroyed; wooden vessels can be rinsed and remain in use. The distinction between earthenware and wood in impurity purifiability is a consistent principle throughout Leviticus (cf. Lev 11:32-35).
15.13: And when the one with a discharge is cleansed of his discharge, then he shall count for himself seven days for his cleansing, and wash his clothes. And he shall bathe his body in running water (mayim hayyim) and shall be clean — the chronic discharge requires seven clear days after cessation before purification is possible; running water (hayyim, living/flowing) is required for the zav's purification as for the metzora's (Lev 14:5-6), a higher standard than still water. The seven-day waiting period confirms that the discharge has truly ended.
15.14: And on the eighth day he shall take two turtledoves or two pigeons and come before YHWH to the entrance of the tent of meeting and give them to the priest — the eighth-day offering mirrors the metzora's eighth-day re-entry (Lev 14:10-20,23); both conditions require sacrifice on the day after a seven-day purification period. The two birds (one sin offering, one burnt offering, v.15) restore full sanctuary access.
15.15: And the priest shall use them, one for a sin offering and the other for a burnt offering. And the priest shall make atonement for him before YHWH for his discharge — the atonement formula (kipper alav lifney YHWH) for discharge, as for tzara'at, skin disease, and childbirth (Lev 12:7-8), indicates that these conditions are not treated as purely physical matters but as states requiring priestly intercession and sacrificial restoration.
15.16: If a man has an emission of semen (shichvat-zera), he shall bathe his whole body in water and be unclean until the evening — incidental semen emission (not the chronic condition of the zav) creates only until-evening impurity after bathing, without requiring the seven-day waiting period or the eighth-day sacrifice. The proportional response: chronic discharge → 7 days + offering; incidental emission → until evening + bathing.
15.17: And every garment and every skin on which the semen comes shall be washed with water and be unclean until the evening — the semen-stained garment or leather article requires laundering; the impurity affects objects as well as the person, but with the same minimal duration (until evening).
15.18: If a man lies with a woman and has an emission of semen, both of them shall bathe themselves in water and be unclean until the evening — sexual intercourse creates mutual until-evening impurity for both partners, requiring bathing for both. This provision is not a moral judgment on marital intercourse but a purity regulation that creates a temporary, resolvable state requiring the same basic purification.
15.19: When a woman has a discharge, and the discharge in her body is blood (vedam yihyeh zova bivisra), she shall be in her menstrual impurity (niddatah) for seven days — the woman's normal menstrual flow creates the niddah state for seven days; anyone who touches her is unclean until evening (v.19b). The term niddah (separation, impurity) names the state the woman enters during menstruation.
15.20: And everything on which she lies during her menstrual impurity shall be unclean. Everything also on which she sits shall be unclean — the niddah woman's bed and seat become secondary impurity sources, identical to the zav's furniture (vv.4-6); the structural parallel between male and female discharge impurity is intentional, treating both conditions under a unified framework.
15.21: And whoever touches her bed shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and be unclean until the evening — contact with the niddah woman's bed creates the standard until-evening impurity with laundering + bathing, identical to contact with the zav's bed (v.5).
15.22: And whoever touches anything on which she sits shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and be unclean until the evening — the systematic coverage of bed and seat for the niddah parallels the coverage for the zav (vv.5-6); the parallel legislation reflects deliberate structural symmetry, not two separate traditions merged together.
15.23: Whether it is the bed or anything on which she sits, when he touches it he shall be unclean until the evening — the summary generalizes from the specific examples; all surfaces on which the niddah has lain or sat create contact impurity. The evening boundary applies universally to this category of secondary contact impurity.
15.24: And if any man lies with her and her menstrual impurity comes upon him (vetihyeh niddatah alav), he shall be unclean seven days, and every bed on which he lies shall be unclean — sexual intercourse during the niddah period creates a seven-day impurity for the man (not merely until-evening), and his bed becomes an impurity source during that period. Intercourse during niddah is treated more seriously than contact with niddah furniture.
15.25: If a woman has a discharge of blood for many days, not at the time of her menstrual impurity, or if she has a discharge beyond the time of her impurity, all the days of the discharge she shall continue in uncleanness — the irregular or prolonged discharge beyond the normal menstrual period creates an extended impurity state. The woman of Mark 5:25-34 (who had a flow of blood for twelve years) would have been in continuous niddah impurity under this provision throughout the twelve years, excluded from temple participation and making her contact with Jesus culturally transgressive.
15.26: Every bed on which she lies, all the days of her discharge, shall be to her as the bed of her menstrual impurity, and everything on which she sits shall be unclean, as the uncleanness of her menstrual impurity — the zavah (woman with irregular discharge) generates the same secondary impurity as the niddah throughout the entire period of the discharge, which may extend indefinitely. Every day of discharge creates the same contamination of her sleeping and sitting surfaces.
15.27: Whoever touches these things shall be unclean, and shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and be unclean until the evening — contact with the zavah's furniture creates the same until-evening impurity as contact with niddah furniture (vv.21-22). The transmission rules for irregular discharge match those for normal menstruation.
15.28: But if she is cleansed of her discharge, she shall count for herself seven days, and after that she shall be clean — as with the zav (v.13), the zavah must count seven clear days after the discharge ends before she can be pronounced clean; the symmetry between male and female chronic discharge legislation is complete. After the seven days she proceeds to the eighth-day offering (v.29).
15.29: And on the eighth day she shall take two turtledoves or two pigeons and bring them to the priest, to the entrance of the tent of meeting — the zavah's eighth-day offering (two birds) mirrors the zav's offering (v.14) exactly; both undergo identical restoration procedures. The turtledoves/pigeons are the standard provision for those who cannot afford larger animals.
15.30: And the priest shall use one for a sin offering and the other for a burnt offering. And the priest shall make atonement for her before YHWH for her unclean discharge — the atonement formula for the zavah is word-for-word identical to the formula for the zav (v.15), completing the structural symmetry of male and female discharge legislation.
15.31: Thus you shall keep the people of Israel separate from their uncleanness (ve-hizzartem et-bney Yisra'el mituma'tam), lest they die in their uncleanness by defiling my tabernacle (mishkani) that is in their midst — the stated purpose of the entire discharge legislation: separation from impurity prevents the death that occurs when uncleanness contacts the holy sanctuary. The tabernacle's presence among the people creates both the gift of divine nearness and the requirement of purity; the two are inseparable.
15.32: This is the law for him who has a discharge and for him who has an emission of semen, becoming unclean thereby — the closing summary of male discharge legislation: the zav (chronic discharge) and the man with semen emission are both included under torat ha-zav.
15.33: And for her who is unwell with her menstrual impurity and for anyone, male or female, who has a discharge, and for the man who lies with a woman who is unclean — the closing summary explicitly names: (1) the niddah, (2) any person with discharge regardless of sex, and (3) the man who has intercourse with a niddah. The final phrase asher yishkav im-niddah (who lies with a niddah) identifies this as a distinct category requiring its own mention in the summary, consistent with the elevated impurity level of v.24.

13.1: YHWH speaks to both Moses and Aaron — the only chapter in Leviticus explicitly addressed to both. The pairing is significant: the diagnostic role belongs to the priest (Aaron's line), while Moses transmits the legal framework. The dual address establishes that priestly authority over ritual purity is covenantal, not merely functional.
13.2: sebeth o sappachat o baheret (śeʾet ō-sappaḥat ō-baheret): three skin-condition terms whose precise distinctions remain debated. Sebeth (from nasa = to lift/carry) denotes a swelling or elevation in the skin. Sappachat (from saphach = to attach/join) is a secondary eruption or scab — something that clings to or spreads from the primary mark. Baheret (from bahar = to be bright/white) is a bright white spot. Together they form the range of surface presentations that may indicate nega tzaraat (negaʿ ṣāraʿat) — literally a blow or plague of tzaraat. Nega (from naga = to touch, strike, afflict) covers any afflicted area; its use implies something divinely inflicted. Tzaraat is traditionally rendered leprosy but is broader — it covers various skin conditions including vitiligo, psoriasis, and fungal infections; modern Hansen's disease was likely unknown in ancient Israel. The priest is the diagnostic authority: not a physician but a ritual judge who determines whether the condition crosses the threshold of covenant-disqualifying impurity.
13.3: veraah hakkohen et-hanega beohr habasar: the priest shall see/examine (raah) the mark. The verb raah (to see, examine, consider) carries the full weight of judicial examination — the same word used for prophetic vision and divine observation. The priest is not diagnosing disease medically but rendering a ritual verdict. The key diagnostic signs are: (1) sear halabban (śeʿar lābān) — white hair (hair in the mark has turned white), and (2) mareh hanega amok mivor habasar — the appearance of the mark is deeper than the surrounding skin. Both signs indicate active, penetrating affliction. The verdict formula nega tzaraat hu — it is a mark of tzaraat — is the priestly pronouncement that places the person in the unclean/quarantine category.
13.4: vehibdilo hakkohen shivat yamim: the priest shall isolate (hibdil) the person for seven days. Hibdil is the same root as havdalah (the Sabbath separation ceremony) — to separate, set apart. The diagnostic isolation of seven days mirrors the creation week: a full cycle of time in which the condition either resolves or develops. If the baheret (white spot) has not spread and the hair has not turned white, the case remains uncertain — it is a sappachat (scall), not full tzaraat, and requires further observation. The seven-day observation period builds discernment time into the diagnostic process, preventing premature condemnation or premature clearance.
13.5: vehibdilo shivat yamim sheniyot: a second seven-day isolation period is required if the mark has neither spread nor changed. The doubling of the observation period (7 + 7 = 14 days) reflects the gravity of the verdict: the priest must be sure before pronouncing someone tameh. This judicial caution — requiring multiple observations before a life-altering verdict — models the legal principle of careful witness and examination.
13.6: vetiher otho hakkohen: the priest shall pronounce him clean (tiher, from tahor = pure, clean). If the mark has faded and not spread, the condition is diagnosed as sappachat — a secondary eruption, not full tzaraat — and the priest declares the person clean. He must wash (kibbes) his garments and is then ritually clean. The washing of garments is a standard purity-restoration action, signaling that the contamination was real but bounded. The priest's declaration tahor hu (he is clean) is not medical opinion but priestly verdict — it restores full participation in the covenant community.
13.7: ve-im pasoah tifsheh hasappachat baohr: if the scall has at all spread (pasoh tifsheh — an infinitive absolute construction indicating emphasis and certainty of the spreading). The spreading is the diagnostic trigger for upgrading the verdict: what was a sappachat (minor eruption) now displays the hallmark of true tzaraat. The priest re-examines and re-pronounces. The double appearance before the priest — first at the initial examination, second if the condition changes — ensures that both clearance and condemnation require priestly oversight.
13.8: vetimaahu hakkohen tzaraat hu: the priest shall declare him unclean — tzaraat hu (it is tzaraat). This is the formal diagnosis that places a person in the category of those requiring isolation, special garments, and verbal self-announcement. Tameh (ṭāmēʾ) in the priestly system is not primarily a moral category but a ritual one — it describes a state of incompatibility with the holy. Those who are tameh cannot participate in worship, approach the tabernacle, or eat sacred food. The declaration is the priest's verbal act of categorization — performative speech that changes the person's social and religious status.
13.9: nega tzaraat ki tihyeh beadam: when a mark of tzaraat is in a person (beadam = in/on a human being, from adam, emphasizing the person's full humanity). This opening verse of the second diagnostic section introduces tzaraat noshenet — chronic, longstanding skin disease. The distinction between new/acute conditions (vv. 2-8) and chronic/established conditions (vv. 9-17) reflects a diagnostic wisdom: established disease that has reached a stable state may be treated differently from active, spreading disease.
13.10: sebeth lebana (white swelling) combined with sear laban (white hair in the swelling) and michyat basar chai basebeth — raw flesh in the swelling. Michyat (from michyah) refers to quick/raw flesh — living, exposed tissue. The combination of white swelling + white hair + raw flesh is diagnostic for full tzaraat. Basar chai (living/raw flesh) appearing within the mark is the key additional sign: the disease has penetrated to the living layer beneath the skin surface, indicating active, deep affliction.
13.11: tzaraat noshenet hi beohr besaro vetimaenu hakkohen lo yisgirenu ki tameh hu: chronic tzaraat — it is an old/established tzaraat in the skin of his body. The priest shall declare him unclean without isolating him, because he is already/clearly unclean. The diagnostic certainty of established disease removes the need for the 7-day observation period. Noshenet (from yashon = to be old/established) — the condition has been present long enough that its nature is not in doubt. This is the classic case of tzaraat that required no further observation: the verdict was self-evident.
13.12: ve-im paroach tifroach hatzaraat baohr: if the tzaraat has completely broken out over the skin — paroach tifroach (infinitive absolute for emphasis). When the disease has covered the entire body surface from head to foot in white, a paradoxical rule applies: the person may be declared clean. This seemingly counterintuitive ruling reflects the diagnostic logic: if the entire surface has turned white (kuloh hifoch laban), the raw flesh that indicates active disease is no longer visible. The condition has reached a kind of completion — it is not spreading, not showing raw flesh. This edge case demonstrates the sophisticated medical-legal reasoning of the priestly diagnostic system.
13.13: tahor hu: he is clean — the paradox of the fully blanched patient declared clean. The priest examines and seeing the entire surface white, pronounces clean. The principle appears to be: active infection shows red/raw flesh; the all-white condition is a static, resolved state even if visually dramatic. This ruling has generated significant rabbinic discussion and suggests the tzaraat regulations are not naive descriptions of disease progression but carefully worked-out ritual categories.
13.14: uveyom hera'ot bo basar chai yitma: on the day that raw flesh (basar chai) appears in it, he shall be unclean. The appearance of raw flesh reverses the clean verdict immediately. The raw flesh is the key diagnostic sign of active disease — open, living, exposed tissue indicates ongoing affliction. The system is dynamic: a person's status can change when the observable signs change. This mutability requires ongoing priestly oversight, not a one-time determination.
13.15: veraah hakkohen et habasar hachai vetimaenu basar hachai tameh hu tzaraat hu: the priest examines the raw flesh and declares unclean — raw flesh is unclean, it is tzaraat. The repetition of the diagnosis formula underscores the diagnostic principle: basar chai (raw/living flesh) = tzaraat = tameh. The formula serves as a diagnostic rule that priests could apply in any case where raw flesh appeared.
13.16: o chi yashov habasar hachai venehpach lelaban: or if the raw flesh turns back and changes to white. The reversibility continues: if the raw flesh later becomes white (the sign that active disease has resolved), the person shall come again to the priest.
13.17: vetiharah hakkohen tahor hu: the priest declares him clean — he is clean. The dynamic back-and-forth between tameh and tahor status depending on the observable condition of the skin marks represents a responsive system: status follows reality. The priest's ongoing access for re-examination means that isolation is not permanent by default; it is reassessed as the condition changes.
13.18: vebasar ki yihyeh vo shechin: and when there is a boil (shechin) in the skin. A new category: disease arising in the site of a previous boil. Shechin (from a root meaning to be hot/burn) is an inflammatory skin infection. The boil itself is not tzaraat, but the site of a healed boil can become a locus of tzaraat-like symptoms. This specificity — categorizing conditions by their etiology and location — demonstrates the detailed observational basis of the priestly diagnostic system.
13.19: sebeth lebana o baheret lebana adamdemet: a white swelling or white-reddish bright spot. A new color term appears: adamdemet (reddish-white) — from adom = red. The combination of white and reddish suggests an inflamed border around a white center, or a bicolored lesion. This variant presentation at the site of a previous boil requires the same priestly examination as primary tzaraat.
13.20: im-mareh shafal min-haohr vesear hefach laban vetimaenu hakkohen nega tzaraat hi bashechin parach: if the appearance is lower than the skin and the hair has turned white, the priest shall declare him unclean — it is a mark of tzaraat that has broken out in the boil. The same diagnostic signs as primary tzaraat (depth below skin level + white hair) applied to the boil-site confirm tzaraat diagnosis.
13.21: ve-im yireenu hakkohen vehineh ein-bo sear laban ushefalah einenu min-haohr vehiv kehavah: but if the priest examines it and there is no white hair in it and it is not lower than the skin but has become dim/faded. Kehavah (dim, faded) indicates a resolving lesion without the active diagnostic signs. This condition is isolated for seven days — the same precautionary observation period as uncertain initial presentations.
13.22: ve-im pasoah tifsheh baohr: if it spreads in the skin, the priest shall declare him unclean. The spreading criterion applies across all skin conditions — spreading = active disease = tzaraat = tameh.
13.23: ve-im-tachteha ta'amod habaheret lo farach tzaraat hi uvahe zakah: but if the bright spot stays in its place and has not spread, it is the scar (tzavevet) of the boil — the priest shall declare him clean. The non-spreading bright spot at the boil site is diagnosed as cicatrix (scar tissue from healing), not tzaraat. This distinction between scar tissue and active disease is clinically perceptive: healed scars often produce white tissue that resembles the baheret but lacks the diagnostic signs of active tzaraat.
13.24: o basar ki yihyeh vo michvat-esh: or when there is a burn (michvat-esh = burning of fire) in the skin. Michvat (from kavah = to burn) and esh (fire) together describe a burn wound. Like the boil site, the burn site can develop tzaraat-like presentations. The range of etiologies — natural skin conditions, post-boil, post-burn — shows the comprehensiveness of the diagnostic framework.
13.25: veraah oto hakkohen vehineh nehpach sear laban babaheret umaraeha amok min-haohr tzaraat hi bamichvah parach: same diagnostic formula as before — white hair in the bright spot + appearance deeper than skin = tzaraat in the burn wound. The consistent application of the same diagnostic criteria across different etiological backgrounds demonstrates the underlying principle: the nature of the condition (its observable markers) determines the verdict, not its origin.
13.26: ve-im lo yireeh bakohenet sear laban: but if no white hair is seen in the bright spot and it is not lower than the skin. The absence of the two primary diagnostic signs leads to the precautionary 7-day isolation. The system defaults to protective isolation when evidence is ambiguous — a harm-reduction principle built into the diagnostic protocol.
13.27: The spreading criterion again determines the verdict: if the baheret has spread in seven days, the priest declares tameh — tzaraat. The uniformity of the spreading test across all presentations demonstrates its central diagnostic significance.
13.28: ve-im tachteha ta'amod habaheret lo farach baohr vehiv kehavah sebeth hamichvah hi: if the bright spot stays in its place and has not spread in the skin but is dim, it is the swelling of the burn — the priest declares him clean. Sebeth hamichvah (swelling/scarring of the burn) — the same principle as boil-site: non-spreading, fading lesions at a burn site are diagnosed as healing scar tissue, not tzaraat.
13.29: ve-ish o isha ki yihyeh vo nega berosh o vezaqan: a man or a woman when there is a mark in the head or in the beard. The head and facial hair regions present a specific diagnostic challenge: nithak (neteq) is the term for a scaly/ringworm-like condition of the scalp or beard area, distinct from the general tzaraat of the body surface. The specificity of nithak as a diagnostic subcategory reflects observation that scalp and beard conditions have distinctive presentations.
13.30: veraah hakkohen et-hanega vehineh maraehu amok min-haohr uso dak tzahov tzaraat harosh o-hazzaqan hu: if the appearance is deeper than the skin and there is fine/thin yellow-gold hair in it — it is a scall (nithak) of the head or beard. The distinctive diagnostic sign here is seara dak tzahov (fine, thin, yellow/golden hair) rather than white hair — a color variant specific to this head/beard condition. The fine yellow hair suggests the hair follicle itself has been affected differently than in body-surface tzaraat.
13.31: vehibdilo et-nega hanithak shivat yamim: the priest shall isolate the person with the nithak-mark for seven days. If the nithak has not spread and there is no yellow hair in it, the priest keeps the affected area shaved except the nithak-mark itself, and the person is isolated a second seven days for reassessment.
13.32: veraah hakkohen et-hanega bayom hashevii vehineh lo-farash hanithak: on the seventh day the priest examines — if the nithak has not spread and there is no yellow hair and its appearance is not deeper than the skin. The clean/unclear diagnostic criteria applied to nithak parallel those for body-surface tzaraat, but adapted for the scalp/beard context.
13.33: vehitgalach veet-hanithak lo yegalach vehibdilo hakkohen et-hanithak shivat yamim sheniyot: he shall shave himself, but he shall not shave the nithak itself, and the priest shall isolate the nithak an additional seven days. The prohibition against shaving the affected area preserves the diagnostic evidence: if the yellow-fine hair fell out, the diagnostic criterion would be lost. The second isolation period serves the same function as in the body-surface cases.
13.34: veraah hakkohen et-hanithak bayom hashevii vehineh lo-farash hanithak baohr umaraehu einenu amok min-haohr vetiher oto hakkohen: on the seventh day — if the nithak has not spread and its appearance is not deeper than the skin, the priest declares clean. The clean verdict requires non-spreading + non-deepening — the same two-sign criterion throughout the chapter.
13.35: ve-im pasoah yifsheh hanithak baohr achar taharo: but if the nithak should spread in the skin after his cleansing. The possibility of relapse after a clean verdict — and the requirement to return to the priest if this occurs — demonstrates that clean status is not permanent if the condition recurs. The ongoing relationship between patient and priest is a dynamic monitoring system, not a one-time determination.
13.36: veraah oto hakkohen vehineh parash hanithak baohr lo-yevaqer hakkohen lasear hatzahov tameh hu: the priest shall examine — if the nithak has spread in the skin, the priest need not look for yellow hair in it — he is unclean. When the spreading sign is present, it overrides the need for the yellow-hair sign. This diagnostic hierarchy shows that spreading is the more definitive indicator — the yellow hair was needed for initial diagnosis but spreading alone is sufficient for re-diagnosis.
13.37: ve-im-beinav amad hanithak vesear shahor tzamach bo nikra hanithak tahor hu vetiharah hakkohen: but if in his sight the nithak has stayed and black hair has grown in it — the nithak is healed. Sear shahor (black hair) indicates complete regrowth of normal hair, a sign that the condition has fully resolved. The regrowth of dark, healthy hair visibly replaces the fine yellow hair of active nithak — a positive diagnostic sign of healing. The priest declares clean.
13.38: ve-ish o isha ki yihyeh beohr besaram behaqot behaqot levanot: a man or woman who has in the skin of the body white-dull spots (behaqot levanot). Bohaq (v39) is the term for this presentation. Behaqot (plural of behaq) refers to dull, pale-white spots — from a root suggesting dimness or pallor rather than the bright white of baheret. This is a separate category from tzaraat.
13.39: veraah hakkohen vehineh beohr besaram behaqot kehot levanot bohaq hu parach baohr tahor hu: the priest examines — if the dull-white spots on the skin are dim-white, it is bohaq (tetter/freckle) — it has erupted in the skin; he is clean. Bohaq appears only here in the Bible; it is classified as a mild, harmless condition that does not require isolation or treatment. Its inclusion establishes that not every white skin condition is tzaraat — the diagnostic system discriminates between similar presentations based on specific observable criteria.
13.40: veish ki yimaret rosho qerach hu tahor hu: a man whose head has lost hair — he is qerach (bald at the back of the head); he is clean. Qerach refers to posterior/occipital baldness. The explicit clean declaration for natural baldness establishes a category distinction: natural hair loss is not a symptom of ritual impurity. The chapter carefully separates pathological from non-pathological presentations.
13.41: ve-im mipeat panav yimaret rosho gibe'ach hu tahor hu: if the hair has fallen from the forehead side — he is gibe'ach (bald at the forehead/front); he is clean. Gibe'ach refers to frontal baldness. Both posterior and frontal baldness are declared clean — natural alopecia of any type is explicitly not tzaraat. This prevents the diagnostic category from being applied too broadly.
13.42: ve-im yihyeh baqarach o vegibbe'ach nega levanah adamdemet: but if in the bald spot (qerach or gibbe'ach) there is a white-reddish mark. The presence of a specific lesion in the bald area, rather than natural baldness itself, triggers the diagnostic protocol. The white-reddish mark on a bald scalp is evaluated as potential tzaraat. The condition of the bald skin, not the baldness itself, determines ritual status.
13.43: veraah oto hakkohen vehineh sebeth-hanega lebana adamdemet beqarchato o begibbe'achto kemareeh tzaraat ohr habasar: the priest examines — if the white-reddish swelling of the mark in his bald-back or bald-forehead looks like the appearance of tzaraat of the body skin, he is a tzaraat person. Kemareeh tzaraat ohr habasar (like the appearance of tzaraat of the skin of the body) — the bald-scalp lesion is evaluated by comparison to the established tzaraat appearance. Analogy to known cases is the diagnostic method.
13.44: ish tzarua hu tameh hu: he is a man diseased with tzaraat (tzarua = tzaraat-afflicted) — he is unclean. The cohen tiameh oto berosh nega-o — the priest shall declare him thoroughly unclean; his mark is on his head. The emphatic construction (tiameh oto, pi'el intensive) combined with specifying the head location indicates that head-tzaraat carries a specific severity in the declaration protocol.
13.45: vehatzarua asher bo hanega begadav yihyu ferumim veroho yihyeh faru vehat-sifam ya'ateh vetumaah yiqra: and the tzaraat-afflicted person in whom the mark is — his garments shall be torn (ferumim), his head shall be uncovered (faru), he shall cover his upper lip (yachpeh), and he shall cry out: Unclean, Unclean (tameh tameh). Four visible markers of the tameh status: (1) torn garments — the sign of mourning (cf. Job 1:20, Joel 2:13); (2) uncovered/disheveled head (faru = to loosen, let hang freely); (3) covered upper lip — a sign of mourning or debasement (cf. Ezek 24:17, Mic 3:7); (4) the ritual cry tameh tameh — the doubled form for emphasis. The public self-announcement serves as warning to others and as the tzaruat person's own acknowledgment of their status. The mourning signs signal that tameh status is experienced as a kind of living death — exclusion from community, worship, and full life.
13.46: kol-yemei asher hanega vo yitma badad yeshev michutz lamachane moshavo: all the days that the mark is in him he shall be unclean — he shall dwell badad (alone, apart) and his dwelling shall be michutz lamachane (outside the camp). Badad (alone, in isolation) — the same root appears in Lamentations 1:1 where personified Jerusalem sits badad (alone) in exile. The tzaraat isolation is described with the same vocabulary of desolation and exile. Michutz lamachane (outside the camp) is the zone of maximum separation — where corpses, human waste, and those under judgment were placed (cf. Num 5:2-3; Exod 29:14). The isolation removes the person from the sacred community surrounding the tabernacle. This geography of exclusion is deliberately structured around proximity to God's dwelling: the closer to YHWH, the higher the purity required.
13.47: vehabeged ki yihyeh vo nega tzaraat: and when a garment has in it a mark of tzaraat. Verses 47-59 extend the tzaraat regulations to fabric — wool, linen, or leather. The application of tzaraat categories to inanimate objects (garments, later houses in ch. 14) is one of the most distinctive features of the Levitical system. This extension is not biologically plausible — fabric does not contract infectious disease in the modern sense — which points toward the ritual/symbolic nature of tzaraat: it is a category of visible corruption/contamination that affects persons, garments, and structures in a spiritually analogous way.
13.48: bishti oba'erev letsemer o lefishtan o beor o bechol-melechet or: in the warp or weft of wool or of linen, or in leather or any article of leather. The precision of specifying both shti (warp threads) and erev (weft threads) demonstrates the detail of the inspection system. The tzaraat can appear in either structural element of woven fabric — the lengthwise or crosswise threads — and either location triggers the same examination protocol.
13.49: vehayah hanega yaraqraq o adamdam babeged o baohr o bashti o baerev o bechol-keli-or: and if the mark in the garment or leather or warp or weft or any article of leather is yaraqraq (greenish-yellow) or adamdam (reddish). Two color terms distinguish fabric tzaraat from skin tzaraat (which was white/bright or white-reddish). Yaraqraq (from yereq = green; the doubled form indicates intensity) = greenish. Adamdam (from adom = red; doubled for intensity) = reddish. These colors may indicate mold (green-black fungi) or oxidation on fabric. The color-based diagnostic for fabric parallels the appearance-based diagnostic for skin.
13.50: veraah hakkohen et-hanega vehibdil et-hanega shivat yamim: the priest shall examine the mark and isolate the marked item for seven days. The same 7-day observation period applied to persons with uncertain skin conditions is applied to potentially contaminated garments. The priest isolates the garment just as he isolates a person — the diagnostic protocol is structurally identical whether the subject is human skin or woven fiber.
13.51: veraah et-hanega bayom hashevii ki farash hanega babeged o bashti o baerev o baohr: if on the seventh day the mark has spread in the garment or warp or weft or leather — it is a tzaraat memaareret (malignant/fretting tzaraat) — it is unclean (tameh hu). Memaareret (from marar = to be bitter, to cause harm, to fret/corrode) describes an active, eating tzaraat — the disease that is spreading and consuming the material. The garment is declared tameh and must be burned (v.52).
13.52: vesaraf et-habeged: he shall burn the garment — wool or linen warp or weft, or any leather article with the malignant tzaraat — it shall be burned with fire. Burning is the most complete form of decontamination: it removes the object entirely from the realm of potential contamination. The destruction of contaminated garments rather than cleaning them reflects the irreversibility of tzaraat memaareret — once active spreading corruption has been established, the object cannot be remediated.
13.53: ve-im yireeh hakkohen vehineh lo farash hanega babeged: but if the priest examines and the mark has not spread in the garment. The non-spreading condition leads to a second option: washing and re-examination rather than immediate burning.
13.54: vetzivah hakkohen vechibesu et asher-bo hanega vehibdilo shivat yamim sheniyot: the priest shall command that the affected part be washed (chibesu) and isolated another seven days. Washing attempts remediation before destruction — the grace of trying to restore before condemning, parallel to the 7+7 day observation for human cases.
13.55: veraah hakkohen acharei hikabes et-hanega vehineh lo-hafach hanega et-einav vehanega lo farash tameh hu ba-esh tisrof: if after washing the mark has not changed its color/appearance and has not spread — it is unclean, burn it in fire, whether the affected part is the inner side or outer side. Lo hafach et-einav (has not changed its eye/appearance) — the mark's appearance is the ongoing diagnostic criterion. Even if it has not spread, the unchanged appearance after washing indicates persistent contamination requiring destruction. Pechemet hi (it is a fretting, whether in the back or face) — the location within the garment is noted but does not change the verdict.
13.56: ve-im raah hakkohen vehineh kehah hanega acharei hibakso: but if after washing the mark has dimmed/faded (kehah), the priest shall tear it out from the garment or from the leather. The fading indicates partial remediation — the contamination is diminishing but not fully cleared. The response is surgical removal of the affected portion rather than destroying the entire garment.
13.57: ve-im yereh od babeged o bashti o baerev o bechol keli-or tzaraat porach hi: but if it appears again in the garment or warp or weft or any leather article — it is tzaraat bursting forth (porach) — burn the item in fire. The recurrence of the mark after partial remediation demonstrates that the contamination is persistent and not merely superficial. At that point, destruction is the only remaining option. Porach (bursting out) describes a vigorous, recurring contamination that resists treatment.
13.58: vehabeged o hashti o-ha'erev o kol keli-haor asher tichabes vesfar mehem hanega uchubes sheniyot vetaher: but the garment, or warp, or weft, or any leather article that is washed and the mark is gone from it — it shall be washed a second time and shall be clean. The double washing (first remediation attempt + second confirming wash) required before declaring clean mirrors the double 7-day isolation for uncertain human cases. The thoroughness of the process ensures that clean declarations are not made prematurely.
13.59: zot torat nega-hatzaraat beged hashemer o-hafishtan o hashti o ha-erev o kol-keli-or letaharo o letameo: this is the law (torah) of the mark of tzaraat in a garment of wool or linen, in warp or weft or any leather article, to pronounce it clean or to pronounce it unclean. Torah (instruction, law) here closes the chapter as a legislative summary — the diagnostic procedures of vv. 47-58 constitute a torah, a formal body of instruction. The chapter as a whole (vv. 1-59) establishes the priestly diagnostic system for tzaraat in persons (vv. 1-46) and objects (vv. 47-59) — a comprehensive framework for managing the ritual contamination category across the material world. The priest's function throughout is not healing but discernment: to recognize, name, and properly classify what is present, then pronounce the appropriate verdict.

2.1: minchah (H4503): 'grain offering, tribute.' From a root meaning 'to give, to present' (related to the verb manah = to apportion). In older biblical usage minchah can refer to any tribute or gift (Gen 32:14 Jacob's gift to Esau; 1 Sam 10:27). By Leviticus it has specialized to the non-animal tribute offering. Solet (H5560): fine/sifted flour (as opposed to coarser qemach). Shemen (H8081): olive oil. Levonah (H3828): frankincense — a costly aromatic resin. The minchah accompanies the animal offerings as a secondary tribute; it is also appropriate as the offering of those who cannot afford animals (Lev 5:11).
2.2: 'azkara (H234): 'memorial portion' — from zakar (to remember). The priest burns a representative handful of flour, oil, and all the frankincense as the 'azkara — the portion that makes the whole offering 'remembered' (zakar) before YHWH. The Latin term is pars pro toto — a part that represents the whole. The theological claim is that the burning of a portion has the full effect of burning the whole: the representational logic underlying the entire sacrificial system (and by extension all atonement by substitution).
2.3: qodesh qodashim (H6944 doubled): 'most holy' — the superlative formed by doubling the noun. The remainder of the minchah after the 'azkara is burned belongs to Aaron and his sons. The classification qodesh qodashim signals that priestly portions carry the highest degree of holiness — they must be eaten within the sanctuary by male priests only. The graduated holiness system (most holy / holy / clean / unclean) is the spatial-moral architecture of Leviticus.
2.4: challot matza and raqiqim: 'unleavened loaves' and 'unleavened wafers.' The minchah baked in an oven must be unleavened. Matza (H4682, unleavened bread) is a requirement for all grain offerings offered as isheh (fire offering to YHWH) — Lev 2:11 will explain that leaven cannot be offered. The unleavened requirement for any grain offering brought into the altar's fire reinforces that the fire-offering category is set apart from everyday baked goods.
2.5: al ha-machavat (machavat, H4227): 'on the flat griddle/pan.' A shallow, flat griddle produces a thin, unleavened cake. Three cooking methods are specified (oven, griddle, deep pan) because the minchah can be prepared at different domestic facilities. The flexibility of preparation method ensures that the grain offering is accessible regardless of the offerer's cooking equipment. The common requirement across all methods: fine flour, oil, and unleavened.
2.6: pittim pittim: 'into pieces, pieces' — intensive repetition indicating small, evenly broken fragments. Pit (H6595): a morsel/fragment. The griddle-baked grain offering is broken into pieces and oil poured over them before being brought to the priest. The breaking and oiling is a preparatory act by the offerer before the priests handle it. The repeated breaking (intensive plural) suggests thoroughness: not roughly torn but properly portioned.
2.7: marcheshet (H4802): 'deep pan' or 'frying pan/cauldron' — a deeper vessel producing a thicker, richer product than the griddle. Only three mentions in the OT (all in Leviticus: 2:7; 7:9; 1 Chr 23:29 variant). The deep-pan grain offering is made with fine flour and oil like the others. The diversity of cooking vessels accommodates the range of domestic food preparation in ancient Israel while maintaining consistent sacrificial requirements.
2.8: hiqriva (from qarav): 'to present/bring near' — the same root as qorban (v. 1:2). The grain offering is 'brought near' to the priest who then carries it to the altar. The repeated use of qarav vocabulary throughout Leviticus consistently frames sacrifice as approach, not payment: the worshiper draws near to God through the medium of the offering.
2.9: The priest takes the 'azkara portion from the grain offering and burns it on the altar — reah nihoah la-YHWH (pleasing aroma to YHWH). The memorial-portion logic applies to all forms of the minchah regardless of preparation method: a representative portion goes up in smoke, and the rest goes to the priests. The 'azkara is always wholly burned; it is the part that enacts the full transaction before YHWH.
2.10: The remainder (ha-noteret) of the grain offering belongs to Aaron and his sons — again classified as qodesh qodashim (most holy), echoing v. 3. The double classification (vv. 3 and 10) bookends the section on preparation variants, establishing that regardless of cooking method, the priestly remainder carries the same high holiness grade and the same consumption requirements.
2.11: chametz (H2557): 'leaven, leavened bread' — from a root meaning 'to ferment.' Devash (H1706): 'honey' (or fruit syrup/date-honey). Both leaven and honey are forbidden from any grain offering brought as a fire offering (isheh) to YHWH. The reasons are not stated explicitly; the probable theological intuition is that fermentation and sweetening represent processes of transformation and corruption that are inappropriate for what is presented as pure tribute. In Israel's broader religious world, leaven and honey were used in Canaanite sacrificial contexts, which may inform their exclusion from Israelite altar practice.
2.12: bikkurim (H1061): 'firstfruits' — from bakhar (to be early, to be first). The first produce of the harvest can be brought to YHWH as an offering (qorban), but the firstfruits are not to be offered on the altar as a reah nihoah. This differentiates the grain-offering system (which can generate reah nihoah via the 'azkara) from the firstfruits offering, which is presented but not burned as incense. Lev 23:9-14 will specify the firstfruits wave-offering in detail.
2.13: melach ha-berit Elohekha (melach, H4417): 'the salt of the covenant of your God.' Salt is a required addition to every grain offering: 'do not omit the salt of the covenant.' Salt preserves and prevents corruption; in ancient Near Eastern contexts, salt-covenant language denotes the permanence and trustworthiness of a treaty or agreement. Berit melach ('covenant of salt') appears also in Num 18:19 (the priestly covenant) and 2 Chr 13:5 (the Davidic covenant) — a perpetual, incorruptible commitment. Every grain offering seasoned with salt is a small enactment of the covenant's permanent character.
2.14: 'omer geresh (geresh, H1643): 'crushed/beaten grain.' Aviv (H24): 'fresh/young grain ears' — in early spring, before full ripening. The firstfruits grain offering uses aviv (the name of the first month, Chodesh ha-Aviv, is derived from this term). The grain is roasted (qalui ba-esh) and then beaten into crushed pieces before being brought. The firstfruits offering thus uses grain at its earliest stage — the beginning of the harvest — as representative of the whole yield to come.
2.15: venatata aleiha shemen vesamt aleiha levonah: oil poured on and frankincense placed on the firstfruits grain offering — making it a proper minchah in form. The combination of crushed grain, oil, and frankincense is the standard minchah triad (as in v. 1). The firstfruits offering, despite its distinct occasion (harvest-beginning), uses the same sacred vocabulary of materials as the regular minchah.
2.16: The priest burns the 'azkara (memorial portion) — some of the geresh (crushed grain) with some of the oil and all the frankincense — as a reah nihoah la-YHWH. The 'azkara pattern closes the grain-offering chapter as it opened it (v. 2): whatever the variety, form, or occasion of the minchah, the memorial-portion logic applies. A part, properly burned, makes the whole acceptable.

3.1: zevach shelamim (H2077+H8002): 'fellowship offering' (also rendered 'peace offering,' 'well-being offering'). Shelem derives from shalom (wholeness, completeness, peace) or from shalem (to complete, to repay, to be at peace). The shelamim is the only offering from which the offerer also eats — after YHWH's portion (the fat burned on the altar) and the priestly portion, the offerer and family share a communal meal. It is a meal of reconciled community between the worshiper, the priests, and YHWH. Tamim (H8549): without defect — applies to both male and female animals in the shelamim, unlike the 'olah which required male only.
3.2: vesomach yado al rosh qorbano: laying of the hand on the offering's head — the semikhah gesture (covered in depth at 1:4). Shachat: slaughter. Zaraq dam: throw/toss blood. The same three acts (hand-laying, slaughter, blood-sprinkling) that characterize the 'olah initiate the shelamim as well. The shared ritual structure across offering types signals that they participate in the same fundamental logic of substitution and approach, even as the disposition of the animal differs.
3.3: chelev (H2459): 'fat' — specifically the superior, internal fat covering the organs (as distinct from the ordinary fat of muscle). The chelev belongs exclusively to YHWH: 3:16 will state kol chelev la-YHWH ('all fat is for YHWH'), and 3:17 forbids any Israelite from eating it. The best fat as YHWH's portion communicates that the highest and richest part of the animal belongs to the giver of all life. The same logic governs firstfruits (agricultural best to YHWH) and firstborn (progeny-best to YHWH).
3.4: kelayot (H3629): 'kidneys' — in Hebrew thought the kidneys (like the heart) are associated with the moral and emotional interior of a person (kelayot va-lev = 'kidneys and heart' = the deepest self, e.g. Ps 16:7; 73:21; Jer 11:20). Offering the kidneys to YHWH symbolizes offering the hidden interior. Yoteret al ha-kaved: the fatty lobe or appendage of the liver. The liver was associated in ANE divination with revealing divine will (haruspicy). Offering this organ to YHWH implicitly rejects human divination: the 'divine indicator' is burned up as YHWH's portion, not inspected by humans.
3.5: al ha-'olah: burned 'on top of the burnt offering' on the altar. The shelamim's fat portions are added to the already-burning 'olah, not offered separately. The layering of offerings on the altar is a feature of temple worship: the perpetual 'olah (the tamid) serves as the fire-base on which all other offerings are placed. The cumulative burning speaks to the multi-dimensional nature of Israel's relationship with YHWH: dedication ('olah), tribute (minchah), and fellowship (shelamim) all ascending together.
3.6: Zevach shelamim la-YHWH min ha-tzon — from the flock. The shelamim allows both male and female animals; this chapter covers cattle (vv. 1-5), sheep (vv. 6-11), and goats (vv. 12-16). The variety of acceptable animals for the fellowship offering reflects its communal and celebratory character — it is not restricted to the most costly animal. The shared meal it enables is available at different economic levels.
3.7: Kesev (H3775): 'lamb' (specifically a male sheep). Presented before YHWH at the altar entrance. The lamb presentation begins the sheep variant of the fellowship offering. The gesture of presenting (hiqriv, from qarav) before YHWH at the tent entrance is the same approach-vocabulary used throughout: every sacrifice begins with drawing near.
3.8: Semicha (laying of hands), slaughter, and blood-tossing are repeated identically for sheep — confirming the structural consistency of the shelamim ritual across animal types. The blood against the altar (al ha-mizbeach saviv) is the same zaraq-gesture as the 'olah. The blood-application at the altar base is the act that formally completes the transfer: the animal's life, represented in its blood, is given to YHWH at the altar.
3.9: 'alyah (H451): the 'fat tail' — a term specific to the fat-tailed sheep breeds (dumba) common in the ancient Near East, which carry a large deposit of fat in the tail. The fat tail is specified for removal along with the chelev in sheep offerings; it belongs to YHWH's portion just as the internal fat does. The specification of a breed-specific anatomical feature shows the precision of the ritual system — it accounts for the biological realities of the available sacrificial animals.
3.10: The kidneys and liver lobe are described identically for sheep as for cattle (v. 4), using the same Hebrew terms. This verbatim repetition across animal categories (kelayot veha-yoteret al ha-kaved) reinforces that the theological significance is in the organs themselves — interior, hidden, decision-making organs — rather than in which animal they come from. The consistency of organ-theology across species is deliberate.
3.11: lechem isheh la-YHWH (lechem, H3899 + isheh, H801): 'food of a fire offering to YHWH.' Isheh is a technical term for what is offered in fire; lechem (bread/food) applied to sacrifice describes what the altar-fire 'feeds' YHWH with — a bold anthropomorphic idiom. The LXX renders it with karpoma (fruit-offering) to avoid the anthropomorphism. The Hebrew text is unashamed to use food language for YHWH's reception of the sacrifice: the covenant God is the host of a meal at which both he and his people eat.
3.12: 'Ez (H5795): goat (female goat or the species generally). The goat variant of the shelamim is presented with the same formula as the sheep (vv. 7-11). The standardized formula for all three animal variants (cattle, sheep, goat) communicates that the shelamim is a genuinely accessible offering: cattle for the wealthy, sheep for the middle, goats for those with less. The fellowship-meal it enables is not socioeconomically restricted.
3.13: The goat's semicha, slaughter, and blood-tossing are identical to the prior animal variants. The ritual-formula repetition is characteristic of Leviticus's literary style: the repetition is not editorial redundancy but liturgical specification. The reader (and the officiating priest) must perform each act for each animal exactly as specified; the verbal repetition encodes the requirement that no shortcut is acceptable.
3.14: The fat covering the entrails (chelev ha-mekaseh et ha-qerev) is specified for the goat offering identically to cattle (v. 3). Chelev is the technically classified 'best fat' — the subcategory of internal organ fat that belongs exclusively to YHWH (v. 16). The exact specification of which fat (not all fat, but the classified chelev) prevents confusion between the dietary law and the offering requirement.
3.15: The kidneys and liver lobe again — identical in all three animal categories. The threefold repetition of these specific organ designations (vv. 4, 10, 15) establishes them as the fixed 'interior theology' of the shelamim: whatever the animal, the inner most-significant organs go to YHWH. The offerer eats the rest; the priest receives the breast and thigh (Lev 7:28-34); YHWH receives the fat, blood, kidneys, and liver-lobe.
3.16: Kol chelev la-YHWH: 'all the fat belongs to YHWH.' This concluding declaration for the shelamim chapters is a categorical claim: the best fat of any animal, in any offering context, is YHWH's portion. Reah nihoah closes the offering formula. The chelev-reservation is paired with blood-reservation in v. 17: the two elements most associated with life and fertility in ANE food and religious culture are both entirely YHWH's — they may not be consumed by humans in any context.
3.17: Chuqat 'olam ledoroteichem (chuqat 'olam, H2708+H5769): 'a perpetual ordinance throughout your generations' — the strongest legal formula in Leviticus, indicating a non-negotiable, permanent prohibition. Chelev and dam (blood) may not be eaten wherever you dwell. The double prohibition (no fat, no blood) applies outside the sacrificial context as well — in any food. The life-blood principle (Lev 17:11) and the best-portion principle (fat to YHWH) together define the outer boundaries of Israelite food ethics: what is most life-containing belongs exclusively to the life-giver.

4.1: The divine-speech formula vayedabber YHWH el-Moshe lemor (the LORD spoke to Moses, saying) introduces the fourth major offering type. This formulaic opener distinguishes the chattat from the offerings in chs. 1-3: the purification offering is presented as a separate divine command, not a continuation of the previous instructions. The break marks the conceptual distinction between tribute/fellowship offerings (chs. 1-3) and the purgation offering for sin (ch. 4).
4.2: bishgagah (H7684): 'unintentionally, through inadvertence' — from shagag (to go astray, to err). The chattat (purification offering) covers only sins committed bishgagah, not intentional high-handed transgressions (beyad ramah, Num 15:30-31, for which no sacrifice is available — only divine mercy or death). The distinction between inadvertent and deliberate sin is fundamental to the sacrificial system's scope. The chattat is for what was done without full moral intent; it does not mechanically erase premeditated rebellion.
4.3: ha-kohen ha-mashiach (mashiach, H4899): 'the anointed priest' — the high priest. The same root as meshiach (Messiah/Christ = the anointed one). The anointed priest's unintentional sin is treated with the gravest category of sin-offering (a bull, the most expensive animal) because his sin carries communal consequences: le'ashmat ha-'am — 'bringing guilt on the people.' The leader's moral failure implicates the entire community he represents. This principle of representative contamination applies at every social level in ch. 4 (priest, community, leader, individual).
4.4: Semicha on the bull's head before the tent of meeting — the same identification gesture as in the 'olah (1:4) and shelamim (3:2,8,13). The laying of hands on the chattat bull formally identifies the offerer with the sacrifice, transferring his sin to the animal. The physical contact transfers not just identity but guilt ('avon) — the animal becomes his representative before YHWH as a guilt-bearer.
4.5: El ohel mo'ed: the blood of the high priest's chattat is brought inside the tent of meeting — a feature unique to this category of offering. The blood-application at the incense altar inside the tent (v. 7) is the most sacred blood ritual in the regular calendar. The spatial escalation (courtyard altar vs. inner sanctuary) reflects the severity of the sin being addressed: the higher the rank of the sinner, the deeper into the sanctuary the atonement must reach.
4.6: Vehizel ha-kohen et etzba'o min ha-dam: 'The priest dips his finger in the blood.' Sheva pe'amim: 'seven times' — the seven-fold sprinkling before the parochet (the sanctuary veil). The number seven is the covenant-completion number throughout the OT (seven-day week, seven-year sabbatical, seven-fold sprinkling). The sprinkling before the veil directs the blood toward the Most Holy Place without entering it — the atonement-effect reaches the holiest space through blood-application at its threshold.
4.7: Qarnot ha-mizbeach (qeren, H7161): 'horns of the altar' — the four projections at the corners of the incense altar. Blood applied to the altar's horns (repeated across multiple offering types) is the most graphic form of blood-application: the horns are the altar's most prominent points, where the power of the sacrifice is concentrated. The pouring of the remainder at the base (el yesod mizbeach ha-'olah) disposes of the blood with reference to the burnt-offering altar — the two altars are connected in the purification ritual.
4.8: Chelev portions specified for the chattat bull — identical to the shelamim (vv. 8-10 repeat 3:3-5 verbatim). The fat-burning on the altar of burnt offering applies across all offering types where an animal is offered: the best fat always goes to YHWH regardless of the offering's purpose. The fat-theology (v. 3:16 kol chelev la-YHWH) is not suspended for the sin offering; purification and tribute both require giving the best.
4.9: Kelayot (kidneys) and liver lobe specified identically to the shelamim — the interior organs belong to YHWH even in the purgation offering. The theological claim is consistent: even when the offering is about removing sin rather than celebrating fellowship, the hidden, interior-self organs are YHWH's portion. What is most inward is most holy and most fully YHWH's.
4.10: 'Just as they are removed from the ox of the shelamim' — the explicit cross-reference to 3:3-5. The identical fat-portion instructions for the chattat and the shelamim signal that the altar's receiving of YHWH's portion is a constant across offering types. The ritual logic is portable: what belongs to YHWH from the fellowship offering belongs to him from the purification offering as well. The divine portion is not renegotiable based on the offering's purpose.
4.11: 'Or ha-par (the bull's hide) + kol bisaro (all its flesh) — head, legs, entrails, and dung — the entire animal minus the fat portions burned on the altar. The priests receive no portion from the high priest's or the community's chattat bull (unlike individual sin-offerings in 6:26 where the priest eats). The complete disposal of the carcass outside the camp marks the severity of these communal or priestly sin offerings: no benefit returns to any human party.
4.12: El-mikhon ma'aneh ha-deshen: to the 'clean place' outside the camp where the ashes are disposed of. The entire bull — hide, flesh, head, legs, entrails, dung — is carried outside and burned on wood. The burning-outside-the-camp is the complete removal of the sin-material from the covenant community. Its exclusion from the priestly food-system and the camp itself visualizes what atonement accomplishes: the sin-bearing material is expelled and destroyed.
4.13: Kat ha-'edah kullah ('the entire community') sinning bishgagah — when the corporate body commits an unintentional violation. The extension of the offering system to cover the community as a collective (not just individuals) shows that Israel is conceived as a covenanted corporate entity before YHWH: the community can sin as a body, and the community must make atonement as a body. Venelam davar min ha-'edah — 'the matter was hidden from the assembly' — unintentional community sin by definition involves obscured moral perception.
4.14: When the communal sin becomes known (nodat hachata't), the assembly brings a par ben baqar (young bull from the herd) — the same animal as the high priest's offering (v. 3). The matched animal for priest and community confirms that the community bears collective guilt equivalent to that of its highest leader. The size of the required offering scales with the scope of the sinner's representative authority.
4.15: Ziqne ha-'edah (zaqen, H2205): 'the elders of the community.' The elders lay hands on the bull's head on behalf of the assembly — they are the community's representatives in the semicha act. The representative structure of Israel's government (elders representing the people) is formally incorporated into the atonement mechanism: the elders' hands = the community's hands. Corporate guilt is addressed through representative action at every level.
4.16: The anointed priest (ha-kohen ha-mashiach) takes the blood inside the tent of meeting — the same procedure as for his own chattat (v. 5). The community's sin, like the priest's, requires the blood to reach the inner sanctuary. The spatial reach of the blood-application is determined by the gravity and representational scope of the sin: the more comprehensive the sin, the deeper into the sanctuary the blood must go.
4.17: Seven-fold sprinkling (sheva pe'amim) before the veil — identical to the procedure for the priest's own offering (v. 6). The communal sin offering uses the identical blood-manipulation technique as the priestly offering. The seven-fold sprinkling before the parochet is the maximum blood-application ritual in the regular calendar (only Yom Kippur adds entry into the Most Holy Place itself).
4.18: Qarnot ha-mizbeach (horns of the altar) — the incense altar inside the tent. Blood on the horns + remainder poured at the base of the burnt-offering altar (v. 18). The double altar ritual (incense altar inside + burnt offering altar outside) links the two spaces of the tabernacle: the purification reaches both the inner court (where YHWH dwells) and the outer court (where sacrifice is performed). Atonement works from the inside out and from the outside in simultaneously.
4.19: Ve'et kol chelvo yarim mimenu: 'All its fat he removes.' The fat-burning on the altar — consistent across all offering types. Even in the community's sin offering, where the entire carcass is burned outside the camp (no priestly food-share), the fat portions are first properly burned on the altar. The two disposals happen in order: first the fat to the altar (YHWH's portion); then the remainder to outside the camp (the removal of sin).
