4.20: Vekipper aleihem ha-kohen venislach lahem: 'The priest makes atonement for them, and they will be forgiven.' Kipper (H3722): to make atonement, to cover, to ransom. Salach (H5545): to forgive, to pardon — exclusively used of YHWH's forgiveness of humans in the OT (never of human-to-human forgiveness). The pairing is the standard outcome formula for the chattat: atonement performed by the priest + forgiveness granted by YHWH. The two acts are distinct and sequential: ritual action enables divine response.
4.21: Chattat ha-qahal: 'the sin offering of the community.' Burned entirely outside the camp, as with the high priest's chattat (v. 12). The outside-the-camp burning marks these two categories (priestly and communal) as the 'whole-consumption' chattat: no one eats any part. The Epistle to the Hebrews (13:11-13) draws on this: 'the bodies of those animals whose blood is brought into the holy places by the high priest as a sin offering are burned outside the camp' — and then applies it to Jesus, who suffered 'outside the gate.'
4.22: Nasi (H5387): 'leader, prince, chief' — the community's civil head (as distinct from the kohen ha-mashiach, the religious head). The term nasi in Ezekiel becomes a key eschatological title for the ideal future leader (Ezek 34:24; 37:25). The nasi's sin offering uses a male goat (se'ir 'izzim, v. 23) rather than a bull — a scaled-down offering reflecting his lesser representative scope compared to the priest or the community as a whole.
4.23: Se'ir 'izzim (H8163+H5795): 'male goat.' The leader's chattat animal is a male goat — identical to one of the two Yom Kippur goats (Lev 16:9) and the offering for national sin on appointed festivals (Num 28-29). The male goat becomes the standard chattat animal for leaders and communities on regular observances. The gradation of sin-offering animals (bull for priest/community; male goat for leader; female goat or lamb for commoner) reflects the graduated-responsibility principle: higher authority = larger animal = more severe accounting.
4.24: The leader lays hands on the goat and slaughters it bimqom asher yishchat et ha-'olah lifne YHWH — in the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered. The chattat animal for the leader is killed in the same location as the 'olah (north side of the altar, cf. 1:11; 6:25), not in a separate ritual space. The shared slaughter-location across offering types means the ritual geography of the tabernacle courtyard is a fixed, non-negotiable spatial order.
4.25: Blood on the qarnot mizbeach ha-'olah (horns of the burnt offering altar) — not the incense altar as in the priestly and communal chattat. The leader's sin offering applies blood to the outer burnt-offering altar, not the inner incense altar. The difference marks the lesser gravity: the leader's sin does not require blood to reach the inner sanctuary. The spatial mapping of sin-severity is precise: the deeper the sanctuary penetration, the graver the sin being addressed.
4.26: Kipper + nislach lo (atonement made + forgiveness granted) — the standard outcome formula for the chattat. The nislach lo ('forgiven to him') is the divine grant of pardon that follows the human ritual of atonement. Throughout Leviticus, forgiveness is never automatic or mechanical: it requires the appropriate ritual action (kipper) but its ultimate granting is YHWH's sovereign act of selach. The two moments (human ritual + divine pardon) are inseparable but distinguishable.
4.27: Am ha-'aretz (am, H5971 + 'aretz, H776): 'a person of the land' — an ordinary member of the Israelite community, as distinct from the priest, the community leadership, or the nasi. The term later takes on various social/religious connotations in the Second Temple period, but in Leviticus it simply means any ordinary individual Israelite who is not in a leadership role. The offering for the individual commoner is a female animal (goat or lamb) rather than a male, reflecting the smaller scale of individual vs. representative sin.
4.28: Se'irat 'izzim (H5795+H8163): female goat — the individual's chattat animal for an intentional-class violation. The female animal for the individual's sin offering reflects the gradation principle throughout ch. 4: bull (anointed priest / community) → male goat (leader) → female goat or female lamb (individual). The systematic gradation is not about the animal's monetary value per se but about the representative scope: those who represent more offer more. The individual offers a female animal because they represent only themselves.
4.29: Semicha, slaughter, and blood at the burnt-offering altar — the same spatial and gestural sequence for the individual's chattat as for the leader's (v. 24-25). The ritual actions are identical across all categories; what differs is the blood-application location (inner vs. outer altar) and the animal used. The structural consistency of the ritual ensures that every level of Israelite society participates in the same system of covenant restoration.
4.30: Blood on the horns of the burnt-offering altar (mizbeach ha-'olah) with the remainder poured at the base — the same as the leader's offering (v. 25). The individual Israelite's blood ritual matches the leader's in location, confirming that both outer-altar offerings share the same spatial category (as distinguished from the inner-altar offerings of the priest and community). The taxonomy is precise and consistent.
4.31: Reah nihoah la-YHWH — the pleasing-aroma formula appears for the first time in the chattat chapter at the individual's offering. The earlier chattat variants (priest, community, leader) do not use this formula; the individual's offering does. This may reflect that the fat-burning of the individual's chattat is treated more like the shelamim fat-burning (which feeds the priestly family rather than being entirely consumed). The kipper + nislach lo outcome formula closes the individual female-goat offering.
4.32: Kivsa (H3775 female): 'a ewe-lamb' — the alternative animal for the individual's chattat. The option of either a female goat or a ewe-lamb provides economic flexibility within the individual-offering category. Both animals are female and must be temimah (without defect). The two-animal option (goat or lamb) for the commoner's sin offering means that the individual's access to the atonement system is the most flexible in the entire ch. 4 gradation.
4.33: The ewe-lamb's chattat ritual is performed linetach ha-'olah — beside the burnt-offering location. The spatial marker repeated from v. 29 and v. 24 confirms the fixed geography: the leader's and individual's sin offerings are both performed at or near the burnt-offering location (north side of the outer altar), not in front of the veil. The spatial distinction separates the priestly/communal category from the leader/individual category throughout ch. 4.
4.34: Blood applied to the qarnot mizbeach ha-'olah — identical to the female-goat procedure (v. 30). The lamb and goat procedures for the individual chattat are structurally identical: same animal-sex category, same spatial location, same blood application point. The two animal options differ only in species; the ritual procedure is the same. The systematic parallelism throughout ch. 4 creates a graduated but unified system: one ritual logic, five implementation levels (priest, community, leader, individual-goat, individual-lamb).
4.35: Kipper alav ha-kohen al chata'to asher chata venislach lo: 'The priest makes atonement for the sin he committed, and he will be forgiven.' The chapter closes with the same outcome formula that appears at each stage (vv. 20, 26, 31) — kipper + nislach. The repetition of this paired formula across all four social categories (priest, community, leader, individual) is programmatic: the entire chattat system exists to make this outcome available to everyone in Israel, from the highest priest to the lowest commoner, through properly executed ritual atonement and YHWH's sovereign grant of pardon.

8.1: YHWH commands Moses to take Aaron and his sons with the vestments, anointing oil, and the animals for ordination. The divine initiative is the key theological point: ordination comes by divine summons, not human ambition. Heb 5:4: “one does not take this honor for himself, but only when called by God, just as Aaron was” — the legitimacy of priesthood rests on being called, not on self-promotion.
8.2: The materials of ordination include the priestly garments, the shemen hamishchah (šemen hammiššāḥ, anointing oil), the bull for the sin offering, and the two rams. The unleavened bread (maṣṣôt) connects the priestly ordination to Passover purity; the new priesthood begins in the context of redemption and sinlessness (1 Cor 5:8 “sincerity and truth”).
8.3: Moses assembles all the congregation at the entrance of the tent of meeting — ordination is a public, corporate act witnessed by all Israel. The public nature of priestly consecration is essential: those who serve the community before YHWH are accountable to the community (1 Tim 3:2; Titus 1:6 “above reproach”).
8.4: “Moses did as YHWH commanded him” — the chapter's repeated refrain; Moses's precise obedience validates every subsequent step. No element is improvised; the entire ceremony is a replication of divine prescription. The pattern governs; creativity is not invited in the holy place.
8.5: “This is the thing that YHWH has commanded to be done” — Moses announces the divine authority behind the ritual before beginning. The declaratory formula grounds the ceremony in divine command rather than human tradition; the ordination's legitimacy is revelatory, not customary.
8.6: Moses washed Aaron and his sons with water — rachats (rāḥaṣ), washing/bathing; the water-washing is the first act of consecration, prerequisite to all that follows. Three elements mark priestly consecration: water (washing), oil (anointing), blood (sacrifice). 1 John 5:6-8 echoes a threefold witness of Spirit, water, and blood in the testimony to Christ; Heb 10:22 calls believers to draw near with “hearts sprinkled clean... and bodies washed with pure water.”
8.7: Moses clothed Aaron in the tunic (kuttonet), sash (ʾavnet), robe (mêʿil), ephod, and breastpiece, with the ephod's band tied around him. The vesting sequence invests Aaron with the symbolic clothing of office; Paul's clothing metaphor applies the same logic: “put on the Lord Jesus Christ” (Rom 13:14); believers are invested with Christ's righteousness as their priestly vestment (Gal 3:27).
8.8: Moses placed the breastpiece (ḥōšen) on Aaron with the Urim and Thummim (hāʾûrîm wᵉhattummîm) — the priestly oracular objects; the exact mechanism of their use is unknown, but they mediated divine decisions for the community (1 Sam 28:6). Christ as the ultimate oracular priest in whom “are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge” (Col 2:3) fulfills the oracular function of the Urim and Thummim permanently.
8.9: Moses set the turban (miṣnepet) on Aaron's head with the tzitz hazahav (ṣîṣ hazzāhāv), the golden plate inscribed qōdeš laYHWH (“Holy to YHWH”). The high priest bore YHWH's name on his forehead before the divine presence, presenting it on behalf of all Israel. Rev 22:4: servants of God will see his face and “his name will be on their foreheads” — the eschatological fulfillment of the high priest's golden crown applied to all new covenant priests.
8.10: Moses took the shemen hamishchah (šemen hammiššāḥ) and anointed the tabernacle and all its furnishings, consecrating them — the anointing of physical objects makes them sacred through the same oil that consecrates persons. The oil is the Spirit's material type; the Spirit's consecrating work makes what it touches holy. The tabernacle's anointing is the precursor of the Spirit's indwelling of the church-as-temple (1 Cor 3:16).
8.11: Moses sprinkled some of the anointing oil on the altar seven times (ševaʿ pᵉʿāmîm) — seven-fold sprinkling = complete and perfect consecration; the number seven throughout Leviticus signals fullness and completion. Christ's once-for-all sacrifice is the complete and final consecration of the true altar (Heb 10:14 “by a single offering he has perfected for all time”).
8.12: Moses poured the anointing oil on Aaron's head — yitzok shemen hamishchah al rosh Aharon (wayyiṣōq miššemen hammiššāḥ ʿal rōʾš ʾahărōn): “he poured the anointing oil on Aaron's head.” This act is the origin of the title māšîaḥ (Anointed One / Messiah); the anointing of Aaron's head made him high priest in perpetuity. Ps 45:7 / Heb 1:9 applies the divine anointing “with the oil of gladness” to Christ; Acts 10:38 identifies Jesus as the one God “anointed with the Holy Spirit and with power.”
8.13: Moses clothed Aaron's sons in simpler vestments — tunics (kuttōnōt), sashes, and caps (migbāʿôt) — as YHWH commanded. The sons of Aaron are dressed differently from the high priest: a hierarchy of holiness and responsibility within the priesthood. In Christ, all believers are priests (1 Pet 2:9) with equal access, but Christ alone wears the full high-priestly vestments of perfect intercession (Heb 7:26-28).
8.14: Aaron and his sons laid their hands on the head of the bull of the sin offering (par haḥaṭṭāʾt) — even the high priest requires a sin offering for his own sins before the ordination proceeds. Ḥaṭṭāʾt (sin offering) is derived from the root ḥēt (sin) — the offering is literally the “sin-thing”; the animal carries the identity of what it removes. Heb 7:27: “He has no need, like those high priests, to offer sacrifices daily, first for his own sins and then for those of the people.”
8.15: Moses put the blood on the horns of the altar (qarnôt hammizbêaḥ) with his finger and poured the rest at the base — blood on the four horns purifies the altar itself; the instrument of sacrifice must be consecrated before it can accept offerings. The base-pour returns the excess blood to the ground; blood is YHWH's (Lev 17:11), and the earth receives what is not burned.
8.16: Moses burned the chelev (ḥēlev, fat) on the altar — the fat portions belong to YHWH in every offering (Lev 3:16 “all fat is YHWH's”). The fat represents the richest, best portion of the animal; offering the fat to YHWH is offering the firstfruits of life's abundance. The NT parallel: “offer your bodies as a living sacrifice” (Rom 12:1) — the best of oneself as the ongoing offering.
8.17: The bull's flesh, skin, and dung were burned outside the camp — the sin offering carcass is entirely destroyed outside the sacred precincts; nothing remains that might contaminate. Heb 13:11-13: “the bodies of those animals whose blood is brought into the holy places by the high priest as a sacrifice for sin are burned outside the camp. So Jesus also suffered outside the gate.” The burning outside the camp is fulfilled in the crucifixion outside Jerusalem.
8.18: The ram of the burnt offering (ʾêl hāʿōlāh) — ʿōlāh from ʿālāh (to go up); the entire burnt offering ascends to YHWH in smoke; complete consecration of everything to God, holding nothing back. The burnt offering has no portion for the offerer; it is entirely devoted. Christ's total self-giving: “who gave himself for us” (Titus 2:14), without reservation.
8.19: Moses threw (zaraq, zāraq) the blood against the sides of the altar all around — the vigorous dashing of blood stands in contrast to the careful finger-application of the sin offering (v15); different application methods signal different functions: the sin offering's finger-dab is precise and priestly; the burnt offering's blood-dash is complete and comprehensive.
8.20: Moses cut the ram into pieces and burned the head, the pieces, and the fat on the altar — the systematic total-offering: every part of the burnt-offering ram is consumed; nothing remains. The completeness of the offering models total consecration. The NT parallel is baptism as dying entirely to self and rising entirely to Christ (Rom 6:3-4).
8.21: Moses washed the entrails and legs with water and burned the whole ram on the altar — a reyach nichoch laYHWH (rêaḥ nîḥōaḥ laYHWH), “a pleasing aroma to YHWH.” The same phrase describes Christ's sacrifice in Eph 5:2: “Christ loved us and gave himself up for us, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God.” The pleasing aroma language signals divine acceptance; the offering is received, not merely performed.
8.22: The second ram is the milu'im (millûʾîm, ordination/fillings) ram — a term unique to this ritual; millûʾîm literally means “fillings” or “investments”; it refers both to the filling of the priests' hands with offerings (v27) and to the complete investiture of the office. The ordination ceremony is a “filling” — the new priests are both invested and equipped, their hands literally filled with what they need for service.
8.23: Moses put blood on the lobe of Aaron's right ear (tenûk ōzen Ahărōn hᵉyāmānît), the thumb of his right hand (bōhen yādô hᵉyāmānît), and the big toe of his right foot (bōhen raglô hᵉyāmānît) — blood on the ear (to hear YHWH's commands), the hand (to do YHWH's work), and the foot (to walk in YHWH's ways); the whole person dedicated to YHWH's service through blood-consecration at every extremity. The pattern mirrors the cleansing of the recovered leper (Lev 14:14) — priest and healed-leper are consecrated by the same blood-to-extremities pattern.
8.24: Moses put blood on the right ear-lobe, thumb, and big toe of Aaron's sons as well — the entire priestly cohort receives the threefold blood-anointing; consecration is not only for the high priest but for all who serve in the sanctuary. 1 Pet 1:2: those who are “sprinkled with his blood” form the entire new covenant priesthood (1 Pet 2:9), all consecrated at their extremities through Christ's atoning blood.
8.25: Moses took the fat, the fat tail, the fat on the entrails, the liver lobe, the two kidneys with fat, and the right thigh — the choicest internal portions and the right thigh set aside as YHWH's premier portion. The systematic identification of the best portions for YHWH across all animal offerings establishes the theological principle: the first and best belongs to God (Prov 3:9 “honor YHWH with your firstfruits”).
8.26: Moses added to the fat one unleavened loaf, one loaf with oil, and one wafer from the basket of unleavened ordination bread — the grain offering (minḥāh) accompanies the animal offerings; bread + animal = the full human food-offering; both the labor of the field and the labor of the flock are presented. Christ as both the bread of life (John 6:35) and the Lamb of God (John 1:29) fulfills both dimensions.
8.27: Moses placed all these in the hands of Aaron and his sons and waved them as a tenufah (tᵉnûpāh, wave offering) before YHWH — the waving gesture presents the offering before YHWH and signifies his reception and return of what belongs to him. The priests' hands are literally “filled” (millûʾîm) with the offerings — hence ordination is called the filling of the hands; the priest is a mediating conduit between YHWH and the offering.
8.28: Moses then took the wave offerings from the priests' hands and burned them on the altar on top of the burnt offering — the ordination sacrifices are placed on top of the already-burning burnt offering; the ordination is built on the foundation of total consecration (burnt offering = total devotion). Ministry rests on prior whole-life dedication.
8.29: Moses took the breast (ḥāzeh) and waved it as a wave offering before YHWH — Moses's portion of the ordination ram, taken as the officiating priest's share. In this unique ceremony, Moses acts as the ordaining priest before the Aaronic priesthood exists; he performs a priestly function that only he can perform. The chapter marks a transition: the one mediator (Moses) installing the ongoing mediation of the Aaronic priesthood.
8.30: Moses sprinkled anointing oil and blood on Aaron, his sons, and their garments — oil + blood together; the dual element of consecration: oil (šemen, anointing/Spirit) and blood (dām, atonement) constitute complete priestly consecration. Heb 9:21 notes that Moses “sprinkled with the blood both the tent and all the vessels used in worship.” The new covenant antitype: Spirit and blood both applied to the believer-priest (1 John 5:6-8).
8.31: Aaron and his sons are commanded to boil the ordination flesh at the entrance of the tent and eat it there with the ordination bread — the covenant meal at the end of the ordination; eating in the presence of YHWH seals the covenant relationship begun in the ceremony. The priestly ordination meal is a type of the Lord's Supper as the covenant meal of the new covenant priesthood (1 Cor 10:16-17).
8.32: What remains of the flesh and bread is to be burned with fire — nothing of the ordination sacrifice may be taken away and profaned outside the sacred precinct; the completeness-of-devotion principle: what is given to YHWH is given entirely. The prohibition against leftovers prevents the holy from mingling with the common.
8.33: Aaron and his sons shall remain at the entrance of the tent for seven days (šibeʿat yāmîm), for it takes seven days to complete the ordination (milu'im) — the seven-day period corresponds to the seven days of creation; the ordination ritual creates a new order of priestly ministry. Only after the full seven-day formation period may the priests begin public ministry; preparation precedes service throughout Scripture (cf. Jesus's forty days of testing before his public ministry, Luke 4:1-14).
8.34: “As has been done today, YHWH commanded to be done lekapper aleichem” (lᵉkappēr ʿᵃlêkem): “to make atonement for you.” The entire seven-day ordination process is fundamentally an atonement-making procedure for the priests themselves; priests require atonement before they can make atonement for others. The Aaronic priesthood is structurally dependent on sacrificial atonement. Christ, by contrast, “offered himself unblemished to God” (Heb 9:14) — his ordination required no atonement because he was the sinless ordainer and the sinless offering simultaneously.
8.35: Aaron and his sons shall remain at the entrance day and night for seven days, performing YHWH's charge “so that you do not die” — the threat of death for deviation from the ordained process; the holiness of the divine prescription carries mortal weight. The death-threat is the measure of the seriousness of the encounter with the holy God. Heb 12:28-29 applies the same logic: acceptable worship requires reverence and awe because our God is a consuming fire.
8.36: “And Aaron and his sons did all the things that YHWH commanded by Moses” — the chapter closes with comprehensive obedience; “all the things” emphasizes completeness, leaving no element unperformed. The obedient conclusion validates the entire ceremony. Christ: “I have accomplished the work that you gave me to do” (John 17:4) — the same complete obedience that validated the new covenant ordination.

9.1: On the yom hashemini (yôm haššᵉmînî), the eighth day — the number eight in biblical numerology signals a new beginning beyond the seven of completion; circumcision on the eighth day (Lev 12:3), resurrection on the first/eighth day of the week. The eighth day of priestly ordination is the dawn of the new era of active ministry after the seven-day formation; the new creation begins when consecration is complete.
9.2: Aaron is instructed to bring a ben baqar (ben bāqār, a young bull/calf) for his sin offering — the same category of animal as his ordination sin offering (8:14); the high priest's inaugural service begins where his ordination began: with his own sin offering. The permanent structural need of the Aaronic high priest for personal atonement before every service is Hebrews' central argument for Christ's superiority (Heb 7:27-28).
9.3: The people bring a male goat (śᵉʿîr ʿizzîm) for a sin offering and a calf and a lamb for burnt offerings — the full suite of atonement and devotion offerings for the community on the inaugural day of priestly service. The variety of animals and offerings reflects the comprehensive scope of the relationship being inaugurated: sin covered, total devotion expressed.
9.4: The shelem (šᵉlāmîm, peace/fellowship offering; from šālôm) is the culminating offering of the inaugural day — the peace offering is the goal of the entire sacrificial system: atonement secured (sin offering) + devotion expressed (burnt offering) = fellowship enjoyed (peace offering). “YHWH will appear to you” (niraʾ ʾalêkem YHWH) is the stated purpose; all the rituals exist for the sake of the divine appearance.
9.5: All the congregation drew near and stood before YHWH — qarov (qārᵃbû, they drew near); the technical verb of priestly approach is here applied to the entire congregation; the priestly ritual brings the whole community into YHWH's presence, not just the priest. The corporate character of Israel's worship: the individual priest mediates for the whole, who are themselves drawn near.
9.6: “That the kevod YHWH (kᵉbôd YHWH) may appear to you” — the glory of YHWH is the stated goal of the entire day's ritual. The kāvôd (weighty presence, glory) is what the tabernacle, priesthood, and sacrifice all exist to mediate. John 1:14: “we have seen his glory, glory as of the only Son from the Father” — the Incarnation is the ultimate appearance of the divine glory that Lev 9 promises.
9.7: “Qarav el hamizbeach” (qᵉrav ʾel-hammizbêaḥ): “draw near to the altar” — Aaron's first official act is the technical priestly motion of approach. Qarav (to draw near/approach) is the defining verb of priestly worship throughout Leviticus; the priest is the one who is permitted to draw near where others would die. Heb 4:16: “let us therefore draw near (proserchōmetha) with confidence to the throne of grace” — the new covenant opens to all what the altar of Lev 9 permitted only to Aaron.
9.8: Aaron drew near to the altar and killed the calf of his own sin offering — his first official act as functioning high priest is self-atonement; the inaugural ministry begins with personal sin offering before any act on the people's behalf. The logic of Heb 7:27 is inescapable: the Aaronic high priest must always begin with himself; Christ began with himself as the offering, not as one needing the offering.
9.9: Aaron dipped his finger in the blood and put it on the horns of the altar (qarnôt hammizbêaḥ) and poured the rest at the base — the finger-dab application of sin-offering blood to the four altar horns is the distinctive application pattern of the sin offering; the horns (power-points) of the altar are the location where atonement blood is applied, and the base-pour returns excess blood to the ground.
9.10: The fat portions (ḥēlev), kidneys, and liver lobe were burned on the altar as YHWH commanded — the prescribed portions for YHWH from every offering; burning these choice pieces on the altar is the act of giving the best to God. Ps 63:5: “my soul will be satisfied as with fat and rich food” — the ḥēlev is the OT's symbol of the richest blessing; giving it to YHWH is offering the finest.
9.11: The flesh and skin of the sin offering bull were burned outside the camp — the sin offering whose blood was not brought into the inner sanctuary is disposed of outside the camp (cf. 6:30 where offerings whose blood enters the sanctuary are burned inside; 10:17-18 revisits this distinction). Heb 13:11-13 interprets this type: “Jesus also suffered outside the gate.”
9.12: Aaron killed the burnt offering (ʿōlāh) and his sons dashed the blood against the sides of the altar — the transition from sin offering (atonement) to burnt offering (devotion) follows the prescribed sequence: first make atonement, then present total devotion. The order is theologically determinative: the cross (atonement) precedes the consecrated life (devotion).
9.13: Aaron's sons handed him the burnt offering piece by piece and the head — the systematic dismemberment and burning of every part of the burnt offering; piece by piece, nothing held back; thorough and complete. The total devotion of the burnt offering is the OT's image of what Rom 12:1 commands: “present your bodies as a living sacrifice.”
9.14: Aaron washed the entrails and legs with water before burning them on the altar — the inward parts must be clean before being offered; the offering is both outwardly and inwardly pure. Christ, the true burnt offering, was pure within and without: “holy, innocent, unstained” (Heb 7:26); his inner life matched his outer righteousness.
9.15: Aaron offered the people's sin offering goat and made atonement for the people — the priestly service for the community follows the priestly service for the self. Aaron first atoned for himself (vv 8-11), then turns to the people. Heb 5:3: “because of this he is obligated to offer sacrifice for his own sins just as he does for those of the people” — the Aaronic sequence that Christ's priesthood transcends.
9.16: Aaron presented the burnt offering kashmishpat (kammišpāṭ): “according to the rule/ordinance” — the offering is validated by its conformity to the prescribed procedure; the rule is not an obstacle but the form of acceptable worship. Jesus: “in accordance with the Scriptures” (1 Cor 15:3-4) — his death and resurrection are likewise validated by conformity to the divine prescription.
9.17: Aaron presented the grain offering (minḥāh), took a handful, and burned it on the altar alongside the morning burnt offering — the grain offering expresses devotion through the work of human hands (harvested, ground, baked, offered); it complements the animal offering by adding the human-labor dimension. The full offering combines animal blood and human agricultural labor — the whole created order in service to God.
9.18: Aaron slaughtered the ox and ram as shelamim (šᵉlāmîm, peace/fellowship offerings) for the people — the peace offering is the goal and culmination of the entire sequence; its name from šālôm (wholeness, peace, well-being) names what it effects: restored relationship, fellowship, and wholeness between Israel and YHWH. The eschatological banquet (Rev 19:9) is the ultimate peace offering — the meal of permanent fellowship.
9.19: Aaron's sons handed him the blood of the peace offering, and he dashed it against the sides of the altar all around — the vigorous blood-dash of the peace offering completes the blood-ritual of the day; blood marks every altar-surface as the meeting point of divine holiness and human sinfulness resolved by sacrifice.
9.20: The fat pieces were placed on the breasts and burned on the altar — YHWH's portion (the fat) and the priestly portion (the breast) are both presented before YHWH before being distributed; the waving and burning that follows (v21) enacts the exchange between YHWH and his priests.
9.21: Aaron waved the chazeh hatenufa (ḥāzeh hattᵉnûpāh, wave breast) and the shoq hayamin (šôq hayāmîn, right thigh) as a wave offering — the breast and right thigh are the priests' statutory portions from every peace offering; the wave offering presents them to YHWH and receives them back as his gift to the priests. The workers are sustained by the work they serve; 1 Tim 5:18: “the laborer deserves his wages.”
9.22: Aaron lifted his hands toward the people and blessed them (wayyiśśāʾ ʾahărōn ʾet yādāyw ʾel hāʿām waybārᵃkēm) — the high-priestly benediction is the culmination of the entire day's service; the Aaronic blessing (Num 6:24-26) is the priest's primary gift to the people. Luke 24:50-51: Jesus “lifting up his hands he blessed them. While he blessed them, he parted from them” — the ascending Christ performs the high-priestly benediction over his disciples, the new covenant fulfillment of Aaron's lifted hands.
9.23: “The kevod YHWH (kᵉbôd YHWH) appeared to all the people” — the stated goal of v6 is achieved; YHWH manifests his presence in visible glory to the entire congregation. The divine glory appearing to “all the people” democratizes the theophany; ordinarily the divine presence is restricted (only the high priest enters the most holy place); here it is publicly manifested. John 1:14: “we have seen his glory” — the Incarnation is the ultimate democratic theophany, the divine glory made publicly visible.
9.24: Vatetzei esh milifnei YHWH (wattēṣēʾ ʾēš milᵉpānê YHWH): “fire came out from before YHWH” and consumed the burnt offering — the theophanic fire is YHWH's sign of acceptance, the same fire that later consumes Nadab and Abihu for unauthorized worship (10:2); the same divine fire blesses complete obedience and destroys presumptuous deviation. The people's response: they shouted (yāronnû) and fell on their faces (wayyippeḷû ʿal pᵉnêhem) — the mixed response of joy and awe before the holy God. Heb 12:28-29: “our God is a consuming fire.”

10.1: Nadab and Abihu offered esh zarah (ʾēš zārāh): “unauthorized/strange/foreign fire” — zārāh means strange, foreign, not belonging; the fire they offered was not the fire from YHWH's altar (16:12 specifies that the high priest must take coals from the altar before YHWH). Their error may have been the fire's origin, the incense formula, the timing, or acting outside their priestly brief — the text does not specify; what matters is the phrase “which he had not commanded them” (ʾăšer lōʾ ṣiwwāh ʾōtām). Unauthorized worship, however sincere or beautiful, is not acceptable to the holy God.
10.2: Fire from before YHWH consumed Nadab and Abihu — the same ʾēš (fire) that accepted the inaugural offering in 9:24 destroys the priests who violated the holy; the divine consuming fire is not primarily a tool of judgment but an expression of YHWH's holiness that both blesses obedience and destroys transgression. Heb 12:29: “our God is a consuming fire” — the NT draws on this episode to ground the call to reverent, acceptable worship.
10.3: “Biqrovai ekadesh” (biqrōbay ʾeqqādēš): “among those who are near me I will be sanctified” — YHWH declares the theological principle Nadab and Abihu violated: proximity to YHWH increases, not decreases, the demand for holiness. Those nearest to the fire feel its full heat. “Before all the people I will be glorified” (wᵉʿal pᵉnê kol hāʿām ʾekkābēd): YHWH's glory is upheld through the deaths of Nadab and Abihu; the people learn the cost of irreverence. 1 Pet 3:15: “sanctify (hagiasate) Christ as Lord in your hearts” — the same qādaš verb applied to the new covenant community's obligation.
10.4: Moses summoned Mishael and Elzaphan (Aaron's cousins) to carry the bodies outside the camp — the dead priests were removed in their priestly coats; non-ordained relatives handle what the surviving priests (Aaron and sons) may not touch during service. The separation of grief and ministry is painfully enforced; YHWH's service does not pause for human sorrow.
10.5: Mishael and Elzaphan carried the bodies outside the camp in their coats (bᵉkuttōnōtām) — the priestly vestments become shrouds; the garments that signified consecration and life now wrap death. The bodies are taken to the outside-the-camp location — the place of what is condemned, discarded, and burned. Heb 13:13: “let us go to him outside the camp and bear the reproach he endured.”
10.6: Moses forbids Aaron and his surviving sons to uncover their heads (rōʾšêkem ʾal tipraʿû) or tear their clothes (ubigdêkem lōʾ tiphrōmû), lest they die — the usual mourning rites (loose hair, torn clothes) are prohibited for the priests during their active service; their anointing has created an obligation that overrides natural family grief. The shemen hamishchah (šemen hammiššāḥ) upon them binds them to a higher call than private emotion.
10.7: “The shemen mishchat YHWH (šemen miššaḥat YHWH) is upon you” — the anointing creates a new identity overriding all prior obligations; the consecrated person belongs to YHWH's service above all else. Rom 12:1: “present your bodies as a living sacrifice” — the new covenant anointing (the Spirit) consecrates the believer to a service that claims all of life.
10.8: YHWH spoke directly to Aaron — notably, this is one of the rare moments in Leviticus when YHWH addresses Aaron directly rather than through Moses; the command that follows (vv 9-11) is given to the high priest as the direct recipient. After the trauma of his sons' deaths, YHWH speaks to Aaron personally, entrusting him with the principle that explains what happened.
10.9: “Yayin veshekhar al tishteh” (yayyin wᵉšēkār ʾal tište): “wine and strong drink you shall not drink” — the priest must approach YHWH with faculties unimpaired; šēkār is fermented drink stronger than wine; the prohibition applies specifically when entering the tent of meeting for ministry. Eph 5:18: “do not get drunk with wine... but be filled with the Spirit” — the NT's counterpart: the Spirit-filled, not wine-altered, is the proper state for worship; the prohibition of wine is a positive prescription for Spirit-dependence.
10.10: “To havdil” (lᵉhavdîl): “to distinguish/separate” — between haqodesh (the holy) and hachol (the common), and between hatame (the unclean) and hatahor (the clean). The root bādal (to separate) is the verb of creation itself (Gen 1:4, 6, 7, 14, 18 — God separates light from dark, waters from waters); the priest perpetuates the creation-work of differentiation, maintaining ordered distinctions within the sanctuary. Heb 5:14: “mature believers trained by constant practice to distinguish (diakrínein) good from evil” — the priestly discernment function extends to the new covenant community.
10.11: The priests are to teach Israel all the statutes (ulᵉhôrôt ʾet bᵉnê yiśrāʾēl ʾēt kol haḥuqqîm) — the priests' teaching function is inseparable from their ritual function; those who maintain the distinctions (v10) also explain and transmit them. Jer 31:34 promises that in the new covenant “they shall all know me, from the least of them to the greatest” — the new covenant distributes the priestly teaching function to all; Christ is the one Teacher (Matt 23:10) through whom all the tôrāh is rightly understood.
10.12: Moses addressed “the remaining ones” (hanotar'im, hānôtārîm) — Eleazar and Ithamar; the Levitical line narrows from four sons to two in a single day; the text marks the reduction without softening it. The priestly succession continues despite the loss; YHWH's program of priestly mediation is not derailed by human failure.
10.13: “Eat it in a holy place (māqôm qādôš), for it is your choq” (ḥōq, statutory due) — the priests' food from YHWH's fire offerings is their permanent statutory portion (ḥōq from the root of “inscribed/decreed”); it is written into the law's permanent structure. The eating is a priestly act of service, not merely sustenance; receiving the holy food is itself a sacred act.
10.14: The wave breast (ḥāzeh hattᵉnûpāh) and the heave thigh may be eaten in a makom tahor (māqôm ṭāhôr), a clean (but not necessarily holy) place — the degree of sanctity of the food determines where it may be eaten; the most holy portions require the holy place; the less restricted portions require only a clean place. The entire priestly food system involves graduated holiness, matching the tabernacle's spatial gradations.
10.15: The right thigh and wave breast are given as the priests' permanent portion to wave before YHWH — the tᵉrûmāh (contribution/heave offering; from rûm, to lift/elevate) and the tᵉnûpāh (wave offering) together constitute the priests' statutory share. The offering is first presented to YHWH and received back — the pattern of divine gift through human mediation that characterizes the entire sacrificial system.
10.16: Moses learned the sin-offering goat had been burned instead of eaten — he “diligently inquired” (dārōš dāraš, intensive form: thoroughly sought); Moses's careful oversight of every sacrificial detail reflects the seriousness with which YHWH's prescriptions are maintained. The doubled verb (dārōš dāraš) signals intensive action: Moses didn't casually notice but actively investigated.
10.17: “Why have you not eaten the sin offering in the place of the sanctuary?” — Moses's rebuke establishes the principle: eating the sin offering (ʾăkal ʾet haḥaṭṭāʾt) is part of the atonement act itself; the priests bear the congregation's iniquity (yiśśᵉʾû ʾet ʿăwōn hāʿēdāh) by eating it. The priestly eating of the sin offering is a vicarious act — the priest absorbs the sin symbolically. Christ as the ultimate sin-bearer: “he himself bore our sins in his body on the tree” (1 Pet 2:24).
10.18: Since the goat's blood was not brought inside the sanctuary, it should have been eaten by the priests in the sanctuary — the distinction that determines eating vs. burning: blood brought inside (most holy place) = carcass burned outside; blood not brought inside = carcass eaten by priests inside. The blood's trajectory determines the flesh's disposition; atonement ritual logic is precise and internally consistent.
10.19: Aaron's explanation: “Such things as these have happened to me” — on the day his two sons were killed, eating the sin offering in a state of grief would have been improper; Aaron invokes the theological principle that the worshiper's heart-state affects the acceptability of the ritual act. The law here accommodates the weight of human grief rather than crushing it; the pastoral wisdom beneath the legal prescription.
10.20: “Moses heard, and vayitav be'einav” (wayyîṭab bᵉʿênāyw): “it was good in his eyes” — Moses accepted Aaron's explanation; the legal framework shows flexibility in extraordinary human circumstances. The law is not a mechanical system but a relational covenant that accommodates human reality. Heb 4:15: Christ as our high priest “is able to sympathize with our weaknesses” — the sympathetic high priest exercises pastoral wisdom rather than rigid legal application, fulfilling what Moses modeled here.

5.1: nefesh ki techeta (a soul/person who sins): the subject of Lev 5 is the individual in specific sinful situations. The four cases in vv. 1-4 all begin with nefesh (soul/person) as the moral agent. veshama qol alah (heard the voice of an oath/curse): the witness who heard the public adjuration to testify but remained silent. venasa avono (he bears his iniquity/guilt): the expression nasa avon (to bear iniquity) is the standard formula for unabsolved moral burden — guilt that remains on the person until resolved. Its opposite is nasa avon in the sense of ‘forgive’ (God bearing/taking away the iniquity, e.g., Exod 34:7). The guilt of the silent witness: failure to speak up in a legal process is a sin requiring atonement.
5.2: asher yiga bechol davar tamei (who touches any unclean thing): the touchability of impurity is a core Levitical axiom — uncleanness transfers by contact. venelam mimenu (and it was hidden from him): inadvertent contact is still real contact; the uncleanness accrues even without awareness. vehu tamei ve-ashem (and he is unclean and guilty, verb asham): the verb asham (to be/become guilty, to incur guilt) is distinct from the noun asham (guilt offering). Both appear in this chapter: the verb describes the state; the noun describes the remedy. The Hebrew legal system takes unconscious infraction seriously — objective guilt accumulates even when subjective awareness is absent.
5.3: tumat adam (human uncleanness): the category of impurity arising from contact with human bodily states (discharge, skin disease, corpse). venelam mimenu (was hidden from him): the second occurrence of the ‘hidden/unaware’ qualifier. The repeated emphasis on inadvertent impurity in vv. 2-4 establishes an important theological principle: the gap between what we know and what is actually true about our moral state. The guilt offering (asham) provides a remedy precisely for this gap — sin whose existence one may not even know about.
5.4: ki yishava letabei bifei (who swears rashly with his lips): the adjective bitui (rash utterance) describes an impulsive, unconsidered oath. lehara o leheitiv (to do evil or to do good): the oath may have been to do something harmful or something beneficial — what makes it a sin is its rashness and subsequent neglect. venelam mimenu (was hidden from him): the same formula for the third time, here in the context of forgotten oaths. The principle: a vow taken and not kept, even if forgotten, creates a real moral obligation that requires resolution. The NT counterpart: James 5:12, ‘let your yes be yes and your no be no.’
5.5: vehitvadah asher chata aleha (he shall confess the sin he has committed): the verb hitvadah (Hitpael of yadah) is the technical term for liturgical confession. This is the first step in the atonement process — oral acknowledgment of specific sin precedes the sacrificial offering. The sequence established here (awareness → confession → sacrifice → atonement) is the fundamental structure of OT repentance. In the NT, 1 John 1:9 (‘if we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins’) preserves the same structure, with Christ's sacrifice as the permanent ground of atonement.
5.6: vehevi et ashamo laYHWH (he shall bring his guilt offering to YHWH): the Hebrew noun asham appears here as the technical offering-type for these cases. There is a complexity in Lev 5 that has puzzled translators: vv. 1-13 use both chattat (sin offering) and asham (guilt offering) apparently interchangeably (v.6 calls it both asham and chattat). The scholarly consensus is that in this section the two terms overlap; the stricter distinction (asham for violations of property rights or sacred objects, chattat for impurity and inadvertent sin) is clearer in chs 4-5:13. nikapper alav hakohen mechatatoh (the priest makes atonement for his sin): the verb kipper (Piel) is the atonement-making act. The full range of kipper covers: ‘to cover,’ ‘to wipe clean,’ ‘to ransom.’ All three nuances apply to what Christ's sacrifice accomplishes.
5.7: ve'im lo tashig yado dei seh (if his hand cannot reach [the value of] a sheep): the idiomatic lo tashig yado (his hand does not reach) is the Levitical expression for financial inability. The graduated sacrifice system in vv. 7-13 is theologically significant: YHWH adjusts the atonement mechanism to the offerer's means. A poor person's turtledoves are as effective as a wealthy person's lamb; a destitute person's flour is as effective as the doves. Atonement is not a privilege of the wealthy. The same principle governs the NT: Christ's sacrifice is equally available to all, regardless of economic status (Gal 3:28; Rev 22:17).
5.8: veqarav el hakohen (he shall bring it to the priest): the priest as mediator is essential — the offerer cannot self-administer atonement. vemalaq et rosho mikol erpo (he wrings its head from its neck without completely severing it): the specific slaughter method for birds differs from four-footed animals (which are slit at the throat). The wringing separates without severing — a detail prescribed and specific. The precision of the sacrificial instructions communicates that YHWH's atonement mechanism is exactly specified, not approximated.
5.9: vehizah middam hachatat al qir hamizbeach (he sprinkles some of the blood of the sin offering on the side of the altar): blood manipulation is the critical act in all sacrifice. The blood goes to the altar — the divine ‘address’ — not merely discarded. vehanish'ar badam yimatzei el yesod hamizbeach chattat hu (the remainder of the blood is drained at the base — it is a sin offering): blood at the base of the altar is the prescribed disposal. The blood declares the offering's nature: the animal's life poured out in the place of the offerer's life.
5.10: veet hasheni ya'aseh olah kamishpat (the second bird he offers as a burnt offering according to the prescribed procedure): the two-bird offering for the poor: one chattat (sin offering) for the atonement function, one olah (burnt offering) for the dedication/consecration function. The pairing reflects the dual dimension of restored covenant relationship: sin dealt with (chattat) + self re-presented to YHWH (olah). venislach lo (and he is forgiven): the declarative formula of forgiveness — the sacrificial outcome is stated as completed, not hoped-for. The same finality characterizes NT forgiveness through Christ (Heb 10:14).
5.11: ve'im lo tasig yado lishnei torim (if his hand cannot afford even two turtledoves): the second concession. asirit ha-ephah solet (a tenth of an ephah of fine flour): approximately 2.3 liters of flour — the minimum possible offering. lo yasim aleiha shemen (he must not put oil on it): the flour offering for the poor is stripped of oil (the characteristic of the grain offering, minchah) to signal that it is not a minchah but functions as a chattat. ve-lo yiten aleiha levonah (nor shall he add frankincense): similarly stripped of incense. The deliberate differentiation signals that the medium is fully interchangeable with blood for the purpose of atonement when poverty demands it — while maintaining the theological distinctions of the system.
5.12: veqamatz hakohen mimenu melo qumtzo (the priest takes from it a handful, his fistful): the kometz (handful, the amount a fist can grasp) is the priest's act that transforms the grain into a sacrifice. azkaratah (its memorial portion, azkarah): the portion burned on the altar — the noun derives from zakar (to remember/mention). The azkarah is the portion that ‘mentions’ the offerer before YHWH — the God-ward dimension of the sacrifice. The concept of memorial/remembrance before God is central to the Passover (Exod 12:14: zikkaron, memorial), and the Lord's Supper is explicitly a memorial (‘do this in remembrance of me,’ 1 Cor 11:24-25).
5.13: vekipper alav hakohen al chatato (the priest makes atonement for him for his sin): identical outcome formula as v.6 — the flour-atonement is fully effective. vehayeta lakohen kaminchah (it shall belong to the priest, as with the grain offering): the remainder of the flour sacrifice goes to the priests — the economy of the temple system sustains the priestly ministry from the substance of the people's atonement offerings. The principle recurs throughout Lev 6-7 in the detailed priestly portions.
5.14: A new divine speech formula: vayedaber YHWH el Moshe lemor (YHWH spoke to Moses, saying) — marking the transition to a distinct section. Lev 5:14-19 introduces the asham proper: the guilt/reparation offering for trespass against holy things (me'ilah, sacred encroachment). The previous section (vv. 1-13) used ‘asham’ loosely for various sin offerings; here the technical distinction begins: the asham is specifically for violations that require reparation in addition to atonement.
5.15: nefesh ki tim'ol ma'al (a soul who commits a trespass/act of unfaithfulness): the verb ma'al (to trespass, commit sacrilege, act treacherously) carries a connotation of covenant violation beyond mere error. It appears in contexts of violation of sacred trust: Numbers 5:6 (unfaithfulness against YHWH), Joshua 7:1 (Achan's ma'al), Ezek 18:24 (apostasy). bequdshei YHWH (against YHWH's holy things): misappropriation of consecrated property — using for secular purposes what belongs to YHWH. ve-asham (he becomes guilty): the state before offering. ayil tamim (a flawless ram): the asham requires a ram — a more substantial animal than the lamb/goat of the chattat, reflecting the reparative dimension of this offering.
5.16: ve-et asher chata min haqodesh yeshalem (he shall make restitution for what he sinned against the holy thing): the asham is unique among offerings in requiring both the sacrificial act AND financial restitution. vachamishito yosif alav (and shall add a fifth to it): a 20% surcharge on the value of the misappropriated sacred property. The combination of sacrifice + restitution + penalty communicates: atonement is not merely an internal religious act but includes concrete redress for objective harm. The NT counterpart: Zacchaeus's fourfold restitution (Luke 19:8) illustrates the same principle — genuine repentance makes restitution where possible.
5.17: ve'im nefesh ki techeta ve-ashetah achat mikol mitzvot YHWH asher lo te'asenah veloh yada (if a soul sins by doing any one of the things YHWH has commanded not to be done, though he did not know it): this is the most expansive case — sin against any divine commandment, even without awareness. ve-asham venasa avono (and he becomes guilty and bears his iniquity): the guilt is objective and real regardless of subjective knowledge. This verse establishes the principle of objective guilt: the moral order has an objective reality that conscience may not track. Romans 5:12-14 expresses the NT version: sin reigned even before the law was given explicitly, because the objective moral order existed before its codification.
5.18: vehevi ayil tamim min hatzon (he shall bring a flawless ram from the flock): the asham for unknown violation of a commandment requires the same ram as for known misappropriation of sacred things (v.15). The equal atonement for known and unknown sin reflects a consistent theology: the remedy is determined by the nature of the offence (violation of the divine order), not by the quality of the offender's awareness. al shiggatoh asher lo yada (for the unintentional error he did not know): shegagah (error, inadvertence) is the technical term for unintentional sin throughout the sacrificial system — it contrasts with be-yad ramah (with a high/defiant hand, Num 15:30), for which no sacrifice is prescribed.
5.19: asham hu asham asham laYHWH (it is a guilt offering — he has truly incurred guilt before YHWH): the emphatic declaration closes the asham section of ch5. The repetition (asham asham) is emphatic: this is genuinely an asham-case, a real guilt incurred before YHWH. The emphatic language addresses a potential objection: how can one be truly guilty for something one did not know? Answer: guilt before YHWH is defined by objective violation of the divine order, not by subjective awareness. The remedy is therefore also objective: the asham ram's blood provides real, not merely symbolic, atonement for real, not merely felt, guilt.

6.1: A new divine speech formula marking the beginning of a distinct subsection. Lev 6:1-7 (English; Hebrew 5:20-26) closes the asham laws by addressing interpersonal fraud and theft — and classifying these as covenant offences against YHWH himself. The section demonstrates that the horizontal and vertical dimensions of sin are inseparable: cheating a neighbor is simultaneously trespassing against YHWH.
6.2: nefesh ki techeta uma'ala ma'al baYHWH (a soul who sins and commits a trespass against YHWH): the key interpretive move of this section: interpersonal fraud — lying about a deposit, robbery, deception of a neighbor — is categorized as ma'al baYHWH (trespass against YHWH). The horizontal wrong (against the neighbor) is simultaneously a vertical wrong (against YHWH). piqqadon (deposit, entrustment): a common commercial arrangement in the ancient Near East, governed by its own legal codes (Hammurabi §§120-126). The Levitical law classifies the violation of such trust as sacred encroachment, not merely civil wrong.
6.3: o nefesh asher yishava al shaqer (or a person who swears falsely): the false oath is the climactic case — it invokes YHWH's name in support of a lie, compounding the interpersonal fraud with blasphemy. The categories in vv. 2-3 (deposit fraud, robbery, extortion, lost-property fraud, false oath) are unified by their common structure: using speech or relationship to take what belongs to another. This is the sin of deception in economic relationships — a violation of the ninth commandment (‘do not bear false witness’) applied to commercial life.
6.4: vehayah ki yecheta ve-ashem (when he has sinned and is guilty): the causal vehayah ki (‘and it shall be when’) marks the consequence. veheshiv et hagazel asher gazal (he shall return what he took by robbery): the verb shub (Hiphil: heshiv) = ‘restore, return, make restitution.’ The atonement process for interpersonal fraud requires first returning the stolen goods — confession and sacrifice without restitution is incomplete. This is the OT foundation for the NT principle that reconciliation with the offended party precedes valid worship: ‘if you are offering your gift at the altar and there remember that your brother has something against you, first go and be reconciled’ (Matt 5:23-24).
6.5: al kol asher yishava alav lashaqer veshallem oto verosho yosif alav chamishitav (for anything about which he swore falsely — he shall make full restitution, adding one-fifth of it): the 20% surcharge (chamishit) is the same penalty as for misappropriation of sacred property (5:16). The theological implication: a neighbor's property, protected by oath, has the same sacred status as temple property. beyom ashmatoh (on the day of his guilt offering): the restitution precedes or accompanies the sacrifice — not a deferrable obligation but an immediate one.
6.6: ve-et ashamo yavi laYHWH ayil tamim (he shall bring his guilt offering to YHWH — a flawless ram): after restitution comes sacrifice. The order is significant: economic justice first, then ritual atonement. The asham ram for interpersonal fraud is identical to the asham for violating sacred things (5:15) — confirming that the neighbor's property is as sacred as temple property in YHWH's sight.
6.7: venikhpar lav lifnei YHWH (atonement is made for him before YHWH): the atonement formula closing the asham section. vekhol asher ya'aseh le'echad mekulam asher yit'ashma bah (for anything he may have done to incur guilt): the comprehensive scope — the asham covers the full range of covenant violations enumerated in vv. 2-5. The forgiveness formula confirms: the combination of restitution + sacrifice provides complete resolution of both the horizontal wrong (the neighbor is made whole) and the vertical wrong (YHWH's covenant is repaired).
6.8: Another divine speech formula: vayedaber YHWH el Moshe lemor. The section vv. 8-13 begins the ‘priestly torah’ or ‘instructions for the offerings’ — a different literary genre from the preceding ‘case law’ format of chs 4-6:7. Lev 6:8-7:38 provides the officiants' manual for handling each sacrifice, including the priestly portions. The divine speech marking a new section also signals a change in addressee: previously addressed to the people (via Moses), now addressed to Aaron and the priests.
6.9: zot torat ha-olah (this is the Torah of the burnt offering): the torat formula introduces each major section of the priestly manual (cf. 6:14, 25; 7:1, 11). hi ha-olah al moqtah al hamizbeach kol halailah (the burnt offering remains on its hearth on the altar all night): the olah is unique among offerings in being entirely consumed on the altar — no portion for priests or offerer. Its all-night burning distinguishes it: the fire continues even when the worshiping community is absent, asserting the continuous character of Israel's consecration to YHWH. veha'eish hamizbeach tuqad bo (the fire of the altar shall be kept burning on it): the perpetual fire is the subject of the next unit.
6.10: velabash hakohen middo bad (the priest puts on his linen garment): the linen (bad) priestly garments — white, undyed, non-wool — are the standard dress for the daily service. venas et hadeshan el mitz ad lamizbeach (he takes up the ashes left from the burnt offering on the altar and places them beside the altar): the removal of ashes is a ritual act requiring the priestly vestments. Even the disposal of what remains after the sacrifice is governed by holiness protocols — nothing about YHWH's altar is treated as ordinary refuse.
6.11: ufashat et begadav velabash begadim acherim (he shall take off his garments and put on other clothes): a change of garments for the ash-removal journey. The holy vestments are worn in the sanctuary; ordinary clothes are worn for the ash disposal outside the camp. The same principle of contextual holiness — different sacred protocols for different spaces — governs the movement between the altar (holy) and the ash-dump (clean but not holy).
6.12: veha'eish al hamizbeach tuqad bo (the fire on the altar must be kept burning): repeated from v.9 to frame vv. 10-11 as an interruption. uveer alav hakohen etzim baboqer baboqer (the priest shall add wood to it every morning): the daily wood-feeding is the priest's first morning act. The fire that began at the altar's inauguration (Lev 9:24: fire from before YHWH) must never be allowed to go out — it is the sign of YHWH's ongoing acceptance and presence.
6.13: eish tamid tuqad al hamizbeach lo tichbeh (a continual fire shall be kept burning on the altar; it shall not go out): the tamid principle (continual, perpetual) is fundamental to Levitical worship. Multiple elements of the tabernacle/temple service are tamid: the fire (6:13), the lampstand (Exod 27:20), the bread of the Presence (Exod 25:30), the incense (Exod 30:8). The tamid offerings frame Israel's life in perpetual liturgy. Revelation 4:8 (‘day and night they never cease to say, Holy, holy, holy’) is the new covenant's eternal tamid — continuous worship before YHWH.
6.14: vezot torat haminchah (this is the Torah of the grain offering): the minchah section begins. bene Aharon yaqrivu otah lifnei YHWH el penei hamizbeach (Aaron's sons shall offer it before YHWH, in front of the altar): the grain offering instructions are now addressed to the officiants. The grain offering (minchah, gift/tribute) consists of fine flour, oil, and salt; it accompanies other offerings and is sometimes made as a standalone gift. It represents the offerer's produce — the agricultural domain consecrated to YHWH.
6.15: veheram mimenu bequmtzo misolet haminchah umishamnah (a priest takes a handful from the grain offering — from the flour and oil): the kometz procedure for the minchah: a fistful is taken and burned as the azkarah (memorial portion). al kol halevonah asher al haminchah (and all the frankincense that is on the grain offering): all the frankincense is burned even though only a handful of flour. The azkarah rises before YHWH as reyach nichoach (pleasing aroma) — the ascending smoke is the ‘memorial’ portion that keeps the offerer in YHWH's remembrance.
6.16: vehannoteret mimenu yochaluhu Aharon uvanav (what remains Aaron and his sons eat): the remainder of the grain offering is priestly food. matzot te'achel (it shall be eaten without leaven/yeast): unleavened bread is required in the holy precinct. The absence of leaven in the priestly food connects to Passover theology — leaven represents the old, corrupted life; unleavened bread is the food of the redeemed, the holy, the new (cf. Paul in 1 Cor 5:7-8: ‘let us celebrate the festival... with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth’).
6.17: lo te'afeh chametz (it shall not be baked with leaven): repeated and emphatic. chelqam natati otah me'ishai (I have given it as their portion from my food offerings): the priestly portion is explicitly YHWH's gift to the priests — ‘my food offerings’ means the offerings belong to YHWH first; he then grants the priests their portion. The same principle governs the NT ministry: those who serve the altar share in the altar's provisions (1 Cor 9:13-14). kodesh kodashim hi kachatat uchaasham (it is most holy, like the sin offering and the guilt offering): the grain offering's remainder is classified as kodesh kodashim (most holy) — the highest sanctity grade, which restricts consumption to the sanctuary precincts and to male priests only.
6.18: kol zachar bibne Aharon yochalenu (every male among Aaron's sons may eat it): the gender restriction on kodesh kodashim consumption reflects the priestly grading system: holiest things require maximum qualification (full-status male priests only). chuqat olam ledoroteichem (a permanent statute throughout your generations): the chuqat olam formula marks permanent, unchangeable ordinances. kol asher yiga bahem yiqdash (whoever touches them becomes holy): ‘contagious holiness’ — contact with the most holy things conveys holiness. This principle is the inverse of the impurity-transfer principle: as uncleanness transfers by contact, so does holiness. Christ's ‘contagious holiness’ is the NT expression: touching him heals and cleanses (Mark 1:41; Luke 8:46).
6.19: Another divine speech formula: the ordination offering section (vv. 19-23) follows. This is the daily grain offering of the high priest — not a one-time event but the permanent daily practice instituted at ordination.
6.20: zeh qorban Aharon uvanov (this is the offering of Aaron and his sons): the high priest's personal offering is distinct from his mediatorial offerings for the people. asirit ha-ephah solet minchah tamid (a tenth of an ephah of fine flour as a regular grain offering): the same amount as the poorest person's offering (5:11) — the high priest offers what the destitute person offers! This theological leveling communicates that the high priest himself depends on the mercy of atonement; he is not above the system he administers. chatzi baboqer vechatzi ba'arev (half in the morning and half in the evening): the twice-daily framing of the priestly day. The tamid sacrifice (Num 28:3-8) structures Israel's daily worship at morning and evening.
6.21: al machabat bashemen te'aseh (it shall be prepared on a griddle with oil): the ordination offering is a cooked grain offering. murbeket tavienah (bring it well mixed/soaked): the preparation method ensures a specific texture. The detail of the high priest's daily offering is provided with precision — nothing in the priestly service is improvised or left to preference. The exactness of YHWH's instructions models the exactness of Christ's obedience: not approximately what the Father required, but precisely (Phil 2:8: ‘obedient to death, even death on a cross’).
6.22: vehakohen hamashiach tachtav mibanov ya'aseh otah (the anointed priest who succeeds him among his sons shall offer it): the ordination offering is inherited — the successor priest continues the same daily offering pattern. The continuity of the anointed priest's daily offering models the continuity of Israel's priestly ministry. khalil le-YHWH tuktar (it shall be entirely burned to YHWH): the high priest's grain offering is entirely consumed — no portion is eaten. The same logic as the burnt offering: the officiant who intercedes for others offers himself entirely, retaining nothing for himself.
6.23: vekol minchah lakohen kalil tihyeh lo te'achel (every grain offering from a priest shall be entirely burned; it shall not be eaten): the general rule confirmed: priests' own grain offerings are entirely YHWH's. When priests eat the people's grain offerings (v.16), that is the appointed portion from YHWH; but priests cannot eat their own offerings, which belong entirely to God. The self-offering of the priest is total and unreserved.
6.24: A fourth divine speech formula in this chapter, introducing the sin offering instructions for priests (vv. 24-30). The shift to direct address to Aaron and the priests continues.
6.25: zot torat hachatat (this is the Torah of the sin offering): the priestly manual for the chattat. bimqom asher yishachet ha-olah tishachet hachatat (in the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered, the sin offering shall be slaughtered): the slaughter location of the chattat is the same as the olah — the north side of the altar (Lev 1:11). The spatial alignment communicates the equivalence of the two: both are equally legitimate, equally holy, at the same place before YHWH. qodesh qodashim hi (it is most holy): the sin offering meat is classified as kodesh kodashim — the highest holiness grade.
6.26: hakohen hamechaper otah yochalenah (the priest who offers it as a sin offering shall eat it): the priest who performs the atonement rite eats the sin offering's meat. This is the economic provision for the Levitical priesthood but also has theological significance: the priest who handles the sin offering participates in it. bimaqom qadosh te'achel bechatzar ohel mo'ed (in a holy place it shall be eaten, in the court of the tent of meeting): the location restriction confines the sin-offering meal to the sanctuary precinct. The sanctity of the meat requires a holy context for consumption.
6.27: kol asher yiga bivisarah yiqdash (whoever touches its flesh becomes holy): ‘contagious holiness’ extended to the sin offering's flesh. The sin offering — which carries the sin of the offerer through the priest's act — is itself kodesh kodashim and communicates holiness to whatever touches it. The paradox: the vessel of sin-bearing becomes the most holy object. Christ as the sin-bearer (‘him who knew no sin to be sin for us,’ 2 Cor 5:21) is the ultimate ‘most holy’ thing — and contact with him through faith sanctifies (Heb 2:11: ‘the one who sanctifies and those who are sanctified are all of one family’).
6.28: ukhli cheres asher tevushal bo yishaber (the clay pot in which it was cooked must be broken): clay is porous and absorbs the fluids of whatever is cooked in it. A clay pot that has absorbed sin-offering residue must be destroyed — the absorbed holiness cannot be fully removed from the absorbent material. keli nechoshet (a bronze pot): bronze, being non-porous and scourable, can be cleaned. The distinction between porous (clay) and non-porous (bronze) materials in relation to absorbed sanctity illustrates the concrete, physical theology of the Levitical system — holiness has real material implications.
6.29: kol zachar bakohanim yochal otah qodesh qodashim hi (every male among the priests may eat it; it is most holy): the sin offering's meat is restricted to male priests in the sanctuary. The graduated access of the sacrificial system mirrors the graduated approach to YHWH: the holy of holies (high priest only once per year), the holy place (priests only), the court (all Israel), outside the court (Gentiles, excluded). The NT dissolution of these gradations is the theological argument of Hebrews and Ephesians: in Christ, the full access of the most holy is opened to all (Heb 10:19-22; Eph 2:18).
6.30: vekol chatat asher yuva midamah el ohel mo'ed lekapper bakodesh lo te'achel ba'eish tisaref (no sin offering whose blood is brought into the tent of meeting to make atonement in the holy place shall be eaten; it shall be burned with fire): the key rule distinguishing ordinary sin offerings from the special ones. When the sin offering's blood is taken inside the veil (as in Yom Kippur, Lev 16, and the offerings for priest and congregation, Lev 4:3-21), the entire carcass must be burned outside the camp — it cannot be eaten. The reason: the blood has entered the most holy place carrying the sin burden; the associated flesh is too charged for ordinary consumption. Hebrews 13:11-12 applies this: Jesus suffered ‘outside the gate’ as the fulfillment of the burned sin-offering whose blood entered the most holy place.

7.1: vezot torat ha-asham qodesh qodashim hu (this is the Torah of the guilt offering; it is most holy): the fifth torat formula. The asham is assigned the highest holiness grade (qodesh qodashim). The prominence of this designation reflects the asham's function: it deals with violations that require reparation — the most serious category of inadvertent sin against YHWH's property (holy things) and against neighbor (6:1-7). Its blood goes to the altar as the most holy things; its meat is eaten only by male priests in the sanctuary.
7.2: bimqom asher yishachatu et ha-olah yishachatu et ha-asham (in the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered, the guilt offering shall be slaughtered): the same location rule as the sin offering (6:25) — the north side of the altar. ve-et damo yizroq al hamizbeach saviv (its blood shall be thrown/sprinkled against all sides of the altar): the blood manipulation for the asham uses zaraq (throw/dash), the same verb as for the burnt offering (1:5) and peace offering (3:2). The blood covers all four sides of the altar — comprehensive atonement in every direction.
7.3: veet kol chelbo yaqriv mimenu (he shall offer all the fat from it): the fat portions are YHWH's portion in all animal sacrifices. The asham's fat offering follows the same pattern as the peace offering (ch3): the fat tail, the fat covering the entrails, both kidneys with their fat, and the lobe of the liver are all burned on the altar. Fat (chelev) in Israelite thought is the richest, most nourishing part of the animal — YHWH's portion is always the best (see also the firstfruits principle).
7.4: ushte hakliayot vehachelev asher aleihen (the two kidneys with the fat on them): the kidneys in Hebrew anthropology are the seat of deep inner life and conscience (cf. Ps 7:9; 16:7; Jer 11:20 — YHWH searches the kidneys). The offering of the kidneys to YHWH symbolizes the consecration of one's deepest interior life, one's most hidden self. veyoteret al hakaved al hakliayot yesirenah (the lobe of the liver, which he shall remove with the kidneys): the liver and kidneys are the internal organs most associated with life and divination in the ancient Near East (cf. Ezek 21:21 for pagan liver divination). Their dedication to YHWH asserts that the seat of life and knowledge belongs to him alone.
7.5: vehiqtir otam hakohen hamizbeach ishah laYHWH (the priest burns them on the altar as a food offering to YHWH): qetoret (burning offering, smoke) — the fat burns into smoke and rises as the divine portion. Ishah laYHWH (‘food offering to YHWH,’ sometimes translated ‘fire offering’ but better as ‘food gift’) is the technical term for the altar's upward portion. The entire sacrificial system uses the metaphor of God receiving a meal — not because YHWH needs food (Ps 50:12-13: ‘I will not accept a bull from your house or goats from your folds’) but because the metaphor communicates covenant fellowship: the host and guest share a meal.
7.6: kol zachar bakohanim yochalenah (every male among the priests may eat it): as with the sin offering, the asham meat is restricted to male priests. bimaqom qadosh ye'achel qodesh qodashim hu (it shall be eaten in a holy place; it is most holy): the sanctuary context requirement for qodesh qodashim consumption. The two-fold holiness restriction (gender and location) enforces the graduated holiness system: the closer one is to the divine center, the stricter the requirements.
7.7: kahatat ka-asham torat achat lahem (like the sin offering, like the guilt offering — one law governs them both): the summary equation of the two most important purification offerings. Despite the distinctions in application (chattat for impurity and inadvertent sin; asham for violated trust and property violations), they share the same procedural law: priestly consumption, most holy status, same-location slaughter. hakohen asher yechapper bo lo yihyeh (the priest who makes atonement with it shall have it): the officiating priest receives the sin and guilt offering meats. This is the economic provision for the priesthood from the atonement offerings — the ministry of reconciliation sustains those who perform it.
7.8: vehakohen hamaqriv et olat ish or ha-olah asher hiqriv lakohen lo tihyeh (the priest who offers a man's burnt offering shall keep the hide of the burnt offering he offered): the burnt offering is entirely consumed on the altar — but its hide remains. The priest who offers the burnt offering receives the hide. Leather was commercially significant in the ancient world; this provision supplements the priestly income from a portion not itself consecrated to the altar. The detailed listing of priestly portions in 7:8-36 reflects the comprehensive economic system supporting the Levitical ministry.
7.9: vekhol minchah asher te'afeh batanur (every grain offering baked in an oven): three types of grain offering preparation: oven-baked, pan-prepared, and griddle-prepared. All baked grain offerings go to the officiating priest. The variety of preparation methods reflects the practical realities of the ancient kitchen — YHWH's instructions are embedded in real economic and culinary life, not abstracted from it.
7.10: vekhol minchah belulah vashemen vachaevah lekol bene Aharon tihyeh ish keachiv (every grain offering mixed with oil or dry shall be shared equally among all Aaron's sons): the distribution rule for non-baked grain offerings. Unlike the baked offering (which goes to the officiant), mixed grain offerings are shared equally among all the priests. Ish keachiv (each like his brother) — equal distribution regardless of rank. The priestly fraternity is characterized by material equality even while it has functional hierarchy (high priest over priests over Levites).
7.11: vezot torat zevach hashelamim (this is the Torah of the sacrifice of the fellowship offerings): the peace offering section. Shelamim (fellowship/peace offerings, from shalom) is the most communal of the offerings: its meat is shared between YHWH (the fat), the priests (portions), and the offerer and family (the rest). It is the covenant meal — YHWH and the offerer eating together. The three subcategories of shelamim are introduced: thanksgiving (todah, v.12), vow offering (neder, v.16), and freewill offering (nedavah, v.16).
7.12: im al todah yaqrivenhu (if he offers it as a thanksgiving): the todah (thanksgiving offering) is the most urgent shelamim — it must be eaten on the same day (v.15). vehiqriv al zevach hatodah challot matzot (he shall offer with it unleavened cakes): the todah includes a complex bread offering: unleavened loaves mixed with oil, unleavened wafers spread with oil, and fine-flour cakes well mixed with oil — three types of unleavened bread. The abundance of accompanying bread signals the festive, celebratory character of the thanksgiving offering.
7.13: al challot lechem chametz yaqriv qorbano (along with unleavened cakes, he shall bring leavened bread as his offering with his thanksgiving fellowship offering): the todah uniquely includes leavened bread alongside the unleavened. The presence of leavened bread in the thanksgiving context (though it is never burned on the altar — only the unleavened portions are offered to YHWH) reflects the ordinary bread of daily life joining the sacred feast. The thanksgiving includes the ordinary alongside the consecrated.
7.14: vehiqriv mimenu echad mikol qorban terumah laYHWH (he shall present one from each offering as a contribution to YHWH): the wave bread (terumah, contribution/heave offering) — one loaf from each type presented as YHWH's portion before being given to the priest. lakohen hazoreq et dam hashelamim lo yihyeh (it belongs to the priest who throws the blood of the fellowship offerings): the priest who performs the blood manipulation receives the wave bread — the officiating minister's portion connects his act to its liturgical reward.
7.15: uvevar zevach todat shelamav beyom qorbano ye'achel (the meat of the thanksgiving fellowship offering shall be eaten on the day it is offered): the todah must be consumed entirely on the offering day. lo yaniach mimenu ad boqer (none of it may be left until morning): no leftovers from the thanksgiving. The urgency of complete consumption on the offering day reflects the character of thanksgiving: gratitude is immediate, whole, and urgent. What remains unexpressed until ‘another day’ is not gratitude but its deferral. Paul echoes this urgency: ‘giving thanks always and for everything’ (Eph 5:20).
7.16: ve'im neder o nedavah zevach qorbano (if his sacrifice is a vow offering or a freewill offering): two-day consumption for vow and freewill offerings — more relaxed than the todah. The distinction communicates: the vow and freewill offerings express ongoing commitment and spontaneous generosity rather than immediate response to specific divine grace. The different time-frames are fitting: thanksgiving is urgent; ongoing dedication is sustained.
