7.17: vehannotar mivevar hazevach bayom hashelishi ba-eish yisaref (any remaining meat from the sacrifice on the third day must be burned up with fire): the absolute limit on three-day-old meat from peace offerings. The burning prevents the sacred meat from becoming ordinary food. bayom hashelishi (on the third day): the three-day deadline is curious — the same three-day pattern appears in other significant contexts (Hos 6:2: ‘on the third day he will raise us up’; 1 Cor 15:4: ‘raised on the third day according to the Scriptures’). The sacrificial meat that must not remain past the third day is the type of the body that did not remain in the tomb past the third day.
7.18: ve'im he'achol ye'achel mibesar zevach shelamav bayom hashelishi lo yeratze (if any of the fellowship offering is eaten on the third day, it will not be accepted): lo yeratze (it will not be accepted, not found acceptable) — the technical term for YHWH's rejection of an offering. piggul yihyeh (it is tainted, piggul): piggul (offensive, detestable) — a sacrifice that has gone bad beyond the ordained time. venashea avono (the one who eats it bears his iniquity): tainted-offering consumption is itself a sin requiring atonement. The boundary of legitimate sacrifice is not negotiable by the offerer's convenience.
7.19: vehavesar asher yiga bekol tamei lo ye'achel (meat that touches any unclean thing shall not be eaten): the contagious impurity principle applied to sacrificial meat. As holiness communicates by contact (6:18, 27), so impurity communicates by contact. Sacrificial meat that has become contaminated must be destroyed. ve-habasar kol tahor yochal et habasar (the clean meat — all who are ritually clean may eat it): the positive rule: clean meat may be eaten by all ritually clean persons, not just priests. The peace offering is the democratic sacrifice — shared broadly.
7.20: vehanefesh asher tochel basar mizevach hashelamim asher laYHWH vetum'ato alav (if a person eats the flesh of the fellowship offering belonging to YHWH while his uncleanness is on him): the grave offense: eating sacred meat in a state of impurity. venichretah hanefesh hahiv me'ameha (that person shall be cut off from his people): the karet penalty — the most severe sanction in the Levitical system. Karet (‘cutting off’) is debated: it may mean premature death, exclusion from the covenant community, or eschatological judgment. All three dimensions are present: violation of the holiness boundary separates from the community of YHWH's people.
7.21: venefesh ki tiga bekol tamei (if anyone touches anything unclean): the rule extended to any unclean contact before eating peace offering meat. The multiple categories of uncleanness listed (‘human uncleanness, an unclean animal, or any unclean detestable thing’) cover the full range of contamination sources. venichretah hanefesh hahiv me'ameha: the karet formula repeated — the holiness of the peace offering fellowship meal is not a matter of preference but of covenant membership.
7.22: A new divine speech formula introducing the fat and blood prohibitions (vv. 22-27), the fundamental dietary restrictions related to sacrifice. These are addressed to ‘the people of Israel’ — not merely the priests but the whole covenant community.
7.23: kol chelev shor vechesef ve-ez lo tocheilu (you shall eat no fat — whether from ox, sheep, or goat): the chelev (suet/hard fat, the internal sacrificial fat) prohibition. This fat is YHWH's portion in all animal sacrifice. It is distinguished from shuman (ordinary fat/suet) which may be eaten. The specific fat forbidden is the chelev of sacrificeable animals (ox, sheep, goat) — not fat in general. YHWH claims the richest portion; the prohibition enforces that claim on the whole community, not just during the offering but in all domestic consumption of these animals.
7.24: vechelev neveilah vechelev terefa ye'aseh lekol melacha (the fat of an animal that dies of itself or is torn by wild animals may be put to other use): the fat of naturally dead (neveilah) or torn (terefah) animals — which cannot be offered — may be used for other purposes (leather-working, lamp fuel, etc.) but not for food. The distinction: YHWH's portion from living animals must not be eaten; the same fat from non-offering animals is not for human food (being still YHWH's in principle) but may be used for other purposes.
7.25: ki kol ochel chelev min habehemah asher yaqriv mimena ishah laYHWH (anyone who eats the fat of an animal from which a food offering may be made to YHWH): the karet penalty for eating the forbidden fat. The same cutting-off threatened for eating the peace offering in impurity (v.20) applies here — eating YHWH's portion is as grave as eating sacred meat while unclean. The consistency of the karet penalty across multiple violations communicates that the holiness boundary is equally serious wherever it appears.
7.26: vekhol dam lo tocheilu bechol moshivoteichem (you shall eat no blood of any kind in any of your dwelling places): the blood prohibition is universal — not limited to sacrificial contexts but extending to ‘all your dwellings.’ Blood is prohibited both because it is the bearer of life (Lev 17:11) and because it belongs to YHWH as the atonement mechanism. The prohibition applies to birds and land animals — the prohibition extends to the entire animal kingdom available for food.
7.27: kol nefesh asher tochel kol dam venichretah hanefesh hahiv me'ameha (whoever eats any blood shall be cut off from his people): the karet for blood consumption is stated as an absolute principle in a single sentence. The severity of the penalty reflects the theological weight of the blood prohibition: blood = life = YHWH's atoning mechanism. The one who consumes blood usurps what belongs to YHWH alone and destroys the very mechanism by which atonement is made. The NT explicitly addresses this: the Jerusalem Council retained the blood prohibition for Gentile Christians (Acts 15:20, 29) while the eucharistic ‘drinking the blood of Christ’ (John 6:53-56) operates in a completely different register — Christ's blood is not literally consumed but received by faith.
7.28: Another divine speech formula introducing the wave and heave portions of the peace offering (vv. 28-34). These specific priestly portions are the subject of a separate divine command — their importance justifies distinct legislative treatment.
7.29: medaber el bene Yisrael lemor hamaqriv zevach shelamav laYHWH yavi et qorbano laYHWH mizivcho shelamav (tell the people of Israel: whoever offers his fellowship sacrifice to YHWH must bring to YHWH his own offering from his fellowship sacrifice): the offerer personally brings the wave offering — this is not delegated to the priest. The personal act of bringing the offering before YHWH is the offerer's worshipful gesture, not just a delivery mechanism.
7.30: yadav teviy'enah et ishei YHWH (his own hands shall bring the LORD's food offerings): the offerer's own hands present the fat and breast before YHWH. et hachelev al hachazeh yavienah lehaniph oto tenufah lifnei YHWH (the fat together with the breast — so that the breast may be presented as a wave offering before YHWH): the tenufah (wave offering) involves a back-and-forth movement before the altar — the offerer presents the portion ‘before YHWH,’ symbolically giving it, then the priest receives it back on YHWH's behalf. The wave motion enacts the giving-and-receiving of the covenant relationship: the offerer gives; YHWH accepts and provides for the priests.
7.31: vehiqtir hakohen et hachelev hamizbeach (the priest burns the fat on the altar): the fat portion goes up in smoke — YHWH's portion returns to him as fire and smoke. vehayah hachazeh leAharon ulvanav (but the breast shall belong to Aaron and his sons): the breast — the wave offering — becomes the priestly community's portion. The wave breast feeds all the priests, including the high priest's household, distributed as a common meal.
7.32: ve-et shoq hayamin titenu terumah lakohen (you shall give the right thigh to the priest as a contribution): the right thigh (shoq hayamin) is the second priestly portion from the peace offering — the ‘heave offering’ (terumah, contribution, literally ‘lifted up’). The right side is consistently the privileged side in Israelite culture (cf. the right hand of God as the place of honor, Ps 110:1). The officiating priest receives the right thigh as his personal portion, distinct from the communal wave breast.
7.33: hamaqriv et dam hashelamim vehahelev mibene Aharon lo tihyeh shoq hayamin lemanat (the son of Aaron who offers the blood and fat of the fellowship offering shall have the right thigh as his portion): the officiating priest specifically — the one who performs the blood and fat manipulation — receives the right thigh. The connection between the ritual act and its economic provision establishes the principle: ministry and provision are linked. Those who do the work of atonement are sustained by the atonement system.
7.34: ki et chazeh hatenufa ve-et shoq haterumah laqachti me-et bene Yisrael mizivchei shelameihem (for I have taken the wave breast and the heave thigh from the Israelites' fellowship offerings): YHWH speaks in first person — ‘I have taken’ — then ‘I have given them to Aaron the priest and his sons as a perpetual due from the Israelites.’ The priestly portions are explicitly YHWH's gift: he first accepts them from the people, then redistributes them to the priests. The economy of provision is triangular: people offer to YHWH, YHWH gives to priests through the people's offerings. Paul applies the same logic: ‘those who preach the gospel should receive their living from the gospel’ (1 Cor 9:14), citing explicitly the temple precedent.
7.35: zot mishchat Aharon umishchat banav me'ishei YHWH (this is the portion allotted to Aaron and his sons from YHWH's food offerings): mishchah (‘anointing portion’ or ‘portion’) — the same root as mashiach (anointed one). The portion of Aaron (mishchat Aharon) is a term that connects priestly provision to priestly anointing: they receive their portion because they are anointed for the service of YHWH. Christ as the Anointed One (Mashiach, Christos) receives ‘the name above every name’ (Phil 2:9) as the priestly-royal share for his completed work of atonement.
7.36: asher tzivah YHWH latet lahem beyom moshcho otam (which YHWH commanded to be given them on the day of their anointing): the priestly portions date from the ordination ceremony — they are not a later supplement but an original covenant provision. chuqat olam ledoroteichem (a permanent statute throughout their generations): the chuqat olam formula applies to the priestly portions as to all the permanent cultic ordinances. The Levitical priesthood's economic provision is as permanent as the priesthood itself.
7.37: zot hatorah la-olah laminchah velachatat vela-asham velamiullim velizevach hashelamim (this is the law of the burnt offering, the grain offering, the sin offering, the guilt offering, the ordination offering, and the fellowship offering): a summary formula covering all six major sacrifice types. The six offerings together constitute the complete sacrificial system of the Torah: olah (burnt, vv. 6:8-13), minchah (grain, 6:14-23), chattat (sin, 6:24-30), asham (guilt, 7:1-7), miluim (ordination, Lev 8-9), shelamim (peace/fellowship, 7:11-36). Hebrews reads this entire list as fulfilled in Christ's one sacrifice: he is the olah (full consecration to God), minchah (pleasing gift), chattat (sin-bearing), asham (reparation), miluim (ordained high priest), and shelamim (peace with God secured).
7.38: asher tzivah YHWH et Moshe behar Sinai (which YHWH commanded Moses on Mount Sinai): the concluding authority citation — the Sinai origin of the sacrificial laws grounds their divine authority. beyom tzivoto et bene Yisrael lehaqriv et qorbane'hem laYHWH bemidbar Sinai (on the day he directed the Israelites to bring their offerings to YHWH in the wilderness of Sinai): the wilderness setting is theologically significant — the sacrificial system was given during the period of covenant formation, in the liminal space between Egypt and Canaan. The wilderness is the space of total dependence on YHWH where the covenant protocols were established in their purity, before territorial and political complications.

20.1: YHWH spoke to Moses — ch 20 provides the capital punishment schedule for violations enumerated in chs 18 and 19. The sequence ch 18 (prohibition) → ch 20 (penalty) is interrupted by ch 19's broad ethical code, which contextualizes the sexual prohibitions within the larger pattern of holiness ethics.
20.2: Say to the people of Israel: Any one of the people of Israel or of the strangers who sojourn in Israel who gives any of his children to Molech shall surely be put to death (mot yumat). The people of the land shall stone him with stones — Molech sacrifice (child-burning, cf. Lev 18:21; 2 Kgs 23:10; Jer 32:35) is the first and most severe prohibition; the double infinitive absolute mot yumat (dying he shall die) signals irreversible capital punishment. The community execution by stoning makes the entire people the instrument of judgment.
20.3: I myself will set my face against that man and will cut him off from among his people, because he has given one of his children to Molech, to make my sanctuary unclean (lema'an tamme et-miqdashi) and to profane my holy name — the double offense of Molech sacrifice: it defiles the sanctuary (the impurity of child-murder touches even YHWH's holy space) and profanes the Name (Israel's child-sacrifice is a desecration of covenant identity). Even if the community fails to execute the offender (v.4), divine karet applies.
20.4: And if the people of the land do at all close their eyes to that man when he gives one of his children to Molech, and do not put him to death — communal negligence (looking the other way at Molech sacrifice) creates communal guilt. The following verse (v.5) extends divine judgment to the negligent community, treating silence in the face of atrocity as complicity.
20.5: Then I will set my face against that man and against his clan and will cut them off from among their people, him and all who follow him in whoring after Molech — the clan (mishpachto) shares in the guilt of the individual Molech worshiper; collective culpability extends to all who follow the same path (ha-zonim acharav, those who whore after Molech). The language of spiritual adultery (znut) frames idolatry as covenant infidelity.
20.6: If a person turns to mediums (ha-ovot) and necromancers (yidde'onim), whoring after them, I will set my face against that person and will cut him off — consulting the dead is spiritual adultery (znut) just as Molech worship is; both divert loyalty due to YHWH toward illegitimate supernatural powers. The divine self-initiated karet (v.6) operates even when the community does not formally prosecute.
20.7: Consecrate yourselves (hitqaddashtem), therefore, and be holy (vihyitem qedoshim), for I am YHWH your God — the positive command embedded within the penalty schedule; holiness is not merely avoiding the prohibited behaviors but actively orienting oneself toward YHWH. The reflexive hitqaddashtem (consecrate yourselves) implies ongoing, personal, intentional separation.
20.8: Keep my statutes (shemartem et-chuqqotai) and do them; I am YHWH who sanctifies you (ani YHWH meqaddishchem) — the declaration that YHWH is the one who sanctifies Israel (meqaddish) is the ground, not the result, of Israel's holiness; Israel is called to embody a holiness that YHWH first effects. The same formula appears in Lev 21:8,15,23; 22:9,16,32.
20.9: For anyone who curses his father or his mother shall surely be put to death (mot yumat); he has cursed his father or his mother; his blood is upon him (damo bo) — the capital offense of cursing parents (Exod 21:17; Prov 20:20) is placed immediately after the holiness call, connecting family order to the covenant structure. The phrase damo bo (his blood is on him) absolves the executioners of blood-guilt; the condemned bears the consequence of his own act.
20.10: If a man commits adultery (nif'al na'aph) with the wife of his neighbor, both the adulterer and the adulteress shall surely be put to death — adultery (Exod 20:14) is capital for both parties; the equal punishment of man and woman is significant in an ancient Near Eastern context where adultery laws often focused exclusively on the woman. Both ha-na'ef (the adulterer) and ha-no'evet (the adulteress) are subject to mot yumat.
20.11: If a man lies with his father's wife, he has uncovered his father's nakedness (ervat aviv gilla); both of them shall surely be put to death; their blood is upon them — intercourse with a father's wife (stepmother, not biological mother) is incest under the Levitical framework. The language ervat aviv gilla (he has uncovered his father's nakedness) implies violation of the father's domain and honor, not only the woman's personhood.
20.12: If a man lies with his daughter-in-law (kalato), both of them shall surely be put to death; they have committed perversion (tevel asu); their blood is upon them — tevel (confusion, perversion) is the term used for violation of species/status boundaries; daughter-in-law intercourse violates the family structure that marriage creates. Both parties receive capital punishment.
20.13: If a man lies with a male as with a woman (mishkevey isha), both of them have committed an abomination (to'evah asu); they shall surely be put to death; their blood is upon them — to'evah (abomination) is the term applied in Lev 18:22; the penalty schedule confirms capital punishment for both parties. The two-term sequence tevel (perverted confusion of order) and to'evah (abomination) covers both natural-order violation and divine-disgust violation.
20.14: If a man takes a woman and her mother also, it is depravity (zimah hi); he and they shall be burned with fire, that there may be no depravity among you — taking a woman and her mother (Lev 18:17) is zimah (wickedness, lewdness); burning is the most severe execution mode in Leviticus, used here and for the high priest's daughter who prostitutes herself (Lev 21:9). The collective judgment (all three burned) reflects that all three participated in the violation.
20.15: If a man lies with an animal, he shall surely be put to death (mot yumat), and you shall kill the animal — bestiality (Lev 18:23; Exod 22:19; Deut 27:21) is capital for the man and requires the killing of the animal; the animal's execution prevents ongoing association with the violation and removes an instrument that caused the defilement.
20.16: If a woman approaches any animal and lies with it, you shall kill the woman and the animal; they shall surely be put to death; their blood is upon them — the female parallel: a woman who approaches an animal for sexual contact is equally subject to capital punishment. The symmetrical treatment of male and female bestiality is consistent with the ch 20 pattern of equal penalties for both parties.
20.17: If a man takes his sister, a daughter of his father or a daughter of his mother, and sees her nakedness and she sees his nakedness, it is a disgrace (chesed hu); they shall be cut off (venikhrevu) in the sight of the children of their people — sibling incest (Lev 18:9) receives karet rather than immediate capital execution; the term chesed here is an archaic use meaning 'disgrace/reproach' rather than the more common covenant-love meaning. Public karet (in the sight of the people) is social as well as divine exclusion.
20.18: If a man lies with a woman during her menstrual period (niddah) and uncovers her nakedness, he has made naked her fountain (et-mekorah ereh), and she has uncovered the fountain of her blood. Both of them shall be cut off from among their people — the niddah violation (Lev 18:19; 15:24) carries karet (not immediate capital punishment) for both parties. The Hebrew mekorah (source, fountain) uses bodily imagery for the generative source; ereh is the same root as ervah (nakedness, the term used throughout chs 18 and 20).
20.19: You shall not uncover the nakedness of your mother's sister or of your father's sister, for that is to make naked one's relative (et-she'ero ereh); they shall bear their iniquity — aunt-nephew relationships (Lev 18:12-13) do not carry capital punishment here but the bearing of iniquity (avon yissau), implying a lesser but still significant degree of guilt. She'er (flesh-relation, close kin) is the basis of the prohibition.
20.20: If a man lies with his uncle's wife, he has uncovered his uncle's nakedness (dodato gilla); they shall bear their sin (chet'am yissau); they shall die childless (ariyrim yamuthu) — uncle's wife violation (Lev 18:14) results in dying childless (ariri) rather than execution; the punishment is the absence of covenant continuation — no posterity. Childlessness in the Israelite context was understood as a covenantal consequence, not merely biological misfortune.
20.21: If a man takes his brother's wife, it is impurity (niddah hi); he has uncovered his brother's nakedness (achiv gilla); they shall be childless — brother's wife violation (Lev 18:16; cf. Deut 25:5-10 where levirate marriage is the exception that proves the rule) results in childlessness. The tension between the Lev 20:21 prohibition and the Deut 25 levirate provision is resolved by noting the Deut 25 exception applies only when the brother died without an heir.
20.22: You shall therefore keep all my statutes and all my rules and do them, that the land where I am bringing you to live may not vomit you out (vataqe ha-aretz etchem) — the land-vomiting image from Lev 18:25-28 recurs; the land itself responds to moral defilement by expelling its inhabitants. Covenant obedience is not merely personal ethics but the condition for continued habitation in the promised land.
20.23: And you shall not walk in the customs of the nation that I am driving out before you, for they did all these things, and therefore I detested them — Canaanite practices (all the sexual and religious violations listed in chs 18 and 20) are the reason for Canaanite expulsion; Israel is warned not to replicate the pattern that brought divine judgment on the prior inhabitants. The conquest is simultaneously punishment of Canaan and warning to Israel.
20.24: But I have said to you, 'You shall inherit their land, and I will give it to you to possess, a land flowing with milk and honey.' I am YHWH your God, who has separated you from the peoples (hifdal eschem min-ha'ammim) — the divine promise of possession (yerushah) frames the holiness requirement: separation from the nations' practices is the precondition for inheriting the nations' land. YHWH's separation of Israel from the peoples corresponds to Israel's separation of clean from unclean (v.25).
20.25: You shall therefore separate (hivdaltem) the clean beast from the unclean, and the unclean bird from the clean. You shall not make yourselves detestable by beast or by bird or by anything with which the ground crawls — the kashrut taxonomy of Lev 11 is recalled here as the dietary dimension of the same separateness principle governing sexual ethics; Israel's dietary, sexual, and social separateness form a unified holiness stance toward the surrounding nations.
20.26: You shall be holy to me, for I YHWH am holy and have separated you from the peoples, that you should be mine (lihyot li) — the climactic statement of the Holiness Code's social and sexual legislation: holiness is YHWH's own character, communicated to Israel through the separation-act of election. The purpose of holiness (lihyot li, to be mine) frames all ethical requirements as expressions of covenantal belonging rather than mere legal compliance.
20.27: A man or a woman who is a medium (ov) or a necromancer (yidde'oni) shall surely be put to death. They shall be stoned with stones; their blood shall be upon them — the capital penalty for mediums and necromancers (cf. v.6 for the client; here the practitioner) closes the chapter. Stoning is the standard communal execution; the blood-guilt formula absolves the community. Saul's violation of this law at En-dor (1 Sam 28:7-19) represents the same pattern of covenant infidelity that led to his rejection.

21.1: And YHWH said to Moses, 'Speak to the priests, the sons of Aaron, and say to them' — the double address (YHWH to Moses → Moses to the priests) signals that the priestly legislation of ch 21 is mediated through Moses to Aaron's sons specifically; the priests are the subject of ch 21's regulations, not the entire people as in chs 19–20.
21.2: No one shall make himself unclean for the dead among his people, except for his nearest kin (le-she'ero ha-qarov elav): his mother, his father, his son, his daughter, his brother — the priest's general restriction from corpse-defilement has explicit exceptions for the seven closest relatives (vv.2-3); corpse impurity (Num 19) lasts seven days and disqualifies from sanctuary service. The priest's vocation requires limiting the normal human obligation to mourn the dead physically.
21.3: His virgin sister (achoto ha-vetulah ha-qerovah elav) who is near to him because she has had no husband; for her he may make himself unclean — the unmarried sister who has no husband to mourn her is included in the permissible exceptions; she remains under her brother's protection until marriage, and he retains the mourning obligation for her as for other nearest kin.
21.4: He shall not make himself unclean as a husband among his people and so profane himself — the Hebrew is difficult; ba'al be'ammav may mean 'as a leading man among his people' (i.e., he must not defile himself in ways inappropriate to his rank) or may refer to defilement for a wife's relatives beyond the immediate household. The priestly status creates a stricter standard than ordinary Israelites face.
21.5: They shall not make bald patches on their heads (lo yiqrechu qorchah be-roshecha), nor shave off the edges of their beards (pe'at zeqanam lo yegalleihu), nor make any cuts on their bodies — the same mourning-rite prohibitions of Lev 19:27-28 are repeated specifically for priests; what is forbidden to all Israelites (Lev 19:27) is especially forbidden to priests whose bodies serve in the sanctuary. The prohibitions align with 21:4: the priest's body must be maintained for sanctuary service.
21.6: They shall be holy to their God and not profane the name of their God (lo yechalelu shem Eloheihem), for they offer the LORD's food offerings (ishei YHWH), the bread of their God (lechem Eloheihem); therefore they shall be holy — the ground of priestly holiness: they handle the food of God. The expression lechem Eloheihem (bread of their God) uses the language of the divine table (Lev 3:11,16; Num 28:2) to explain why the priest's body and life must be consecrated above the ordinary Israelite level.
21.7: They shall not marry a prostitute or a woman who has been defiled (zonah vechalala lo yiqqachu), neither shall they marry a woman divorced from her husband, for the priest is holy to his God — the priest's marriage partner must share the holiness standard; a zonah (prostitute) or chalala (profaned woman, one born of a prohibited priestly union) or divorcee would introduce defilement into the priestly household. The prohibition is not judgment on the woman's person but recognition that the priestly household must maintain a higher purity standard.
21.8: You shall sanctify him therefore, for he offers the bread of your God. He shall be holy to you, for I, YHWH, who sanctify you, am holy (qadosh ani YHWH meqadishchem) — the congregation is addressed: they bear responsibility for maintaining the priest's holiness standards. The formula qadosh ani YHWH meqadishchem (I am YHWH who sanctifies you) grounds the priestly holiness requirement in the divine character, not merely in cultic regulation.
21.9: And the daughter of any priest, if she profanes herself by whoring (ki tachel liznot), she profanes her father; she shall be burned with fire — the priest's daughter who prostitutes herself receives the most severe execution (burning, cf. Lev 20:14) because her conduct profanes her father's sacred office. The priest's household represents an extension of his sacred character; its defilement defiles his priestly capacity.
21.10: The priest who is chief among his brothers (ha-kohen ha-gadol me-echav), on whose head the anointing oil has been poured and who has been consecrated to wear the garments, shall not let the hair of his head hang loose (lo yifra rosho) or tear his clothes — the high priest has stricter mourning restrictions than ordinary priests; he may not display any of the standard mourning gestures (loose hair, torn garments, cf. Lev 10:6) even for the death of nearest kin.
21.11: He shall not go in to any dead bodies nor make himself unclean, even for his father or for his mother — the high priest's prohibition extends even to parents; no exception is made as it is for ordinary priests (vv.2-3). The high priest's complete separation from corpse impurity reflects his unique role as the one who enters the most holy place; his continual cultic purity cannot be interrupted even for family bereavement.
21.12: He shall not go out of the sanctuary lest he profane the sanctuary of his God (ve-et-miqdash Elohav lo yechalel), for the consecration of the anointing oil of his God is upon him; I am YHWH — the high priest is confined to the sanctuary area even during mourning periods; his departure would profane the sanctuary by associating it with the corpse-defilement he would contract. The anointing oil that consecrates him creates an inseparable bond with the sanctuary that constrains his movements.
21.13: And he shall take a wife in her virginity (bevtuloteha yiqqach ishah) — the high priest must marry a virgin; his marriage partner requirement is the strictest of all (ordinary priests may not marry prostitutes or divorcees, v.7; high priests must specifically marry virgins). The requirement ensures the high priest's household is free of prior sexual history that could compromise ritual purity.
21.14: A widow, or a divorced woman, or a woman who has been defiled, or a prostitute, these he shall not marry. But he shall take as his wife a virgin from his own people (min-amav) — the four prohibited marriage partners for the high priest: widow (almanah), divorcee (gerushah), defiled woman (chalala), prostitute (zonah). The positive requirement adds min-amav (from his own people), which rabbinic tradition interprets as requiring an Israelite woman, not a proselyte.
21.15: That he may not profane his offspring among his people, for I am YHWH who sanctifies him — the purpose of the marriage restriction: to preserve the holiness of the high priestly lineage. Zar'o (his seed/offspring) must not be mixed with women whose status would compromise the priestly succession. The divine self-identification formula again grounds the requirement in YHWH's own sanctifying character.
21.16: And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying — a new revelation formula introduces the legislation on disqualifying physical conditions (vv.17-23), separating it as a distinct legal unit from the marriage and mourning regulations of vv.1-15.
21.17: Speak to Aaron, saying: None of your offspring throughout their generations who has a blemish (mum) may approach to offer the bread of his God — the legislation on physical disqualification is addressed to Aaron specifically (not to Aaron's sons as in v.1), because it concerns his lineage's priestly capacity. Mum (blemish, defect) is the same term used for sacrificial animals (Lev 1:3; 3:1; 22:20); the parallel between unblemished animal and unblemished priest is structural and deliberate.
21.18: For no one who has a blemish shall draw near: a man blind or lame (iver o pisseach), or one who has a mutilated face or a limb too long (charumm o sarua) — the first four disqualifying conditions: blindness, lameness, mutilated nose (or flat nose), disproportionate limb. These are physical conditions that would make the priest visually conspicuous at the altar; the rationale is aesthetic-representational, not moral.
21.19: Or a man who has an injured foot or an injured hand (shever regel o shever yad) — broken foot or hand (whether healed with deformity or actively broken); the term shever (fracture) suggests structural damage rather than temporary injury. These conditions would impair the physical actions of priestly service at the altar.
21.20: Or a hunchback or a dwarf (gibben o daqq), or a man with a defect in his sight (tevalul be-eyno) or an itching disease (garav) or scabs (yalefet) or crushed testicles (meroach ashekh) — six additional conditions extending the list: spinal deformity, stunted growth, eye disorder (cataract/spot), chronic skin conditions, and genital damage. The comprehensive list covers conditions visible, structural, dermal, and reproductive.
21.21: No man of the offspring of Aaron the priest who has a blemish shall come near to offer the LORD's food offerings; since he has a blemish, he shall not come near to offer the bread of his God — the summary prohibition: the blemish disqualifies from approach (yiqqrav) to the altar and from offering (lehaqriv) the food-offerings. The disability does not excommunicate the priest from the community or from his priestly identity, only from active altar ministry.
21.22: He may eat the bread of his God, both of the most holy and of the holy things (miqqodesh ha-qedashim umin-ha-qedashim yokhal) — the physically disqualified priest retains full access to priestly food portions: both the most-holy offerings (Lev 2:3,10; 6:17,25,29; 7:1,6) and the lesser-holy offerings. His altar service is suspended but his priestly identity and its material privileges remain fully intact.
21.23: But he shall not go through the veil (el-ha-parochet lo yavo) or approach the altar, because he has a blemish, that he may not profane my sanctuaries (mikdashay lo yechalel), for I am YHWH who sanctifies them — the two forbidden zones: the veil (access to the inner sanctuary/most holy place) and the altar. The priest with a blemish would profane these spaces not through moral failure but through structural incongruity with the symbolism of wholeness the sanctuary requires. The meqadishchem formula closes the section with the same divine sanctifying authority that grounds all priestly requirements.
21.24: So Moses spoke to Aaron and to his sons and to all the people of Israel — Moses addresses all three audiences: Aaron (head of the priestly order), his sons (the priests in service), and all Israel (who must honor the disqualification provisions). The broad audience confirms that the physical qualification system is public knowledge, not an internal priestly secret.

18.1: Standard address formula opening the Holiness Code proper. Chapter 18 begins with a framing preamble (vv. 1-5) before listing the sexual prohibitions. The placement of the sexual ethics code immediately after the blood/sacrifice laws (ch. 17) signals that purity of body, like purity of sacrifice, is part of the holiness program.
18.2: ani YHWH Eloheikhem: 'I am YHWH your God.' This self-identification formula appears throughout chs. 18-26 as a refrain anchoring each law in YHWH's own identity and covenant relationship. The laws are not arbitrary rules but expressions of who YHWH is and what the covenant relationship requires. The formula occurs 48 times in Leviticus, clustering in the Holiness Code.
18.3: kema'asei erets-Mitsrayim asher yeshavtem bah lo ta'asu ukema'asei erets-Kena'an: 'You shall not do as they do in the land of Egypt, where you lived, and you shall not do as they do in the land of Canaan.' Israel's sexual ethics are defined negatively against both past (Egypt) and future (Canaan) environments. The logic: Israel is not to assimilate to surrounding cultures. The double prohibition creates Israel's ethical identity as a people genuinely distinct from both its origin and its destination.
18.4: et-mishpatai ta'asu ve'et-ḥuqqotai tishemru lalekhet bahem: 'Keep my rules and observe my statutes and walk in them.' Two legal categories: mishpatim (judgments/rules — laws whose rationale is accessible to reason) and ḥuqqot (statutes — laws whose rationale is YHWH's sovereign decree). Both require obedience; the distinction is not between easier and harder laws but between transparent and opaque ones. Lalekhet bahem (to walk in them) — the covenant life as a path, not a set of discrete acts.
18.5: ushmartem et-ḥuqqotai ve'et-mishpatai asher ya'aseh otam ha'adam vaḥai bahem: 'Keep my statutes and my rules; the person who does them shall live by them.' Vaḥai bahem (and he shall live by them) — the life-promise of the covenant commands. Paul quotes this verse in Gal 3:12 and Rom 10:5 to distinguish law-observance from faith: the law promises life to those who do all its commands; faith promises life to those who trust. Paul's point is not that the law was wrong in its life-promise but that no one fully does all its commands — only Christ did. Lev 18:5 is the positive statement of what covenant obedience achieves; Gal 3:10-13 is Paul's analysis of why it cannot be achieved without Christ.
18.6: kol-ish ish el-kol-she'er besaro lo tiqrevu legallot erva: 'None of you shall approach any close relative to uncover nakedness.' She'er basar (close relative, lit. 'flesh relative') — the term for those sharing kinship bonds. Erva (nakedness/genitalia) — the consistent term throughout ch. 18 for the sexual domain. The root glh (to uncover/expose) when used with erva means to engage in sexual relations. Lo tiqrevu (you shall not approach) uses the same approach-language as the sanctuary access rules — sexual impropriety violates sacred space.
18.7: First specific prohibition: father's sexual partner (mother). Erva avikha ve'erva immekha (the nakedness of your father and the nakedness of your mother) — the mother's body is described as 'your father's nakedness,' reflecting the marital one-flesh bond: to approach her is to violate your father. The double identification (she is your mother; she is your father's nakedness) closes both possible loopholes.
18.8: erva eshet avikha lo tegalle: 'You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father's wife.' This covers step-mothers and other wives beyond one's birth mother — the prohibition extends to anyone in the role of father's wife. Erva avikha hi (it is your father's nakedness) — the same logic as v. 7: the wife's body participates in the marital bond's sanctity.
18.9: Prohibitions on half-sisters (paternal and maternal, born inside or outside the family household). The comprehensive listing ('whether born in the house or elsewhere') closes kinship loopholes — adoption, mixed family structures, and children born outside wedlock are all included. The Holiness Code's sexual legislation is notably more comprehensive than earlier prohibitions in the Torah.
18.10: Granddaughters (son's daughter or daughter's daughter) are prohibited. Ki ervatekha henah (for they are your own nakedness) — the granddaughter participates in the grandfather's identity through descent, making incest a form of self-violation. The kinship logic: you are diminished by what happens to those who share your flesh.
18.11: The daughter of one's father's wife (step-sister through the father) — 'begotten by your father; she is your sister.' The prohibition covers relationships that might be considered distant enough to be permitted in some ancient Near Eastern contexts (e.g., Abraham's marriage to Sarah, who was his half-sister, Gen 20:12, which predates this legislation).
18.12: Father's sister — she'er avikha hi (she is your father's blood relative). The she'er (flesh) language grounds the prohibition in kinship: she shares your father's body, which makes her body an extension of your own family identity. The prohibition extends the incest boundary to the parental generation's siblings.
18.13: Mother's sister — the same reasoning. Ki she'er immekha hi (she is your mother's flesh). The symmetry between paternal and maternal prohibitions (vv. 12-13) ensures no distinction between the two lines of descent.
18.14: Father's brother's wife (aunt by marriage): you shall not approach his wife. Dedat avikha hi (she is your aunt) — the specific term dodah (aunt) appears here. The prohibition on the uncle's wife extends the sexual boundary to affinal (marriage-based) as well as consanguineal (blood-based) relationships.
18.15: Daughter-in-law: eshet binekha hi (she is your son's wife). Lo tegalle ervatah (you shall not uncover her nakedness) — this case is also mentioned in the Judah/Tamar narrative (Gen 38), where Judah unknowingly sleeps with his daughter-in-law. The Levitical prohibition makes what was transgressive in Genesis into an explicit legal offense.
18.16: Brother's wife. Erva aḥikha hi (it is your brother's nakedness) — parallel to the father-wife logic. The notable exception to this rule is the levirate law (Deut 25:5-10), which commands a man to marry his brother's widow when the brother died childless; Leviticus 18:16 prohibits this relationship in normal circumstances, while Deuteronomy creates a specific exception for line-preservation purposes.
18.17: erva isha uvittah lo tegalle: 'You shall not uncover the nakedness of a woman and her daughter.' The prohibition on simultaneous relations with mother and daughter is absolute — zimmah hi (it is depravity). The term zimmah (depravity/wickedness) marks the most egregious violations within the sexual code. The prohibition extends to granddaughters in both directions.
18.18: ve'isha el-aḥotah lo tiqqaḥ litsror: 'You shall not take a woman as a rival wife to her sister.' Tsarah (rival wife/adversary) is the technical term for a co-wife in a polygynous marriage. The prohibition applies only while both sisters are alive — be'ḥayyeiha (in her lifetime). This directly addresses the situation of Jacob's marriages to Leah and Rachel (Gen 29-30), who were biological sisters and produced the conflict that tsarah implies.
18.19: Sexual relations during menstruation (niddah). The prohibition connects the sexual laws with the broader purity system: menstruation generates ritual impurity (Lev 15:19-24), and sexual contact during that time compounds the impurity. The niddah category bridges the sexual ethics of ch. 18 and the bodily-discharge purity laws of ch. 15.
18.20: ve'el-eshet amitekha lo titten shekhovtekha lezara: 'You shall not have sexual relations with your neighbor's wife, defiling yourself with her.' Amitekha (your neighbor/fellow Israelite) — the covenant community term. Lezara (for impurity/defilement) — the sexual violation is framed as a form of contamination parallel to ritual impurity. This is also the seventh commandment (lo tin'af, Exod 20:14), here given in its Levitical Holiness Code formulation.
18.21: umiZar'akha lo-titten leha'avir laMolech: 'You shall not give any of your offspring to be offered to Molech.' Molech (also vocalized Molek) is the deity associated with child sacrifice, practiced in Canaan and apparently sometimes in Israel (2 Kgs 16:3; Jer 32:35). Leha'avir (to cause to pass through) is the technical term for child sacrifice in the OT. velo teḥallel et-shem Elohekha: 'do not profane the name of your God' — child sacrifice is framed as a desecration of YHWH's name, not merely a social atrocity. The insertion of the Molech prohibition within the sexual ethics code reflects its association with fertility-cult worship.
18.22: ve'et-zakar lo tishkav mishkevei isha to'evah hi: 'You shall not lie with a male as with a woman; it is an abomination.' To'evah (abomination/detestable thing) is a strong term of categorical rejection, used elsewhere for idolatry (Deut 7:25) and child sacrifice (Deut 12:31). The verse prohibits male same-sex intercourse in terms drawn from the sexual categories of Lev 18 as a whole (mishkevei isha, literally 'lyings of a woman' = the manner of lying with a woman).
18.23: uvekol-behema lo-titten shekhovtekha letame'ah bah: 'You shall not have sexual relations with any animal, defiling yourself with it.' Tevel (perversion/violation of created order) — the term used for bestiality; it conveys disorder at the level of creation categories, not merely moral transgression. The female parallel is explicit: a woman must not stand before an animal for sexual purposes. Both are tevel — violations of the created order of kinds.
18.24: al-tittame'u bekhol-elleh: 'Do not defile yourselves by any of these things.' Nitme'u (became defiled) applies to the Canaanites — the land's former inhabitants who practiced these things and were expelled by YHWH. The defiling practices are not merely matters of cultural preference; they generated genuine moral-ritual impurity that affected the land itself.
18.25: vatitma ha'arets vaeafqod avonah aleiha vataqqi ha'arets et-yoshveiha: 'The land became defiled; I punished it for its iniquity, and the land vomited out its inhabitants.' The land as a moral agent: vataqqi (vomited out) — the land cannot tolerate sustained moral pollution and expels its inhabitants. This is not mythological; it is the theological explanation of Canaanite displacement. The same threat is directed at Israel in v. 28 — the land has no ethnic loyalty; it vomits out whoever defiles it.
18.26: The obligation falls on Israel and on resident aliens (hager hagar betokham) equally — the sexual prohibitions are not merely Israel's cultural particularity but reflect moral norms applicable to all who live in the land. This extension to the alien is consistent with the Holiness Code's pattern of including non-Israelites in fundamental ethical obligations.
18.27: The Canaanites had committed all these abominations (to'evot) — hence their expulsion. The historical note grounds the prohibition in demonstrated moral-historical consequence: these practices bring divine judgment that results in national displacement. Israel is warned by precedent, not merely by abstract command.
18.28: velo-taqqi ha'arets etkhem betam'akhem otah: 'Lest the land vomit you out when you defile it, as it vomited out the nation that was before you.' The land's moral intolerance is turned as a warning against Israel: identical behavior will bring identical consequences regardless of covenant status. The exile of 722 BCE (Israel) and 586 BCE (Judah) vindicated this warning — the land did indeed 'vomit out' Israel when it persisted in these practices.
18.29: ki kol-asher ya'aseh mikkol-hatto'evot ha'elleh venikh'ru hanaefashot ha'osot: 'For anyone who commits any of these abominations — the persons who do them shall be cut off from among their people.' The karet penalty applies comprehensively across all the listed prohibitions. Karet (being cut off) in its most severe form may mean premature death at divine hands; in less severe applications it means exclusion from the covenant community.
18.30: ushmartem et-mishmarti: 'Keep my charge.' Mishmeret (charge/obligation, what is kept/guarded) — the term for the covenant responsibility of obedience. The closing exhortation frames the entire chapter: Israel is to guard against the practices that caused Canaan's expulsion. Ani YHWH Eloheikhem — the opening refrain closes the chapter, forming an envelope structure around the entire sexual ethics code.

22.1: Chapter 22 continues the regulations for the priestly office, shifting from personal qualifications (ch. 21) to ritual qualifications for eating sacred food and presenting acceptable sacrifices. The chapter operates at two levels: (1) the priestly household's access to the holy portions (qodashim), and (2) the physical integrity of animals brought as offerings. Both sections share the same principle: holiness demands wholeness, and what is given to YHWH must be the best, not the defective or disqualified.
22.2: yinnazru (Niphal imperfect of nazar, 'to separate, consecrate, dedicate'): 'Aaron and his sons shall keep away from the holy things of the people of Israel.' The root nzr gives us both nazir (Nazirite, one who separates himself by vow) and nezer (the high priest's diadem/crown). The same root covers both voluntary consecration and required separation. Here the Niphal (reflexive passive) means the priests must separate themselves — in context, this means while ritually impure they must abstain from the holy offerings. Holiness and impurity are mutually exclusive poles; the priest cannot stand in both simultaneously.
22.3: nitmetah (Niphal of tame): ritual defilement cuts a priest off from the holy food. The text says such a priest will be 'cut off' (nikhretah) from YHWH's presence — the same karet language used for the gravest covenant violations. This reflects the seriousness of holiness-boundary violations even within the priestly system: the remedy is purification and waiting, but there is no shortcut. The priest who eats holy food while impure is not merely ritually irregular — he has treated the holy as common (chillel).
22.4: The list of disqualifying impurities — tzara'at (skin disease), genital discharge (zob), contact with a corpse-contaminated person, semen emission — mirrors the purity laws of chapters 13-15. The common thread is that all these conditions involve either death (corpse, skin disease), reproductive disruption, or bodily emission — all associated with mortality and the body's boundaries. Holiness is associated with life, wholeness, and integrity; these conditions mark the opposite. The solution is time, washing, and waiting for evening — the body's natural restoration cycle.
22.8: nevela (carcass of an animal that died naturally) and trefah (an animal torn by wild beasts) — these two categories are prohibited food for priests, though they could be given to the resident alien or sold to a foreigner (Deut 14:21; contrast with Lev 22:8 prohibiting the priest from eating them). The issue is not the food category in the abstract but the priest's heightened holiness standard. Trefah later becomes the broad term in rabbinic halakha for any non-kosher meat; here it has its original specific meaning of an animal mauled by predators. The priest must not eat what was not killed in the prescribed sacrificial manner.
22.9: chet (sin/penalty) and metu bo (they will die because of it): the phrase 'they shall therefore keep my charge' (shamru et mishmarti) employs the standard custodial language of priestly responsibility. The doubling of the root in 'keep my charge' (mishmeret = 'what is to be kept/guarded') emphasizes the active, ongoing nature of priestly duty. Death as the penalty for mishandling holy things appears also in Numbers 4:15-20 (Kohathites), reinforcing that the Levitical system treated holiness violations as capital matters.
22.10: The category of those permitted to eat the terumah (priestly portion) is carefully defined: zar (outsider, non-priest), toshav (resident/tenant of the priest), and sakhir (hired laborer) are excluded. The contrast with permitted persons (v.11-13) shows the rule is about covenant status and household belonging, not ethnicity: a slave purchased by a priest or one born into his household — bound to the priestly household — may eat. The priestly household is a closed economy of holiness; membership matters more than origin.
22.14: be-shegagah (inadvertently, by mistake) — if a layman eats the holy food by accident, he must restore it at full value (chamishito yosif alav, 'he shall add a fifth to it'). The 20% surcharge appears also in guilt offering law (Lev 5:16) and restitution law (Lev 6:5). The principle: even unintentional violations of the holy have real consequences requiring real remedy. Grace does not mean consequences are erased; it means a path of restoration is provided. The fifth part (chamishit) signals that trespass against the holy incurs more than simple restitution — there is a premium for having encroached.
22.18: tamim (perfect, without blemish, whole): the term used throughout the offering laws for the required quality of sacrificial animals. Its root tmm means 'to be complete, perfect, whole' — the same root gives tom (integrity, wholeness, blamelessness) used of Job's character (Job 1:1) and the psalmist's walk (Ps 15:2). Applied to animals, tamim means without physical defect. Hebrews 9:14 applies the concept directly to Christ: he offered himself 'without blemish' (amōmon, the LXX translation of tamim) to God. The animal regulation of Lev 22:18-25 is the type that explains why Christ's offering could be effective where animal offerings could not.
22.20: lo leratzon yihyeh lachem (it will not be acceptable/pleasing for you): the passive sense of ratzon (pleasure, acceptance, favor) as the criterion for a valid offering. Ratzon comes from the root meaning 'to be pleased with, to accept favorably.' The acceptance of an offering by YHWH is not automatic — it depends on the integrity of what is offered and the integrity of the offerer. Isaiah 1:11-15 shows YHWH rejecting Israel's abundant offerings because the offerers' hands were bloodied — acceptability is not merely a ritual category but a relational one.
22.21: shelem (peace/fellowship offering): the term is from the root shalom (peace, wholeness, completeness). The peace offering is the only sacrifice where the offerer, the priests, and YHWH all receive portions — it is inherently communal. The three subtypes (todah/thanksgiving, neder/vow, nedavah/freewill) appear in ch. 7. The tamim requirement applies here too: lo yihyeh bo mum (there shall be no defect in it). The completeness of the peace offering's animal mirrors the shalom it embodies — communion with a holy God requires wholeness in what is brought.
22.22: The catalog of disqualifying blemishes: blind (iveret), injured (shavurah), maimed (charutz), running sore (yabbelet), scab (garav), scurvy (yallefet). The specificity of the list is important: it is not a general principle but an enumeration, preventing priestly discretion in accepting substandard animals. The purity of the offering system depends on clear, public, verifiable standards. The same principle appears in mal. 1:8 where Malachi condemns the priests of his era for accepting blemished animals — the law was meant to prevent precisely this corruption.
22.24: Prohibition on offering animals with crushed, torn, or cut testicles. The emasculation of animals was common in surrounding cultures for various purposes. The prohibition here may relate to the wholeness-requirement: the animal must be complete, not altered. In the Holiness Code's logic, cutting or crushing represents a deviation from created wholeness. Note that Deut 23:1 extends similar logic to human membership in the assembly: the eunuch is excluded from the assembly (though Isa 56:3-5 reverses this exclusion for eunuchs who keep the Sabbath and hold fast the covenant).
22.25: min ben nechar (from the hand of a foreigner): even sacrifices brought by resident aliens or non-Israelites must meet the tamim standard. This confirms that the blemish laws are not merely about Israelite social purity but about what is intrinsically acceptable to YHWH. The offering standard transcends the identity of the offerer. This aligns with the trajectory toward universal worship: Isaiah 56:7 envisions foreigners whose burnt offerings and sacrifices are accepted on YHWH's altar.
22.27: A newborn animal must remain seven days with its mother before it is eligible for offering. The seven-day waiting period mirrors the circumcision-timing (Lev 12:3) and possibly the completion-of-a-week principle that runs through the creation narrative. The prohibition on killing the mother and her young on the same day (v.28) has parallels in Deut 22:6-7 (the bird's nest law) — a concern for the natural order and against gratuitous destruction of reproductive capacity, reflecting the creation stewardship mandate.
22.28: oto ve-et beno lo tishchatu beyom echad (it and its young you shall not slaughter on the same day): the prohibition on killing parent and offspring the same day. This is one of several Levitical laws that recognize animal family bonds — not by anthropomorphizing animals but by maintaining a certain reverence for the natural order that YHWH created. Maimonides (Guide 3:48) explained it as prevention of cruelty; modern readers see it as ecological wisdom. The underlying logic is consistent with the Holiness Code's concern that Israel not act with callous disregard for creation.
22.29: toda (thanksgiving offering): a subtype of the peace offering in which the motivation is gratitude for a specific act of divine deliverance. The same-day eating rule (v.30) applies: leratzonkhem tizbachu — the urgency of consuming it reflects the immediacy and specificity of the thanksgiving. Psalm 107 is the great thanksgiving liturgy envisioning four types of delivered people bringing their toda; Ps 50:14 calls the toda the true sacrifice YHWH desires. The toda is the offering of relationship and response, not transaction.
22.30: mimacharat lo yinotir (you shall not leave any of it until morning): the time-constraint on the todah offering creates urgency that distinguishes it from the two-day peace offering. The perishability-limit on holy food prevents its commercialization: you cannot stockpile thanksgiving. The pattern also appears in the manna (Exod 16:19-20) — YHWH's provision is meant to be received and consumed in trust, not hoarded. Each day's grace is sufficient for that day.
22.31: ushemar'tem mitzvotai va-asitem otam (You shall keep my commandments and do them): the transition to the Holiness Code's ethical summary. The dual verb — keep (shamar) and do (asah) — is the standard covenant obedience formula: internalize and then act. Ani YHWH (I am YHWH) grounds the command in divine identity, not merely divine authority.
22.32: velo techallelu et shem qodshi veniqdashti betoch benei Yisrael: 'You shall not profane my holy name, but I will be hallowed among the people of Israel.' Chillul hashem (profaning the Name) and kiddush hashem (sanctifying/hallowing the Name) are the two poles of this verse — the most concentrated statement in the Torah of the concept that YHWH's reputation among the nations is at stake in Israel's behavior. The Niphal of qadash — 'I will be hallowed/sanctified' — is passive: YHWH's holiness is revealed through Israel's obedience. Ezekiel extensively develops this theme (Ezek 36:20-23): Israel's exile profaned YHWH's name among the nations; the restoration will vindicate it.
22.33: ani YHWH mekadishchem hamotzi etchem me-eretz Mitzrayim: 'I am YHWH who sanctifies you, who brought you out of the land of Egypt.' The closing formula links sanctification to the Exodus: YHWH's holiness-making activity in Israel is inseparable from his historical redemption of Israel. Israel was not first made holy and then redeemed — redemption came first (Exod 12-14), then the covenant and its holiness demands (Exod 19-Lev). The logic is grace → law, not law → grace.

23.1: Chapter 23 is the Torah's primary festival calendar, presenting the annual cycle of moadim (appointed times/meetings). It organizes Israel's time theologically: the year is structured around YHWH's appointments with his people. The chapter is not merely a religious calendar but a theology of time — every week (Sabbath), every spring (Passover, Firstfruits, Weeks), every fall (Trumpets, Yom Kippur, Sukkot) is claimed as YHWH's territory. The NT reads the festival calendar typologically: Christ is the Passover lamb (1 Cor 5:7), the firstfruits of the dead (1 Cor 15:23), and the Spirit is poured out at Pentecost/Shavuot (Acts 2).
23.2: moadei YHWH (appointed times of YHWH): the plural moadim from the root y'd (to appoint, meet at an appointed time). Moed is the word for 'tent of meeting' (ohel moed) — the tabernacle is itself a moed, a place and time of divine appointment. By calling the festivals moadei YHWH, the text frames them not as Israel's religious observances but as YHWH's invitations: he sets the times, he calls the assembly. mikra qodesh (holy convocation/sacred proclamation): mikra from qara (to call, proclaim) — these assemblies are called assemblies, summoned by divine invitation. The festivals are not merely human celebrations but responses to divine summons.
23.3: shabbat shabbaton (sabbath of rest): the intensive doubled form (shabbaton adds the nominal suffix for emphasis) distinguishes the weekly Sabbath as the foundational moed. The Sabbath heads the list because all the festivals are built on the Sabbath-principle: sacred rest is the paradigm that the annual festivals replicate and elaborate. mikra qodesh recurs — the Sabbath is not merely a rest day but an assembly day, a day of sacred gathering. kol melacha lo taasu (you shall do no work) — the negative form of Sabbath observance; the positive form is the mikra qodesh.
23.4: The transition phrase eleh moadei YHWH (these are the appointed feasts of YHWH) marks the beginning of the annual festival calendar proper, distinguishing the recurring weekly Sabbath (v.3) from the annual appointments. The phrase asher tiqreu otam bemoadam (which you shall proclaim at their appointed time) confirms that proclamation is essential: the festivals do not simply arrive, they are announced. The priestly calendar function included proclaiming (literally calling out, qara) the new moon and festival dates — a calendrical authority that later became significant in rabbinic disputes.
23.5: Pesach laYHWH (Passover of/for YHWH): the 14th of the first month (Nisan/Abib) at twilight (bein ha-arbayim, literally 'between the two evenings'). The Passover lamb was killed at twilight and consumed during the night that followed — the night of the 14th becoming the 15th (Jewish day begins at nightfall). Paul's identification of Christ as 'our Passover lamb who has been sacrificed' (1 Cor 5:7) and John's chronology of the crucifixion on the eve of Passover (John 18:28; 19:14) both operate within this precise calendrical framework.
23.6: Chag haMatzot (Festival of Unleavened Bread): the seven-day feast beginning on the 15th, immediately following Passover. Matzot (unleavened bread) commemorates the hasty exodus (Exod 12:33-34, 39 — no time for the dough to rise). Paul reads the leaven allegorically: 'Cleanse out the old leaven that you may be a new lump, as you really are unleavened. For Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed. Let us therefore celebrate the festival, not with the old leaven, the leaven of malice and evil, but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth' (1 Cor 5:7-8). The seven-day feast creates a week of consecration following the Passover.
23.7: melechet avodah (laborious work, heavy labor): the compound phrase distinguishes forbidden work on festival days from permitted work. Melacha alone (as in the Sabbath command, Exod 20:9-10) means any ordinary work; melechet avodah specifies burdensome/servile labor. This distinction allowed the preparation of food on festival days (Exod 12:16) — not every activity was suspended, only the servile labor associated with ordinary workdays. The rabbis developed this distinction extensively in tractate Yom Tov.
23.10: reishit qetzirchem (the first of your harvest): the wave-sheaf offering of the firstfruits of the barley harvest — the first grain to ripen in the spring. Reishit (first, beginning) carries the same root as Bereshit (In the beginning, Gen 1:1) and connects to the firstfruits theology running through the Torah: the first of everything belongs to YHWH and is given as the pledge that secures the whole harvest. omer (a sheaf or a unit of dry measure): the specific term for the wave-sheaf of barley. 1 Cor 15:23 applies the firstfruits type to the resurrection: 'Christ the firstfruits, then at his coming those who belong to Christ.'
23.11: mimocharat hashabbat (on the day after the Sabbath): the contested phrase that determines when to begin counting the 50 days to Pentecost (Shavuot). Two interpretations dominated in the Second Temple period: (1) the Pharisaic/rabbinic: 'Sabbath' = the Passover high-day Sabbath (the 15th of Nisan), so the count starts on the 16th; (2) the Sadducean/Karaite: 'Sabbath' = the weekly Sabbath within the Passover week, so the count always starts on Sunday, making Shavuot always fall on Sunday. The dispute is still unresolved. If interpretation (2) is correct, the Resurrection (first Sunday after Passover), the Wave Sheaf offering, and Pentecost all fall on Sundays by design — the day after the Sabbath becomes the day of new creation.
23.13: minchah chadasha laYHWH (a new grain offering to YHWH): the wave-sheaf offering includes both the sheaf and a grain offering (minchah) plus a whole burnt offering of a lamb. The grain offering at firstfruits is described as 'new' (chadasha) — it marks the opening of the new harvest year before YHWH. Until this offering is made, the new grain cannot be eaten (v.14). The logic: the first gives permission for the rest. In giving the first to YHWH, the whole harvest is consecrated as given by him.
23.14: ad etzem hayom hazeh (until this very day): the restriction on eating grain from the new harvest until the wave-sheaf offering is made applies universally ('wherever you dwell') — it is not limited to the tabernacle/temple context but is a national calendar discipline. The phrase 'until this very day' marks a permanent statute (chuqqat olam). The theological logic: first acknowledge YHWH as the giver before receiving the gift. The firstfruits offering is an act of faith that what has been given can be given back without loss.
23.15: usefartem lachem (you shall count for yourselves): the counting of the Omer — 49 days from the wave-sheaf to Shavuot. The Omer counting is unique in the Torah in that the counting itself is the commandment, not merely the endpoint. The act of counting each day creates anticipation and intentionality; the 49-day journey from Passover (redemption from Egypt) to Shavuot (giving of the Torah at Sinai, in rabbinic tradition) structures the spiritual journey from liberation to covenant. The Sefirat HaOmer (counting of the Omer) became one of the most prominent Jewish daily practices.
23.16: chamishim yom (fifty days): the number that gives Pentecost its Greek name (pentekostos, fiftieth). The Greek word passed directly into Christian use for the festival of the Spirit's outpouring (Acts 2). The fifty-day structure connects to the Jubilee (50-year cycle) and suggests a pattern of completion-plus-one: seven times seven = 49 (completion), plus the 50th = the day that transcends the cycle. Shavuot/Pentecost is the day after completion — the day of the new gift that follows the full cycle.
23.17: lechem tenufah shetayim (two loaves as a wave offering): the two leavened (chametz) loaves waved at Shavuot are unique — virtually every other grain offering at the altar was unleavened (Lev 2:11). The use of leavened bread may symbolize Israel as it actually is (not idealized) — brought before YHWH with its mixture of good and bad, its fermentation, its imperfection. Some see the two loaves as representing Israel and the Gentiles who would be incorporated at Pentecost/Acts 2 (the reversal of Babel, with people from every nation understanding). The loaves are baked, not merely raw grain — processed, mature harvest brought as an offering.
23.22: The gleaning laws (Lev 19:9-10 repeated) appear in the middle of the Shavuot regulations — an interruption that is actually an integration: the harvest festival must include care for the poor and the stranger. The festival of thanksgiving for YHWH's provision is incomplete without sharing that provision with the vulnerable. The Book of Ruth enacts this law: Boaz's harvest fields at Shavuot-time become the site where Ruth (a Moabite stranger) gleans and is redeemed. Ruth is traditionally read at Shavuot.
23.24: zikhron teruah (a memorial of blowing/alarm): the Feast of Trumpets on the first of the seventh month. Teruah is the loud, broken blast of the shofar (or silver trumpets) — associated with alarm, battle-cry, and proclamation. The term zikhron (memorial, remembrance) raises the question: what is being remembered? The text does not specify; the content of the memorial is left implicit. In rabbinic tradition, Rosh HaShanah (the New Year, which later became the name for this day) is the day of divine remembrance — YHWH remembers his people and inscribes their fate for the year. The shofar blast calls both Israel to attention and YHWH to remembrance.
23.25: mikra qodesh (holy convocation) and lo taashu melechet avodah (you shall not do servile work): the same formula as other festival days. The seventh month (Tishri) contains the most concentrated cluster of holy days: Trumpets (1st), Yom Kippur (10th), Sukkot (15th-21st), and Shemini Atzeret (22nd). The seventh month's density of festivals corresponds to the seventh day's Sabbath — the seventh is the consecrated, climactic unit in the Creator's calendar.
23.27: Yom HaKippurim (Day of Atonements, plural): the plural form (kippurim) may intensify the singular or refer to the multiple atonement acts of the day (for the high priest, for the sanctuary, for the people). The word kippur here is a noun from the verb kipper (to make atonement/to expiate) — Yom Kippur is literally the 'day of the kipper-act.' ve-initem et nafshoteichem (you shall afflict your souls): innui nefesh (soul-affliction) is the physical observance of Yom Kippur — fasting being its principal expression. Nefesh here refers to the whole person through the physical appetite: afflicting the body-self is the Yom Kippur discipline.
23.28: ki yom kippurim hu lekapper aleichem (for it is a Day of Atonement to make atonement for you): the kipper (Piel, make atonement) verb here takes Israel as its object (aleichem). This verse is the only occurrence in Leviticus 23 that uses the atonement language in connection with Yom Kippur — the festival calendar references the sacrifice theology of chapter 16. The prohibition on work (kol melacha) and the fast together constitute the full observance: both abstinence and affliction on the day when the high priest's blood-work atones for the whole covenant community.
23.29: karet penalty for not observing the Yom Kippur fast: 'Every person who is not afflicted in that very day shall be cut off from his people.' Karet (cutting off) is the covenant excision penalty — removal from the community either through death or exclusion. Its application here to the Yom Kippur fast shows that atonement was not merely a priestly ritual done on Israel's behalf without Israel's participation — the people were obligated to participate through the fast. The atonement required active reception, not passive observation.
23.30: vehibadeti et hanefesh hahi (I will destroy that soul/person): in addition to the karet penalty from the community, YHWH himself will 'cut off' the non-observant — divine excision reinforces covenant excision. The doubled sanction (cut off from the people, AND destroyed by YHWH) intensifies the gravity of Yom Kippur. This is one of only a few places where both forms of the karet penalty are stated together. The day is not to be taken lightly.
23.32: shabbat shabbaton hu lachem: the intensive shabbat shabbaton (used also of the weekly Sabbath in v.3 and the Sabbatical year in Lev 25:4) is applied to Yom Kippur — marking it as the Sabbath of Sabbaths among the festival calendar, the supreme rest-day. The fast from sunset to sunset on the 10th of Tishri makes it the only twenty-five-hour Sabbath in the calendar. ve-initem et nafshoteichem repeated: the soul-affliction command bookends the Yom Kippur section (vv.27, 32) for emphasis.
23.33: The Sukkot/Booths festival section (vv.33-44) is the longest in chapter 23, reflecting its importance as the climactic harvest festival. Sukkot runs seven days (15th-21st of Tishri) plus an eighth day solemn assembly (atzeret, v.36). It combines agricultural thanksgiving (end of the harvest year) with historical memorial (the wilderness sojourn). The abundance of Sukkot — freewill offerings, communal celebration, the four species — contrasts with Yom Kippur's austerity five days earlier. Together they form a theological dyad: atonement then celebration, affliction then joy.
23.34: Chag haSukkot (Festival of Booths/Tabernacles): sukkot (booths, temporary shelters) are the defining feature — Israelites dwell in them for seven days to remember the wilderness journey (v.43). The word sukkah appears in various non-festival contexts to mean a temporary shelter or the divine canopy over Israel (Isa 4:6; Amos 9:11 — 'I will raise up the sukkah of David'). The Johannine prologue's 'the Word became flesh and dwelt (eskēnōsen, tabernacled) among us' (John 1:14) may deliberately echo Sukkot language — the Incarnation as the ultimate sukkah.
23.36: atzeret (solemn assembly, closing assembly): the eighth day of Sukkot — not technically part of the seven-day Sukkot festival but a concluding 'gathering' day. The root atzar (to restrain, hold back, close) suggests the atzeret is the day that closes the festival season — the final day before returning to ordinary time. John 7:37-38 sets Jesus's proclamation of living water on 'the last day, the great day of the feast' — likely the Hoshana Rabbah (seventh day of Sukkot) or the Shemini Atzeret (eighth day), when water-libation ceremonies were performed in Second Temple practice.
23.37: eleh moadei YHWH: the summary formula restates the calendar's structure. The variety of offerings required at each festival (olah, minchah, zevach, nesakhim — burnt offering, grain offering, sacrifice, and drink offering) shows that the full range of Israel's offering vocabulary was engaged throughout the year. No single offering type alone served the full relational range; the calendar pressed all of them into use at their appropriate times.
23.40: peri etz hadar (fruit of a splendid/beautiful tree, traditionally the etrog/citron), kaparot temarim (branches of palm trees, lulav), anaf etz avot (boughs of leafy/thick trees, traditionally myrtle), and arvei nachal (willows of the brook): the four species carried during Sukkot. Hadar (splendid, glorious) appears elsewhere of YHWH's majesty (Ps 104:1) and of human dignity (Lev 19:32 'honor the face of the old man'). The binding of the four species into the lulav bundle for waving became one of the most distinctive Jewish festival rituals. Nehemiah 8:15 describes Ezra's restoration of Sukkot including gathering these specific branches.
23.41: chuqqat olam leDoroteichem (a perpetual statute for your generations): the permanent, trans-generational character of the Sukkot ordinance. The chok (statute) distinguishes laws whose reasons are not stated from mishpatim (judgments/laws) whose rationale is implied by justice. Sukkot is given as a chok — its observance is commanded before its rationale is given (which comes in vv.42-43). Obedience precedes explanation; trust in the lawgiver precedes understanding of the law.
23.42: bashsukkot teshvu shivat yamim (you shall dwell in booths seven days): the dwelling (yashav) is to be actual habitation, not merely decoration. The seven-day duration mirrors the creation week. kol ha-ezrach beYisrael (all who are native Israelites): the commandment is universal within Israel — not just the wealthy who can build elaborately or the priests, but everyone. The leveling democratic character of Sukkot: for seven days all Israelites live in identical temporary shelters, recalling their ancestors' common precariousness in the wilderness.
23.43: lema'an yeda'u doroteichem ki basukkot hoshavti et-benei Yisrael behotzi'i otam me-eretz Mitzrayim: 'so that your generations may know that I made the people of Israel dwell in booths when I brought them out of the land of Egypt.' The lema'an yeda'u (so that they may know) pattern is the knowledge-of-YHWH formula: ritual acts are designed to produce generational knowledge of YHWH's identity and saving acts. The wilderness booths were not a choice but a condition — temporary, exposed, dependent on YHWH for provision. Annual booth-dwelling re-enacts that dependence, preventing amnesia of the original neediness from which YHWH redeemed Israel.
23.44: vayedabber Moshe et moadei YHWH el-benei Yisrael: 'Thus Moses declared to the people of Israel the appointed feasts of YHWH.' The closing formula for the festival calendar confirms that Moses is the mediator who communicates YHWH's appointed times to Israel. The moadei YHWH (YHWH's appointments) bookend the chapter — the festivals are YHWH's, not Israel's invention. Israel's role is reception, proclamation, and observance; the calendar is given, not constructed.

24.1: The divine-speech formula vayedabber YHWH el-Moshe lemor introduces two distinct units: the lamp and bread instructions (vv. 2-9) and the blasphemer case (vv. 10-23). The juxtaposition is structural — the perpetual presence of light and bread in the sanctuary (the ongoing covenant relationship) is immediately followed by a case-law that tests the covenant's protection of the divine Name.
24.2: Shemen zayit zakh katit (zakh, H2134; katit, H3795): 'pure pressed olive oil.' Zakh = clear, pure, unadulterated; katit = beaten/crushed in a mortar (not extracted by pressing the whole olive, but from olives beaten to release oil without bitter residue). Ner tamid (tamid, H8548): 'perpetual/continual lamp' — tamid means unbroken continuity, not necessarily a flame never extinguished but one always maintained in attendance. The same adjective qualifies the daily burnt offering ('olat ha-tamid) and the bread of presence — it marks the perpetual character of covenant worship.
24.3: Lifne YHWH tamid: 'before YHWH continually.' Aaron tends the lamp from evening to morning — the light burns through the night (when natural light is absent) before YHWH. Chuqat 'olam (H2708+H5769): perpetual statute — the same formula used for the Sabbath (Ex 31:16) and the fat-prohibition (Lev 3:17). The lamp's maintenance is not merely practical (lighting the sanctuary) but relational — a covenant obligation to keep the light of YHWH's presence burning.
24.4: Menorah ha-tahor: the pure (gold) lampstand. The lampstand's specification as 'pure' (tahor) refers to the refined gold of its construction (Ex 25:31). Aaron tends the lamps lifne YHWH tamid — the repeated phrase from v. 3 emphasizes that the lamp-tending is always before YHWH, not merely in the sacred space. The orientation of the priest's work is toward YHWH, not toward the congregation or any human audience.
24.5: Solet (H5560) fine flour for the bread; shetim 'esronim (two-tenths of an ephah) per loaf — approximately 4.4 liters of flour each. The twelve loaves correspond to the twelve tribes: the bread represents the totality of Israel's covenant community before YHWH. Challot (H2471): loaves or flat-cakes. The specific quantity per loaf and the total of twelve encode the theological numerology of the covenant: completeness of the people before the complete God.
24.6: Shisha ha-madreg: 'six in a row.' Two rows of six on the shulchan ha-tahor (the pure gold table, Ex 25:23-30). The table and bread together constitute the lechem ha-panim (H3899+H6440): 'bread of the face/presence' — bread that stands in the face-to-face space before YHWH. The term panim (face) echoes the promise of Presence in Ex 33:14-15 (panai yelekhu = 'my face will go'). The bread at YHWH's face is the covenant meal held in perpetuity.
24.7: Levonah zakkah (H3828+H2134): pure frankincense — a costly aromatic resin. It is placed on each row as an 'azkara (H234): memorial/representative portion.
