The frankincense is burned as the reah nihoah for YHWH; the bread itself is not burned but presented and then given to the priests (v. 9). The 'azkara structure here (frankincense = the burned representative) means the bread-rows are acceptable to YHWH through the frankincense memorial even though the bread itself goes to the priests.
24.8: Beyom ha-Shabbat: every Sabbath the bread is renewed — fresh loaves replace the previous week's. The renewal cycle on the Sabbath connects the bread of presence to the covenant's temporal rhythm: the bread is not a one-time offering but a perpetually renewed covenant act. Berit 'olam (H1285+H5769): 'covenant forever' — this specific phrase, linking the bread to the eternal covenant, elevates the showbread from a food-ritual to a covenant-sign. Compare Gen 9:16 (rainbow), 17:7 (circumcision), Ex 31:16 (Sabbath).
24.9: Qodesh qodashim: 'most holy' — the bread, though not burned, carries the highest holiness grade when eaten by the priests. Aaron and his sons eat it bimqom qadosh (in a holy place). Me'ishei YHWH chok 'olam: from YHWH's food offerings, a perpetual portion. The priestly consumption of the bread of presence is the completion of the covenant meal: YHWH presents (the bread before his face), and the priests receive and eat. The meal is conducted between YHWH and his mediating household.
24.10: Ben isha Israelit and ish Mitzri (Egyptian father): a man of mixed ancestry. This narrative interruption in the legal material highlights that the laws which follow apply across ethnic lines. The legal case emerges from a fight (vayinnatzu) in the camp — the blasphemy happened in a social conflict context, not in a cultic one. The blasphemer's identity (mixed heritage) sets up the ruling in v. 22 that the same law applies to the foreigner and the native-born.
24.11: Vayiqov (naqav, H5344): the specific verb for blasphemy of the Name — 'to pierce, to bore through, to profane by explicit use.' Naqav differs from the more common galah (to uncover/profane) or hillel (to desecrate): it implies a direct, specific use of the Name in a degrading or magical-manipulative way. Wayyeqalel (qalal, H7043): 'to curse, to treat as light/contemptible' — the opposite of kaved (to honor/weigh heavily). The blasphemy is both specific name-use (naqav) and contemptuous devaluation (qalal).
24.12: Lifros lahem al pi YHWH: 'until the matter was made clear to them through YHWH's mouth.' The custody pending divine ruling reflects the legal system's acknowledgment of a hard case: the community knows the act is serious but does not yet know the prescribed response for this specific crime. The consultation model — bringing exceptional cases to YHWH for ruling — parallels the daughters of Zelophehad (Num 27) and the Passover exception (Num 9).
24.13: Second divine-speech formula (vayedabber YHWH) within the chapter — signaling that the following ruling on blasphemy is a direct divine legal decision, not Moses's inference from existing law. The narrative structure (case → consultation → divine ruling → execution → legal principle → execution) is the standard form for midrash-type legal development in the Torah.
24.14: The execution procedure for blasphemy: vesamkhu khol ha-shom'im et yedeihem al rosho — 'all who heard lay their hands on his head.' The witnesses become the first executioners — the laying of hands by witnesses reverses the semikhah of sacrifice (where the offerer's hand identifies with the animal). Here the witnesses' hands transfer the guilt back to the blasphemer before the community executes judgment. Venirgemuhu (H7275, H7275): stoning by the whole congregation.
24.15: Ish ish ki yeqalel Elohav venasa cheto: 'Any person who curses his God shall bear his guilt.' The formulation 'ish 'ish ('any man, any man' — an emphatic repetition in Hebrew law) universalizes the principle to every person without exception. Venasa cheto: 'shall bear his guilt/sin' — the language of guilt-bearing used across Leviticus for violations that fall on the transgressor himself. The passive form emphasizes that the guilt does not disappear but remains attached to the person.
24.16: Niqev shem YHWH mot yumat: 'Whoever pierces/blasphemes the name of YHWH shall be put to death.' The same naqav verb from v. 11. The death penalty applies to ger (resident alien) and ezrach (native-born) equally — the only verse in the Torah that explicitly applies the blasphemy law to non-Israelites. The equal application reflects the Name's absolute status: YHWH's name is not protected as Israel's tribal property but as the identity of the Creator God of all people.
24.17: Ki yakkeh kol nefesh adam mot yumat: 'Whoever strikes down any human being (nefesh adam) shall be put to death.' Nefesh (H5315): life-self, person, soul — here in the compound nefesh adam emphasizing the full personhood of the one killed. The term adam (human being, from adamah = ground) makes the protection of human life universal: not 'any Israelite' but 'any human being.' The life-protection law is grounded in the image of God (tselem Elohim, Gen 9:6) in which all humanity shares.
24.18: Makkeh nefesh behemah — killing an animal. Yeshallemenah nefesh tachat nefesh: 'he shall restore it — life for life.' The nefesh tachat nefesh formula applies here to animal restitution (monetary compensation, not execution), establishing that the same phrase can function at both capital (human) and property (animal) levels. The principle: equivalent compensation is owed; the nature of the victim determines the nature of the compensation.
24.19: Mum be'amito ken ye'aseh lo: 'as he has done to his neighbor, so it shall be done to him.' The lex talionis formula — introduced here before its detailed enumeration in v. 20. This general statement of equivalent retribution is the principle underlying all the specific applications that follow. Mum (H3971): a blemish, defect, or disfigurement — the same word used for physical disqualification from the priesthood (Lev 21:17-23). The legal concept of mum maps from the sacrificial system to personal injury law.
24.20: Shever tachat shever, 'ayin tachat 'ayin, shen tachat shen: 'fracture for fracture, eye for eye, tooth for tooth.' The lex talionis triad (also in Ex 21:24; Deut 19:21) is a principle of proportional justice, not a license for personal revenge. The formula limits punishment to the equivalent of the injury done — no more. In the ANE context, where wealthy offenders could simply pay fines and powerful ones could exact disproportionate retribution, the lex talionis was a leveling law: the punishment must fit the crime, regardless of relative social status.
24.21: The verse summarizes and connects both law categories: animal killing → restitution; human killing → execution. The juxtaposition (behemah... adam) makes the hierarchy explicit: human life carries capital weight; animal life carries restitution weight. The same nefesh term applies to both (both have life-essence), but the tselem Elohim of human beings uniquely grounds the capital response.
24.22: Mishpat echad yihyeh lachem: 'You shall have one law for you.' Lager kaeezrach: for the ger (resident alien) and the ezrach (native-born) alike. The legal egalitarianism of the Mosaic code — one standard across social and ethnic categories — is a defining feature repeatedly insisted upon (cf. Ex 12:49; Num 15:16,29). The covenant community is not a tribal law-zone where insiders are protected differently from outsiders; the law's authority extends to all who dwell in YHWH's land.
24.23: The blasphemer is brought outside the camp and stoned — Moses and the Israelites ka'asher tsivah YHWH et-Moshe ('as YHWH commanded Moses'). The narrative closure formula emphasizes exact obedience to the divine ruling: no deviation, no mercy plea. The legal case serves as a paradigm for all subsequent blasphemy cases: the procedure established here is the normative precedent. The outside-the-camp execution removes the guilty person and the pollution of blasphemy from the covenant community.

25.1: Behar Sinai: 'on Mount Sinai' — the sabbatical and jubilee laws are explicitly anchored at Sinai. This geographical grounding is unusual for laws given in Leviticus (where the default setting is the tent of meeting). The Sinai reference underscores that the land-rest laws are not agricultural policy but covenant obligations carrying the same authority as the Ten Words. The opening of the Mishnah tractate Avot — 'Moses received Torah from Sinai' — cites this verse as its proof-text.
25.2: Shabbat ha-aretz la-YHWH: 'the land shall have a sabbath to YHWH' — the land itself is the subject of sabbath observance, not merely its human inhabitants. Shabbat (H7676): cessation/rest. The land sabbath (shemittah) extends the seventh-day creation pattern (Gen 2:2-3) to the agricultural cycle: as YHWH rested from creation, the land rests from cultivation. The land belongs to YHWH (v. 23 ki li ha-aretz); its sabbath is owed to him, not chosen by the farmer.
25.3: Six years of active cultivation — sowing, pruning, gathering. The six-year agricultural cycle mirrors the six-day creation week, with the seventh given to YHWH. The enumeration of the three activities (tizra... tizmor... asafta = sow, prune, gather) covers the full range of agricultural labor. The positive description of productive labor in the six years sets up the contrast: not sloth but trust is demanded in the seventh.
25.4: Shabbat shabbaton la-YHWH (shabbat shabbaton, H7676+H7677): 'a complete sabbath rest to YHWH' — the double noun formation is the superlative or intensive: an absolute, total rest. The same intensive form is used for Yom Kippur (Lev 16:31; 23:32). Shemittah (H8057): the sabbatical year; from shamat (H8058): to let drop, release, desist. Shamat is also used in Deut 15:1-3 for the remission of debts in the seventh year: the same release principle applies to both land and debt.
25.5: Sephiach (H5599): the 'aftergrowth' — grain that grows spontaneously from seeds left from the previous harvest, without deliberate sowing. Karmekha ha-nazir (nazir, H5139 in agricultural sense): your 'unpruned vine' — the same root as the Nazirite (nazir = set apart/let grow unrestrained). In the sabbatical year the vine is left untended, 'set apart,' as the Nazirite lets his hair grow without cutting. The sabbatical year makes the land itself a kind of Nazirite — set apart from human management.
25.6: Shabbat ha-aretz lachem leokhlah: 'the sabbath produce of the land shall be food for you.' The sabbatical year's spontaneous produce is not wasted — it becomes shared food: for the owner, male and female servants, hired workers (sakir), resident aliens (toshav), and strangers. The sabbatical year converts private agricultural yield into shared communal provision. The land owner relinquishes exclusive claim to the seventh year's produce; it belongs to all who dwell in the land.
25.7: The land's sabbatical produce also feeds the livestock (behemtekha) and the wild animals (chayat ha-aretz). The inclusion of wild animals is remarkable: the benefits of the sabbatical year extend beyond the covenant community to the non-domesticated creation. This ecological dimension of the sabbatical mirrors the Sabbath command in Ex 23:12 ('so that your ox and donkey may rest') and the Noahic covenant's breadth (Gen 9:10 — 'every living creature').
25.8: Veasafta lekha sheva shabbetot shanim: 'You shall count for yourself seven sabbaths of years — seven times seven years.' The seven-times-seven counting produces forty-nine; the fiftieth year is the jubilee. The same arithmetic appears in the counting for Shavuot (Lev 23:15-16: seven sabbaths + one day = fifty). The jubilee's fifty-year cycle is the macro-scale counterpart to Pentecost's fifty-day cycle: both are completions beyond the perfect number seven, adding one to arrive at a new beginning.
25.9: Bishvi'i bahodesh ha-asiri beyom ha-kippurim: on the tenth day of the seventh month — Yom Kippur — the yovel is inaugurated. The jubilee begins on the Day of Atonement, not on the new year: freedom (deror) is proclaimed after atonement is made. Veha'avarta shofar teru'ah: sound the shofar of great noise throughout all your land. The jubilee is announced by sound — not a written decree but a trumpet blast heard everywhere. The shofar (qeren ha-yovel, the ram's horn) is the instrument that gives the year its name.
25.10: Yovel (H3104): 'jubilee' — likely from yobel (ram) referring to the ram's horn used to announce it, or possibly from a root meaning 'to carry/flow.' Deror (H1865): 'freedom, liberty, release' — a rare and powerful word (Isa 61:1 'to proclaim freedom to the captives' uses this exact term). The jubilee proclamation is: veniqatem deror ba-aretz lekhol yosheveha — 'you shall proclaim freedom throughout the land to all its inhabitants.' Every person returns to their ancestral property; every Israelite debt-slave goes free. The jubilee is the structural undoing of accumulated inequality.
25.11: Yovel hi: 'it is the jubilee.' The fiftieth year is qodesh (holy) — set apart, like the Sabbath itself. No sowing, no reaping of aftergrowth, no harvesting of the untended vine. The jubilee year adds to the sabbatical year's prohibitions the dimension of universal return: it is not merely an agricultural rest but a social reset. The land's rest and the social reset happen simultaneously, bound together in the single concept of yovel.
25.12: Me'asher ha-sadeh tochalenu: 'you may eat what it produces directly from the field.' The jubilee's produce is eaten directly (al pi hasadeh) without processing, storage, or commerce. The immediacy of eating from the field without economic mediation is a return to pre-agricultural intimacy with the land — reminiscent of the garden (Gen 2:9 'every tree that is pleasant to eat'). The jubilee year's food-access pattern reverses the post-Eden labor curse (Gen 3:17-19) at least temporarily.
25.13: Bishnat ha-yovel ha-zo tashuvu ish el achuzato: 'In this jubilee year each of you shall return to his own property.' Achuzah (H272): ancestral property, the holding given by YHWH to each family at the original distribution of the land (Num 26-36; Josh 13-21). The return to the achuzah is not merely a property transfer but a restoration of the original covenant allocation: the jubilee resets the land back to YHWH's initial apportionment, undoing all subsequent alienations.
25.14: Lo tonu ish et achiv: 'Do not oppress one another.' Yanah (H3238): to oppress, to deal wrongfully with, to take advantage of — a term particularly associated with economic exploitation. The land-sale law is reframed as an anti-oppression principle: any transaction involving land near the jubilee must account for the remaining years until restoration. Overcharging (many years left = high price; few years = low price) is the only legitimate pricing principle; to charge otherwise is economic oppression.
25.15: Bemispar shanim acharei ha-yovel tiqneh me'et amitekha: purchase according to the number of years remaining after the jubilee. The jubilee transforms all land sales into time-limited leases: what is actually purchased is not the land but the harvests remaining until the next jubilee. The Hebrew economic concept is that YHWH's land cannot be permanently alienated — it belongs to him (v. 23) and returns to his original allocation at the jubilee. 'Selling land' is linguistically inaccurate in this system; you sell harvest rights.
25.16: Lafi rov ha-shanim tarbeh mikhnato velafi mi'ut ha-shanim tamit mikhnato: 'according to more years increase the price; according to fewer years decrease it.' The variable pricing of land based on jubilee proximity is economically equivalent to a lease: a house rented for 20 years costs more than one rented for 5 years. The law forces this temporal accounting into every land transaction, preventing the pretense that land can be permanently sold and thereby permanently concentrated in wealthy hands.
25.17: Lo tonu ish et amito veyareta me-Elohekha: 'Do not oppress one another, but fear your God.' The prohibition is grounded not in a contractual ethic but in yir'at Elohim (H3374+H430): the fear of God. Yir'at Elohim in Leviticus (here; 19:14,32; 25:36,43) is the consistent motivating ground for ethics that cannot be externally enforced: God sees what human courts cannot — inner intentions, covert overcharging, hidden coercion. The fear of God fills the gaps where human law has no reach.
25.18: Va'asitem et chuqotai ve'et mishpatai tishmevu: 'Do my statutes and keep my rules.' The sabbatical/jubilee laws are part of the covenant obligations whose keeping brings lavetach (security) in the land. The conditional promise ('do this → live securely') embeds the land's wellbeing in covenant faithfulness. Sabbatical observance is not merely ecological rest but covenant trust: trusting YHWH to provide in the seventh year is the concrete test of whether the covenant relationship is functional.
25.19: Venatna ha-aretz piryah: 'the land will give its fruit.' The responsive land — a personified agent in Leviticus's theology — yields or withholds according to the covenant's condition. Compare Lev 26:3-5 (obey → land yields) and 26:20 (disobey → land withholds). The land itself is embedded in the covenant as a responsive party: it is YHWH's land (v. 23), and it responds to whether YHWH's laws are kept on it.
25.20: Vekhi tomervu: 'And if you ask' — the anticipated objection to the sabbatical year. The question 'what will we eat in the seventh year if we do not sow or gather?' is the practical test of faith: the sabbatical year asks for a full year of agricultural trust in YHWH's provision. The law anticipates the objection (as it anticipates the Passover question in Ex 12:26 and the son's question in Deut 6:20) — the system is designed to generate the question so that the answer can teach.
25.21: Vetziviti et birkati lachem bashanah hashishit: 'I will command my blessing on you in the sixth year.' YHWH's advance provision for the sabbatical year: a triple yield in the sixth year that carries through the seventh and eighth until the ninth year's harvest arrives. The provision is not natural agricultural abundance but divinely commanded blessing (birkati). The sabbatical system requires a prior act of divine generosity that makes the seventh-year trust feasible; the law does not demand trust without providing the means.
25.22: The precise arithmetic of the provision: the sixth year's crop feeds from the eighth year's sowing until the ninth year's harvest. The triple-yield provision spans three calendar years, bridging the gap created by the sabbatical year's non-sowing. The specificity of the timeline (sixth → seventh → eighth → ninth) shows that the sabbatical system was designed with awareness of the agricultural calendar's gaps and was guaranteed adequate provision at each transition.
25.23: Veha-aretz lo timmakher litsmitut ki li ha-aretz: 'The land shall not be sold permanently, for the land is mine.' This is the theological ground of the entire jubilee system: ki li ha-aretz — YHWH's ownership claim on the land of Canaan is absolute and permanent. Ki gerim vetoshavim atem imadi: 'you are but aliens and sojourners with me.' Israel's tenure in the land is as resident aliens with YHWH as the true landowner — the identical status Israel held in Egypt (gerim = resident aliens), now applied to their relationship to YHWH in their own land.
25.24: Uge'ulah titenu la-aretz: 'you shall provide redemption for the land' throughout all the land you hold as a possession. Ge'ulah (H1353): redemption — from ga'al (H1350, the kinsman-redeemer verb). The right of redemption is embedded in every land transaction from the outset: no land sale in Israel is final, because redemption rights are permanently attached to every piece of land. The law does not merely permit redemption but requires that its possibility be built into every transaction.
25.25: Im dash achikha: 'if your brother becomes poor.' The poor Israelite is consistently addressed as ach (brother) in ch. 25 (vv. 25,35,36,39,47) — a relational vocabulary that frames poverty relief as a family obligation. Go'alo ha-qarov elav (go'el, H1350): 'his nearest kinsman-redeemer.' The go'el is the closest male relative who has the right (and obligation) to restore lost property, redeem a debt-slave relative, and avenge blood. The institution of the go'el is one of the most theologically loaded in the OT: YHWH himself is described as Israel's go'el (Isa 41:14; 44:6; Job 19:25).
25.26: Ve'ish ki lo yihyeh lo go'el: 'If a man has no one to redeem for him' — the case of the man without a kinsman-redeemer available. The law provides a fallback: self-redemption if the original seller becomes financially capable. The progressive cascade of options (go'el first; then self-redemption; then jubilee) ensures that alienated property returns to its original family eventually — the jubilee is the absolute backstop when all other redemption mechanisms fail.
25.27: Vichishav et shne mimkaro: calculation of years since the sale to determine the refund amount. The sell-back price is the purchase price minus the value of years already used — the elapsed harvests are deducted. This actuarial calculation at every potential redemption point requires that the original sale price be documented and remembered. The redemption system demands financial transparency and precise accounting across potentially decades.
25.28: If the seller cannot afford to buy back, the property remains with the buyer until the jubilee (yovel), when it goes out free — veyatsa bayovel. The jubilee serves as the ultimate economic reset mechanism: what human poverty and failed redemption could not restore, the jubilee automatically restores. The fiftieth year is the covenant's structural guarantee that no Israelite family loses its land permanently regardless of economic misfortune.
25.29: Ish ki yimkor beit moshav ir chomah: 'if a man sells a dwelling house in a walled city.' The urban exception: houses in walled cities have a one-year redemption window after which the sale becomes permanent (v. 30). The walled-city exception distinguishes between agricultural land (which embeds tribal covenant identity and cannot be permanently alienated) and urban real estate (which is more fungible). The jubilee system protects the covenant foundation — ancestral agricultural land — more strongly than urban property.
25.30: If the house is not redeemed within the calendar year, lo yetse bayovel: it does not go out in the jubilee — it remains permanently with the buyer. The walled-city exception from jubilee release reflects a recognition that urban property markets function differently from tribal land tenure. The permanent sale of urban housing is permitted because such houses are not part of the covenant land-distribution that the jubilee is designed to protect.
25.31: Batei ha-chatzerim: 'houses of the open villages' (without surrounding walls). These are treated like open-field land (al sadeh ha-aretz yechashev) — redeemable at any time and released in the jubilee. The village/walled-city distinction is a legal category based on defensibility and market integration: villages function economically as extensions of the agricultural land system; walled cities participate in trade networks with different alienation patterns.
25.32: Arei ha-Leviyim: the Levitical cities. The Levites received no ancestral land-share (Num 18:20-24; Josh 13:14) — their possession within Israel is the 48 designated cities. Their houses in those cities carry permanent redemption rights: a Levite can always buy back a sold house, and the houses in Levitical cities always return in the jubilee. The Levites' unique land-status (no tribal territory; only city allotments) requires special jubilee provisions to protect their right to their only fixed possession.
25.33: Even if a Levite's house is purchased by a non-Levite, it returns in the jubilee because the Levitical cities are achuztam betokh bne Yisrael: 'their possession among the Israelites.' The jubilee's return mechanism ensures that the Levitical city allotments remain in Levitical tenure permanently — the cities that serve as YHWH's mediators' bases cannot be permanently absorbed into tribal territories.
25.34: Uve'migrash areichem lo yimmakher: 'the open land around their cities may not be sold at all.' The migrash (H4054): the pastureland surrounding Levitical cities (Num 35:1-8) — its size was specifically specified in the original land allocation. This pastureland is categorically excluded from the sale system entirely: not merely subject to redemption and jubilee, but unsaleable in any year. The Levites' grazing land is permanently YHWH's provision for his servants.
25.35: Im dash achikha umata yado imakh: 'If your brother becomes poor and his hand falters beside you.' Vehechezaqta bo: 'you shall support him' — the verb chazaq (H2388) means to strengthen, to hold firm, to grasp. The poor Israelite's hand 'faltering' (mutt = slipping, tottering) is met by the community's holding hand. The imagery is physical: one person's grip weakens; another grabs it. Ger vetoshav: even resident aliens qualify for this support — the solidarity extends across ethnic categories within the covenant community.
25.36: Al tiqqach me'itto neshekh vetarbith: 'do not take interest or profit from him.' Neshekh (H5392): interest — from nashakh (H5391): 'to bite.' The image of interest as biting suggests predatory extraction: each payment-period the debt 'bites' the debtor more. Tarbith (H8635): increase, profit — from rabah = to multiply. The pairing of neshekh (interest on money) and tarbith (profit on goods) covers all forms of lending-for-gain to the poor brother. Veyareta me-Elohekha: fear your God — YHWH sees the exploitative lending that courts cannot detect.
25.37: Et kasphekha lo titen lo beneshekh vebemarkheit lo titen lo letarbith: no interest on money, no profit on food. The dual prohibition — money-lending and food-lending — covers the two most basic resources: liquidity and subsistence. Lending food at profit to a starving neighbor extracts maximum value from their desperation. The prohibition names both because both would occur in practice. Deuteronomy 23:19-20 extends the prohibition to any kind of loan to a fellow Israelite while permitting it with foreigners.
25.38: Ani YHWH Eloheikhem asher hotzeti etchem me'eretz Mitzrayim: 'I am YHWH your God who brought you out of the land of Egypt.' The Exodus-grounding formula provides the theological foundation for the interest prohibition: YHWH rescued Israel from economic exploitation (Egyptian slave labor); to exploit a poor fellow-Israelite with predatory lending contradicts the community's founding experience. The character of YHWH as liberator grounds all obligations to the economically vulnerable.
25.39: Ve'im dash achikha imakh umikhar lakh: 'If your brother becomes poor and sells himself to you' — debt slavery as the extreme of poverty: the person himself becomes the collateral. Lo ta'avod bo avodat eved: 'do not make him work as a slave.' The Israelite debt-laborer must not be treated as a chatsi (permanent property-slave) but as a sakir vetoshav (a hired worker and resident alien). The distinction is categorical: same labor; different relationship; different dignity.
25.40: Keshakir ketoshav yihyeh imakh: 'He shall be with you as a hired worker and a resident alien.' The hired worker (sakir, H7916) and resident alien (toshav, H8453) are free persons who work for wages or lease arrangements — not slaves. The Israelite debt-laborer retains the legal personhood of a free contractor even while working off a debt. 'Ad shenat ha-yovel: his service ends at the jubilee — the outermost limit of any Israelite's bonded labor.
25.41: Veyatsa me'immakh hu uvanov: 'He goes free from you — he and his children with him.' The jubilee release includes the debt-laborer's children: the family unit is freed together. The family-inclusive release prevents the perpetuation of servitude into the next generation — children born into debt-service are not thereby born into permanent servitude. Veshav el mishpachto: return to his family; vel'achuzat avotav: and to his ancestral property. The jubilee restores three things: freedom, family, and land.
25.42: Ki avadai hem asher hotzeti otam me'eretz Mitzrayim: 'For they are my servants, whom I brought out of the land of Egypt.' YHWH's prior claim on every Israelite prevents any other person's permanent ownership of them. The Exodus is the theological ground for the Israelite's inalienable freedom: having been liberated by YHWH, no Israelite can be permanently enslaved to a human master. Lo yimmakeru mimkarat eved: 'they shall not be sold as slaves' — the sale of a human being as permanent property is prohibited for any Israelite.
25.43: Lo tirdeh vo befarekh: 'You shall not rule over him with ruthlessness.' Parekhk (H6531): crushing/crushing labor — the same word used for Egypt's treatment of Israel in Ex 1:13-14 ('they made the Israelites work with parekhk'). The term is a loaded one: to treat an Israelite debt-laborer with parekhk is to become Pharaoh. The Exodus narrative is the implicit reference text for every labor-relations law in Leviticus 25; the memory of Egyptian oppression is the standard against which all Israelite master-servant relations are measured.
25.44: Ve'avdekha va'amatekha asher yihyu lakh: foreign slaves may be acquired from surrounding nations. The distinction between permanent foreign slavery (permitted) and permanent Israelite debt-service (prohibited) reflects the covenant community's unique status rather than a universal social ethics. The jubilee system is specifically a covenant institution for the covenant people — it does not purport to be a global economic program. The Mosaic law's protections are strongest for those within the covenant community and progressively weaker for those further outside it.
25.45: Uvetemishpachot ha-ger ha-toshav: and from the children of the resident aliens who sojourn among you — the second generation of resident aliens is also eligible for permanent purchase. Ger toshav (H1616+H8453): the resident alien — a person dwelling permanently within Israel without the full covenant status of a native Israelite. The graduated system of persons (native Israelite / resident alien / foreign sojourner / outside national) maps onto graduated degrees of economic protection.
25.46: Vehitnahaltem otam livneikhem: 'You may bequeath them to your children as an inheritance.' Foreign slaves may be passed through inheritance as permanent property, unlike Israelite debt-laborers who go free at jubilee. Va'acheikhem bne Yisrael lo yirdeh ish be'achiv befarekh: 'but over your brothers, the Israelites, you shall not rule harshly' — the parekhk prohibition applies categorically to Israelite-on-Israelite relations. The verse thus establishes a two-tier system: permanent servitude for foreign slaves; limited, humanely constrained labor for Israelites.
25.47: Ve'im tasig yad ger vetoshav imakh: if the resident alien grows rich while the Israelite beside him becomes poor, and the Israelite sells himself to the resident alien. The scenario is socially inverted from the norm: the native becomes the alien's dependent. Even in this unusual situation the same redemption rights (ge'ulah) apply: the Israelite's kinsmen may redeem him, and the jubilee remains the ultimate backstop.
25.48: Acharei nimmakhar ge'ulah tihyeh lo: 'after he is sold he retains the right of redemption.' The right of redemption is not forfeited by the act of selling oneself — it is a permanent, inalienable legal right attached to every Israelite's person. Ge'ulah (H1353): the right and obligation of kinship-redemption. Even the most desperate economic act (self-sale) does not extinguish the kinship-solidarity mechanism.
25.49: Dodo o ven-dodo yig'alenu: his uncle or uncle's son may redeem him; or any close relative from his family (mishpachto) may redeem him. The kinship network of redemption expands outward through the family tree: brother → uncle → cousin → more distant relative → self-redemption. The graduated kinship cascade ensures that the redemption obligation falls on the closest available relative first, maximizing the chance that redemption actually occurs before the jubilee. The theology of the go'el as the one who comes to the aid of a helpless relative is one of the OT's richest: Ruth 4 (Boaz as go'el), Job 19:25 (YHWH as go'el).
25.50: Vichishav im qoneihu: 'he shall reckon with his buyer' — a financial calculation from the year of sale to the jubilee. The self-sale price is divided by the years of service, and the remaining years' value is the redemption price. The actuarial precision required maintains financial fairness in both directions: the buyer is not defrauded by early redemption; the seller's family is not gouged by an excessive buyout price. The law creates a formula rather than leaving redemption to negotiation.
25.51: Im od rabbot bashanim: if many years remain, the redemption payment is proportionally larger — 'from the money of his purchase he shall refund a proportionate portion as redemption.' The graduated redemption price (lafi shanim = according to years) treats the original purchase as a future-labor annuity. Each year of service is worth one fraction of the total; years already worked are paid off; years remaining are what the buyer loses through redemption. The formula is transparent and calculable.
25.52: Ve'im me'at nish'ar bishnot: if few years remain until the jubilee, the calculation produces a small redemption payment. The asymmetric redemption pricing (large early, small late) creates an economic incentive for the family to redeem early when the cost is high: the longer they wait, the less they pay — but also the fewer years their kinsman is freed early. The system incentivizes early redemption without making late redemption prohibitively expensive.
25.53: Keshkir shanah beshanah yihyeh imo: 'he shall be with him as a worker hired year by year.' The Israelite self-sold to a foreigner retains the legal status of an annual contract worker — not a permanent slave. Lo yirdenu befarekh le'einekha: 'he shall not rule over him harshly in your sight.' The Israelite community is charged with oversight (le'einekha = in your eyes/sight) of how a kinsman in foreign service is treated. Social accountability extends to monitoring the treatment of Israelites working for resident aliens.
25.54: Ve'im lo yiggal be'eleh: 'if he is not redeemed in any of these ways, he goes free in the year of jubilee — he and his children with him.' The jubilee is the absolute floor: regardless of the failure of all redemption mechanisms, the jubilee releases every Israelite from bonded service. The jubilee cannot be contracted away: no agreement between buyer and seller can override it. It is YHWH's structural guarantee for every Israelite, operative regardless of any human transaction.
25.55: Ki li bene Yisrael avadim avadai hem asher hotzeti otam me'eretz Mitzrayim: 'For to me the Israelites are servants; they are my servants whom I brought out of the land of Egypt: I am YHWH your God.' The chapter closes with the Exodus-ownership declaration. The jubilee system's entire logic rests on this foundation: YHWH owns every Israelite because he liberated them from Egypt. His ownership right on them is prior to and stronger than any human economic claim. The jubilee is not social generosity but YHWH's reclamation of what has always been his.

26.1: lo taasu lachem elilim: you shall not make for yourselves idols (elilim). The Holiness Code's climax (ch. 26) opens with a prohibition on idolatry. Elilim (from el = god, but diminutive/pejorative — worthless things, nothings) is distinct from pesel (carved image) or asherah (fertility pole). The placement of this prohibition at the head of the covenant consequence chapter signals that idolatry is the fundamental covenant violation — the root cause of all the curses that follow. Pesel (carved idol) and matsevah (standing pillar) are also prohibited, along with bowing down to any figured stone (even maskiyt). The triple prohibition covers the full range of idolatrous worship forms.
26.2: shabbetotai tishmoru umikdashi tirau: you shall keep my Sabbaths and reverence my sanctuary. The Sabbath command and sanctuary reverence close the prohibition section and immediately precede the covenant consequences. The bracketing of the covenant laws with Sabbath (the time-marker) and sanctuary (the space-marker) emphasizes that covenant faithfulness is expressed in both temporal and spatial dimensions — how Israel orders its time and how it treats God's dwelling place. The double command provides the positive foundation for what follows: if these are kept, blessing follows; if violated, curse.
26.3: im bechukotai telechu: if you walk (telechu, from halak) in my statutes. The conditional structure of the covenant blessings opens with the most comprehensive formulation: walking (halak) in the statutes. Halak as a covenantal term describes the ongoing movement of a life — not one-time decisions but the habitual direction of daily conduct. Chukotai (my statutes, from chaq = to engrave, to inscribe) — the statutes are inscribed by divine authority, not negotiated. The conditional if establishes the covenant as a bilateral relationship: YHWH's blessings are real but not unconditional.
26.4: venatatai geshmeihem beitam: I will give your rains in their season (beitam = at their proper time). The first blessing is agricultural: seasonal rain. In the ANE, rain at the right time was the primary indicator of divine favor — drought signaled divine disfavor. The connection between covenant faithfulness and agricultural fertility is not primitive causalism but covenant theology: the land is part of the covenant gift, and its fruitfulness is tied to the covenant relationship. The Deuteronomic parallels (Deut 11:14; 28:12) confirm this is standard covenant framework, not coincidental correlation.
26.5: vehissig lachem dayish et-batzir uvatzir yassig et-zara: the threshing shall last until the harvest, and the harvest until sowing. Abundant harvest is described as continuous: one season's work overlaps the next because the yield is so great. This hyperbolic abundance is the covenant blessing imagination at full stretch — not just enough but overflowing. The image reverses Amos 9:13 (eschatological restoration) and is fulfilled in the new creation's abundance.
26.6: venatatai shalom baaretz: I will give peace (shalom) in the land. Shalom (wholeness, wellbeing, peace) encompasses security from predators, freedom from war, and social harmony — the full flourishing of communal life. The removal of harmful beasts (chayah raah) and the lying-down-in-safety image describe a restored Eden-like condition. Shalom is the covenant's comprehensive goal: not merely absence of conflict but the positive flourishing of all that God intended in creation.
26.7: uredeftem et-oiveichem venapelu lifneichem lacherev: you shall pursue your enemies and they shall fall before you by the sword. Military victory as covenant blessing — not aggression but the ability to defend and prevail. The pursuit (radaf) rather than mere resistance implies initiative: the covenant community does not merely hold its ground but advances when threatened.
26.8: vechamisha mikem yirdfu meah umeah mikem revavah yirdofu: five of you shall chase a hundred, and a hundred of you shall chase ten thousand. The asymmetric military blessing — disproportionate victory — is a hallmark of divine warfare theology. The exponential ratio (5:100 then 100:10,000 = 20:1 then 100:1) describes not just success but overwhelming, miraculous victory. Gideon's 300 against 135,000 (Judg 7-8) is the classic narrative fulfillment of this promise.
26.9: uphariti etchem vehirbeti etchem: I will turn to you and make you fruitful and multiply you. Panah (to turn to, face toward) — YHWH's face/attention directed toward Israel is the source of all blessing. The fruitfulness and multiplication echo the Adamic and Abrahamic promises (Gen 1:28; 17:6) — covenant faithfulness is the path to fulfilling the creation mandate. Vahakimoti et-briti itchem: I will establish (hekim) my covenant with you — not make a new covenant but confirm/uphold the existing one. Covenant-keeping by Israel does not create the covenant; it activates its promised consequences.
26.10: vaachaltem yashen noshan venoshan mipnei chadash totziu: you shall eat old grain long stored, and you shall clear out the old to make way for the new. The abundance is so great that old stores must be discarded to make room for new harvests. This is the blessing of superabundance — not scarcity management but generous overflow. The image requires Israel to imagine a problem of excess rather than deficit.
26.11: venatati mishkani betochechem velo-tigal nafshi etchem: I will place my dwelling (mishkan) among you, and my soul shall not loathe you. Mishkan (dwelling/tabernacle, from shakan = to dwell/abide) — the divine dwelling among Israel is the crown of all blessings. Everything else flows from this: YHWH's presence in the midst of his people is the source of rain, harvest, peace, and victory. Lo-tigal nafshi etchem (my soul shall not reject/loathe you) — the negative formulation emphasizes the fragility of relationship: divine disgust would be the worst possible outcome, removing the presence that sustains everything.
26.12: vehithalachti betochechem vehayiti lachem lelohim veatem tihyu-li leam: I will walk among you (hithalachti, hithpael of halak = to walk back and forth, to be present in ongoing movement) and will be your God, and you shall be my people. The covenant formula (ani YHWH eloheichem) in its fullest expression. Hithalachti (walking-about, being present in ongoing relational movement) echoes God walking in the garden (Gen 3:8, mithalech = same hithpael form) — covenant life restores Eden's intimacy. Paul quotes this verse in 2 Cor 6:16 as the basis for the church as the temple of the living God.
26.13: anochi YHWH eloheichem asher hotzeiti etchem meeretz mitzraim: I am YHWH your God who brought you out of the land of Egypt — the exodus-redemption warrant that grounds all covenant obligation. The covenant blessings are not offered to strangers but to those already redeemed. Vaeshbor motot ulchem vaolech etchem komemiyut: I broke the bars of your yoke and made you walk upright (komemiyut = erect, with dignity). Komemiyut appears only here — the upright posture of those freed from slavery's crouch. The Exodus is not merely release but dignity-restoration.
26.14: ve-im lo tishmehu li: but if you do not listen to me (shama = to hear, to obey). The curse section begins. Shama in covenant context encompasses both hearing and responding — the Hebrew does not distinguish passive hearing from active obedience. Not-listening is not a cognitive failure but a covenantal one: refusing to respond to what is heard.
26.15: ve-im bechukotai timasu: if you reject (maas) my statutes. Maas (to reject, despise, refuse) is a strong volitional term — not passive neglect but active repudiation. Paired with tikgeah nafshchem et-mishpatai (your soul abhors my judgments) — the escalation from not-listening (v14) to rejecting statutes (v15a) to the soul abhoring judgments (v15b) describes a progressive hardening of resistance. Lefru et-briti (to break my covenant) — covenant-breaking is the culmination of the sequence.
26.16: af-ani eseh-zot lachem: then I also will do this to you — the divine af (also, indeed, even) introducing the curse sequence. Behalah (terror, sudden dread) is the first curse — psychological collapse. Shachefet (wasting disease, consumption) and qaddachat (fever, burning inflammation) — specific diseases with Mesopotamian parallels. The counterpart of covenant blessing's abundance is the withering of the body and the harvest: mazre kom zarchem larik (your sowing shall be in vain, for enemies will eat it). The same agricultural framework as the blessings, now reversed.
26.17: venatati fanai bachem veningaftem lifnei oiveichem: I will set my face against you (natati fanai bachem) and you shall be struck before your enemies. The divine face, which was the source of blessing when turned toward (v9: uphariti etchem), becomes the source of curse when turned against. Natati panai as covenant curse is the exact reversal of the Aaronic blessing (Num 6:25: YHWH lift his face to you). The enemies who were fleeing from Israel (vv. 7-8) now become the ones before whom Israel flees.
26.18: ve-im ad eleh lo tishmehu li: and if in spite of all this you will not listen to me, I will discipline you seven times more for your sins. The escalation structure of Lev 26 is unique in the OT: four escalating rounds of punishment, each introduced by the if-not-listen refrain. Sheva al-chatoteichem (seven times for your sins) — the sevenfold intensification is not arithmetical but signifies completeness of judgment, the full covenant curse without mitigation.
26.19: veshavarti et-geun uzechem: I will break the pride of your power (geun uzechem). Gaon (pride, majesty) here refers not to sinful arrogance but to national strength and dignity — the very qualities the blessing of v13 restored (komemiyut). The breaking of this dignity is the reversal of the Exodus-dignity. Venatati et-shameichem kabarzel veet-artzchem kanechoset: I will make your heavens like iron and your earth like bronze — the sky that should give rain becomes impenetrable; the earth that should yield crops becomes hard and unyielding. The inversion of the rain-blessing (v4) as an image of covenant withdrawal.
26.20: vetam lashav kochechem: and your strength shall be spent in vain. Agricultural effort without yield — the curse of futility. Even when Israel works, there is no result: the labor is real but the harvest is empty. This is the deep dread of the ANE farmer whose entire economy depends on productive crops. The irony is not that Israel is lazy but that diligence produces nothing under covenant curse.
26.21: ve-im telechu imi keri: if you walk contrary to me in keri. Keri (from qarah = to meet/happen) is used uniquely in Lev 26 (vv. 21, 23, 24, 27, 28, 40, 41) — 7 occurrences in this chapter only. Its meaning is debated: chance/accident (treating covenant as random event), hostility/opposition, or stubbornness/perversity. The common denominator is treating YHWH's discipline as coincidental rather than covenantal — failing to recognize divine intention in adversity. This refusal to read history theologically is itself a sin — the sin of treating YHWH as absent or irrelevant.
26.22: veshilachti bachem et-chayyat hasadeh: I will send wild animals against you (chayyat hasadeh = the beasts of the field). The reversal of the peace-blessing of v6 (harmful beasts removed). Vishikla etchem vechirita et-behemtechem vehimit'ata etchem veshamemu darkeichem: they will bereave you and destroy your livestock and make you few, and your roads will be desolate. The progressive devastation — children, livestock, population, travel routes — describes the spiral of agricultural and social collapse.
26.23: ve-im-eleh lo tivaseiru li vhalachtem imi keri: if by these things you are not corrected but still walk contrary to me in keri. Vasar (to discipline, instruct, correct) — divine punishment is simultaneously pedagogical: the purpose of the curses is correction, not destruction. The recurrence of keri (for the third time) emphasizes that the sin being judged is not just disobedience but the refusal to recognize and respond to correction.
26.24: vehalachti af-ani imchem bekeri vehikeiti etchem af-ani sheva al-chatoteichem: then I will walk contrary (keri) to you — I also — and I will strike you seven times for your sins. YHWH meets keri with keri: Israel's refusal to treat YHWH as covenantally present is met with YHWH treating Israel with the same reciprocal distance. This is the covenant's dark logic: God honors the mode of relationship Israel chooses — if Israel treats the covenant as irrelevant, YHWH acts accordingly.
26.25: vehaveiti aleichem cherev nokat niqam brit: I will bring a sword upon you, the avenger of covenant vengeance (niqam brit). Niqam brit (covenant vengeance/avengement) — the sword is not arbitrary violence but the covenant's enforcement mechanism. Naqam (vengeance, retribution) in covenant context refers to the covenant's self-executing consequence clause: the curses function as stated. Dever (plague/pestilence) in the besieged cities completes the sword-and-plague combination that recurs in prophetic judgment oracles (Jer 14:12; Ezek 6:11-12).
26.26: beshivri lachem mateh lechem: when I break the staff of bread (mateh lechem) for you. Mateh (staff, rod) metaphorically describes bread as the staff that supports human life — the basis of strength and survival. Breaking the staff of bread = removing the sustaining supply. The rationing of bread by weight in a single oven is the picture of extreme scarcity — the inverse of the abundance-overflow of v10. Ten women baking for a whole household in one small oven, and the bread not satisfying.
26.27: ve-im bezot lo tishmehu li vehalachtem imi bekeri: if in spite of this you will not listen to me but walk contrary to me in keri — the fourth and final escalation trigger. The persistent keri, now after four rounds of escalating discipline, reaches the point of final, comprehensive judgment.
26.28: vehalachti imchem bachamat-keri: then I will walk with you in the fury of keri (bachamat-keri) — my burning anger in keri-mode. The divine keri, previously described as reciprocal detachment, now becomes charged with chemah (fury, heat, burning wrath) — the full intensity of divine judgment. This is the highest escalation in the series.
26.29: vaachaltem besar beneichem uvesar benoteichem tochelu: you shall eat the flesh of your sons, and the flesh of your daughters you shall eat. Cannibalism during siege — the most terrible curse imaginable in the ANE. This is not rhetorical exaggeration: the siege of Jerusalem (2 Kgs 6:28-29; Lam 4:10) records exactly this. Lamentations 4:10 quotes this horror as fulfilled reality. The reversal of the parental love that lies at the heart of social bonds represents the total collapse of covenant community into self-devouring chaos.
26.30: vehishmadeti et-bamoteichem: I will destroy your high places (bamot). Bamot (high places) — the sites of illicit worship that were the primary expression of keri (treating YHWH as one among many). Vegidate et-hammoteichem: I will cut down your incense altars (chammanim). Chammanim (incense stands, related to chamah = sun) — possibly sun-worship altars. The destruction of the worship infrastructure that replaced YHWH is itself the judgment: the objects of misplaced devotion are destroyed in the punishment for misplaced devotion. Venafonati et-pigreichem al-pigrei giluleichem: I will throw your corpses upon the corpses of your idols (gilulim). Gilulim (a uniquely derogatory term for idols, possibly related to galal = dung-balls) — a term of contempt. Israel and its idols share the same fate: corpse upon corpse.
26.31: venatati et-areichem chorba: I will make your cities a desolation (chorbah, from charev = to be dry, waste). Vahahsimoti et-mikdesheichem: I will make your sanctuaries desolate (hashimmoti). The divine abandonment of the sanctuaries — making holy places into ruins — is the extreme reversal of v11-12 (I will set my mishkan among you, I will walk among you). God removing his presence from his own house is the covenant curse's theological climax: worse than military defeat or famine is divine absence.
26.32: vahasimoti ani et-haaretz: and I myself (ani emphatic) will make the land desolate. The divine first-person emphasis underscores that the devastation is not accidental but intentional and judicial. Veshamemu aleha oiveichem: your enemies who settle in it shall be appalled at it — even the conquerors find the land so destroyed that they are horrified rather than pleased with their acquisition. The land's desolation exceeds the expectations of conquest.
26.33: veetchem ezareh vagoyim: and I will scatter you among the nations (ezareh vagoyim). Zarah (to scatter, to winnow) — the image of winnowing (separating grain from chaff by scattering in the wind) applied to Israel among the nations. Vahrikoti achareichem cherev: I will unsheathe the sword after you (vahrikoti = to draw out, bare). The sword pursues the scattered. Vehayetah artzechem shemamah vearekeichem yihyu chorba: your land shall be desolation and your cities shall be ruins — the land-curse expressed in its final and most complete form.
26.34: az tirtzeh haaretz et-shabbetoteha kol yemei hashemamah: then the land shall enjoy (tirtzeh, from ratzah = to be pleased with, to accept favorably) its Sabbaths all the days it lies desolate. The land gets the Sabbath rest it was denied when Israel worked it continuously in covenant violation. Ratzah is a key priestly term for acceptance of sacrifice (the offering being favorably received by YHWH) — here the desolate land is an offering of Sabbath rest. 2 Chronicles 36:21 cites this verse as fulfilled in the Babylonian exile: 70 years of exile for the Sabbaths the land was denied (assuming 490 years of violation).
26.35: kol yemei hashemamah tishbot et asher lo shavtah beshabbetoteichem beshivtechem aleha: all the days it lies desolate it shall rest — what it did not rest during your sabbatical years while you were dwelling in it. The Sabbath theology of the land (Lev 25:1-7) is here enforced retroactively by exile: the land keeps the years it was owed. This is not merely agricultural reasoning but covenant justice — YHWH ensures that the Sabbath year commands are fulfilled even if it requires removing Israel from the land to accomplish it.
26.36: vehannish'arim bachem vehaveti morekh bilevavam beertzot oiveichem: and as for those of you who survive, I will send faintness (morekh = cowardice, despair) into their hearts in the lands of their enemies. Morekh (from rakah = to be tender/soft/fearful) is the opposite of chazaq lev (strength/courage). The psychological devastation of exile — the loss of identity, the sense of divine abandonment — produces the faintheartedness described here. Venas etchem kekol aleh nidaf: they shall flee as though fleeing from a sword, though no sword pursues — paranoid flight with no objective cause, the psychological breakdown of those under covenant curse.
26.37: vechishot ish beauchiv kemipnei-cherev verodef ayin: they shall stumble over one another, as if to escape a sword, though none pursues. Social disintegration: the exiles trip over each other in their panic. Veloh-tihyeh lachem teqomah lifnei oiveichem: and you shall have no power to stand before your enemies. The military collapse is complete — not just defeat but inability even to take a stance. The covenant-curse has unmade the warrior nation of v7-8.
26.38: avadetm bagoyim vaacelah etchem eretz oiveichem: you shall perish among the nations, and the land of your enemies shall eat you up. Aved (to perish, be lost) — the scattering (v33) leads to absorption and disappearance among the nations. Tochel (to eat, consume) — the land devours its inhabitants. The verb reverses the eating of the land's produce in the blessings: instead of Israel eating the land's fruit (v10), the land of enemies eats Israel.
26.39: vehannish'arim bachem yimmaqequ beavonam beertzot oiveichem: and those who survive shall rot away (yimmaqequ, from maqaq = to rot, dissolve, waste away) in the iniquities of their fathers along with them they shall rot away. The exile generation inherits and shares in the consequences of their ancestors' covenant violations. This is not arbitrary: the exiles in Babylon discovered themselves implicated in the long trajectory of Israel's covenant unfaithfulness. Ezekiel addresses this explicitly (Ezek 18) — while individuals bear their own responsibility, the national consequence is multigenerational.
26.40: vehitvadu et-avonam veet-avon avotam: they shall confess their iniquity and the iniquity of their fathers. Yadah (hithpael = to confess, acknowledge) — voluntary, first-person acknowledgment of sin. The path out of exile begins with confession: specifically confessing maalam asher malelu bi veasher halchu imi bekeri (their treachery in which they acted faithlessly against me, and also that they walked contrary to me in keri). The keri that caused the exile must be named and acknowledged before restoration can begin. Daniel 9 and Nehemiah 1 are explicit examples of exactly this confession following exile.
26.41: af-ani alechem bekeri veheve'ti otam beeretz oiveichem: I also walked contrary to them in keri and brought them to the land of their enemies. The divine keri (v24, v28) is here retrospectively acknowledged as what happened: YHWH's keri-response to Israel's keri was precisely the exile. Az yikkana levavam heorel: then their uncircumcised heart shall be humbled (yikkana, from kana = to be subdued, humbled). Lev arel (uncircumcised heart) — the heart that has not been cut open by the covenant's mark. Jeremiah 4:4 and Deut 10:16; 30:6 use the same idiom: the external sign of circumcision is meant to correspond to an interior cutting-open of the heart. The exile humbles the uncircumcised heart into the confessing heart of v40.
26.42: vezecharti et-briti yaaqov vaf-et-briti yitzcheq vaf-et-briti avraham ezkor vehaaretz ezkor: I will remember my covenant with Jacob, and also my covenant with Isaac, and also my covenant with Abraham I will remember, and the land I will remember. The triple covenant-remembrance formula is the theological turning point of the chapter: after the full deployment of the curses, YHWH's initiative in remembering — not waiting for Israel to earn restoration — provides the basis for hope. Zakar berit (to remember the covenant) is a key theological act in Scripture (Gen 9:15; Exod 2:24; 6:5). The order is reversed (Jacob-Isaac-Abraham) — starting with the most recent, moving to the foundational. The land is remembered alongside the people: the land's restoration is part of the covenant promise.
26.43: vehaaretz te'azev mehem vitretzeh et-shabbetoteha beshomemah mehem: the land shall be abandoned by them and shall enjoy its Sabbaths while it lies desolate without them. The land's Sabbath-rest (v34-35) is repeated here, but now in the context of restoration hope — the Sabbath-rest of the land during exile is not only punishment but preparation, making the land ready to receive Israel again. Vehem yirtzeh et-avonam: and they shall make amends for their iniquity — ratzah in the hiphil sense of bearing penalty and having it accepted. The satisfaction of covenant penalty creates the condition for restoration.
26.44: veaf gam-zot biheyotam beeretz oiveichem lo meatiytim velo gealtim lechalotam lehafar briti itam: yet for all that, when they are in the land of their enemies, I will not spurn them or loathe them so as to destroy them utterly, to break my covenant with them. The final theological statement of the chapter: despite exile, YHWH will not make a kallah (complete destruction) of Israel. The double negatives (lo meatiytim velo gealtim) — I will not reject, I will not abhor — are the exile's last line of defense against despair. The covenant-destruction that would make Israel like the nations is what YHWH explicitly refuses. Ki ani YHWH eloheichem: for I am YHWH their God — the divine self-identification that grounds the irrevocable nature of the covenant.
26.45: vezecharti lahem brit rishonim: I will remember for them the covenant of the ancestors (brit rishonim), whom I brought out of the land of Egypt before the eyes of the nations, to be their God — I am YHWH. The covenant with the ancestors (rishonim = first ones, founders) — the patriarchal covenant — is the oldest and most foundational. Its remembered quality (zecharti) ensures that exile cannot be the final chapter: the covenant made before the nation existed cannot be unmade by the nation's failure. The Exodus-authorization (asher hotzeti otam meeretz mitzraim) re-grounds the covenant in the prior act of redemption that called Israel into existence.
26.46: eleh hachukim vehamishpatim vehatorah: these are the statutes, the rules, and the laws (torah). The chapter closes with a summary formula: the three legal categories — chukim (statutes, things inscribed by divine authority), mishpatim (judgments, case-law precedents), and torah (instruction, teaching as a whole) — together encompass the full body of covenant law delivered to Moses on Mount Sinai for the people of Israel. The formal closure identifies the entire Holiness Code (chs. 17-26) as a unified legal corpus with Sinai authority.

27.1: Chapter 27 is a legal appendix to the Holiness Code, addressing the commutation of dedicated persons and objects. YHWH speaks to Moses with the familiar introductory formula.
27.2: ish ki yafle neder beerekcha nefashot l-YHWH: when a person makes a special vow (yafle neder) involving persons at your valuation (beerekcha) to YHWH. Pala (in the hiphil: to make extraordinary, to set apart by vow) — a special/extraordinary vow as distinct from regular offerings. Erekh (valuation, assessed value) is the central term of ch. 27: the monetary value assigned to persons or things dedicated to YHWH by vow. The erekh system provides a mechanism for commuting the dedication of persons into money payments, allowing the person to continue normal life while fulfilling the vow financially.
27.3: vehayah erkecha hazachar miben esrim shanah vead ben-shishim shanah: the valuation for a male from 20 to 60 years old shall be 50 shekels of silver. The erekh schedule by age and gender: males 20-60 = 50 shekels. The scale reflects productive economic capacity — the peak working years carry the highest valuation. This is not a measure of human worth before God but a practical financial schedule for commuting vows in a subsistence economy.
27.4: ve-im neqevah hi vehayah erkecha sheloshim shoqel: if female, the valuation shall be 30 shekels. The female valuation (30 shekels, 20-60 years) reflects the economic productivity differential in an agricultural society. This is an economic assessment, not a theological statement about the equal dignity of women before God (cf. Gal 3:28 for the new covenant's transformation).
27.5: ve-im miben-chamesh shanim vead ben-esrim shanah: from 5 to 20 years: male 20 shekels, female 10 shekels. The reduced valuations for younger persons reflect lower economic productivity. The age-brackets (birth-1 month, 1-5 years, 5-20, 20-60, 60+) provide a comprehensive schedule covering the entire life cycle.
27.6: ve-im miben-chodesh vead ben-chamesh shanim vehayah erkecha hazachar chamisha sheqalim kesef: from one month to five years: male 5 shekels, female 3 shekels. The inclusion of infants (from one month) in the valuation schedule shows that the vow system covers even the dedication of newborns (cf. Hannah's dedication of Samuel, 1 Sam 1:11). The 1-month threshold corresponds to the age at which first-born sons are redeemed (Num 18:16), suggesting a connection between these systems.
27.7: ve-im miben-shishim shanah vamaalah im zachar: if 60 years and above: male 15 shekels, female 10 shekels. The elderly receive lower valuations — reduced economic productivity in old age. The entire schedule from birth through old age demonstrates that the vow system was designed to be comprehensive and practically workable rather than a burden that could not be met.
27.8: ve-im mak hu meerecha vehemido lifnei hakkohen veherimu hakkohen: if the person is too poor (mak = reduced, impoverished) to pay the valuation, he shall be presented before the priest, who shall assess him. Mak (from muq = to be dissolved, weakened, reduced) describes economic reduction below the ability to meet the standard valuation. The priest then sets a proportional amount based on what the person can afford — al pi asher tasig yad hannoder (according to what the hand of the vow-maker can reach). This income-proportional adjustment is a built-in equity mechanism: the vow system must not be so burdensome that poor Israelites cannot participate in worship.
27.9: ve-im behemah asher yaqrivu mimena qorban l-YHWH kol asher yitten mimena l-YHWH yihyeh qodesh: if the vow concerns livestock fit for offering to YHWH, everything given to YHWH shall be holy. Qorban (offering, from qarav = to bring near) — any dedicated animal becomes qodesh (holy/set apart) the moment it is designated for YHWH. The holiness is not achieved by the act of sacrifice but by the act of dedication.
27.10: lo yachaliyfenu velo yamir oto tov bera o ra betov veim hamir yamir beheimah beheimah vehayah-hu utemurato yihyeh qodesh: he shall not exchange or substitute (yachaliyfenu... yamir) it, good for bad or bad for good; if he should substitute one animal for another, both it and its substitute shall be holy. The anti-substitution rule protects the integrity of the dedication system: once an animal is designated for YHWH, it cannot be swapped out for a lesser animal. The attempted substitution makes both animals holy — a disincentive that discourages manipulation of the vow system.
27.11: ve-im kol behemah temeh asher lo yaqrivu mimena qorban l-YHWH: and if it is an unclean animal, not fit for offering to YHWH — the person shall present the animal before the priest. Unclean animals cannot be sacrificed but can still be the subject of dedication vows. The priest values them, and the person may redeem them by paying the valuation plus one-fifth (chamishito). The redemption-plus-fifth (20% surcharge) is a recurring feature of the ch. 27 redemption system.
27.12: veherimu hakkohen bein tov uvein ra: the priest shall value it between good and bad — at whatever valuation the priest sets, that it shall be. The priestly valuation authority for unclean animals is absolute: kemerkah hakkohen ken yihyeh (as the priest values it, so it shall be).
27.13: ve-im ga'ol yig'alenu: and if he redeems it. Ga'al (to redeem, buy back) — the key term of the Levitical redemption system. Ga'al in Leviticus 25-27 covers the redemption of land, persons, and dedicated property. The same root underlies go'el (kinsman-redeemer) — the family member with both the right and obligation to buy back what a relative has lost. The theological extension to YHWH as Israel's go'el (Isa 41:14; 44:6; 48:17) makes the redemption system a type of divine redemption.
27.14: ve-ish ki yaqadish et-beito qodesh l-YHWH: when a man consecrates (yaqadish) his house as holy to YHWH — the priest values it as good or bad, and at the priest's valuation it shall stand. Houses can be dedicated and then redeemed; the 20% surcharge applies here as well.
27.15: ve-im hamaqadish yigal et-beito veyasaf chamishit kesef erkecha alav vehayah lo: if the consecrator redeems his house, he shall add a fifth to the valuation price and it shall be his. The redemption-with-surcharge rule ensures that dedicating and immediately redeeming is not a zero-cost exercise: there is a cost to changing one's mind about a dedication. The surcharge incentivizes completion of vows and discourages treating dedication as a reversible financial instrument.
27.16: ve-im misedeh achuzzato yaqadish ish l-YHWH: if a person consecrates to YHWH part of his ancestral land (sedeh achuzzato). Achuzzah (possession, inherited land, from achaz = to hold, take possession) — the ancestral land of the tribe. The valuation of ancestral land is connected to the Jubilee: the value is calculated by the number of years remaining until the Jubilee. Zerah seor (seed of barley/homer) is the unit of measurement — 1 homer of barley seed = 50 shekels for a full 50-year Jubilee period.
27.17: im mishenat hayovel yaqadish sadahu keerkecha yaqum: if he consecrates his field from the Jubilee year, the full valuation applies. The land-valuation system is explicitly linked to the Jubilee (Lev 25): the closer to the next Jubilee, the less the land is worth for valuation purposes, since the buyer only has use of it until the Jubilee year when it reverts to the original family.
27.18: ve-im achar hayovel yaqadish sadahu: if after the Jubilee he consecrates his field. The priest calculates the proportional value: the remaining years until the next Jubilee determine the price. This is a time-value-of-money calculation built into the Torah — the principle that future possession is worth less than present possession.
27.19: ve-im ga'ol yig'al et-hasadeh hamaqadish oto: if he who consecrated the field redeems it — he shall add a fifth to the valuation price and it shall remain his. The redemption option with surcharge applies to land as to houses. The redemption right belongs to the original owner (or family member), consistent with the Jubilee's principle of preserving ancestral inheritance.
27.20: ve-im lo yig'al et-hasadeh ve-im-machar et-hasadeh le-ish acher: if he does not redeem the field, or if he has sold the field to another person — it cannot be redeemed anymore. The forfeiture-if-sold clause: if the consecrated land is sold to a third party before being redeemed, it permanently becomes temple property rather than reverting to the original family at Jubilee. This rule prevents using the consecration-and-sale sequence as a way to alienate ancestral land permanently.
27.21: vehayah hasadeh betzeto bayovel qodesh l-YHWH kesadeh hachereem: when the field reverts in the Jubilee, it shall be holy to YHWH like a devoted field (sadeh hachereem) — it becomes the priest's property. Cherem (devoted thing, irrevocable dedication) is introduced here — the category of absolute dedication that cannot be redeemed. The transition from regular consecration to cherem occurs when the normal redemption pathway is blocked.
27.22: ve-im et-sedeh miqnato asher lo misedeh achuzzato yaqadish l-YHWH: if he consecrates to YHWH a field that he has purchased, which is not his ancestral field. Purchased land (as opposed to ancestral inheritance) is valued differently: it belongs to the original owner at the Jubilee regardless. The valuation runs to the Jubilee year, when the field reverts to its original owner.
27.23: vechishuv lo hakkohen et michsat haerkcha ad shenat hayovel: the priest shall calculate for him the amount of the valuation up to the Jubilee year. The priest as financial calculator — the technical role the priesthood plays in the complex redemption-and-valuation system. The exactness of the calculation (down to specific year) reflects the systemic precision of the Jubilee framework.
27.24: beshenat hayovel yashov hasadeh laasher qonahu meito laasher lo achuzzat haaretz: in the Jubilee year the field shall revert to him from whom it was bought, to whom the land belongs as a possession. The Jubilee's land-restoration principle (Lev 25:13) applies even to consecrated purchased land. The purchased field cannot be permanently alienated from its original owner by consecration any more than by sale.
27.25: vechol-erkecha yihyeh besheqel hakodesh: all valuations shall be according to the shekel of the sanctuary (sheqel hakodesh). Sheqel hakodesh (sanctuary/holy shekel) — a standardized unit of weight used for temple transactions, likely equivalent to 20 gerahs. The specification of the sanctuary shekel prevents monetary manipulation through use of lighter civil shekels. The sanctuary maintains its own weight standard — a form of financial integrity built into the system.
27.26: ach bechor asher yevukkar l-YHWH behemah lo yaqadish ish oto: but a firstborn of animals, which belongs to YHWH as a firstborn, no one may dedicate. Bechor (firstborn) animals already belong to YHWH by right of firstborn (Exod 13:2; Num 18:15-17) — they cannot be further dedicated because they are already YHWH's. You cannot give what you do not own. This rule clarifies the scope of the vow/dedication system: only what belongs to the individual can be dedicated.
27.27: ve-im babeheimah hatemeah ufadah veerkecha veyasaf chamishito alav: if it is an unclean animal, he shall redeem it at the valuation price plus one-fifth, or if it is not redeemed, it shall be sold at the valuation. The consistent surcharge-for-redemption rule applied to unclean firstborn animals. The 20% surcharge appears seven times in the chapter — its recurrence establishes it as the chapter's primary financial principle.
27.28: ach kol-cherem asher yacherim ish l-YHWH mikol-asher-lo me-adam ubehemah umisedeh achuzzato lo yimmacer velo yigael kol-cherem qodesh qadashim hu l-YHWH: but nothing that a person devotes (yacherim) to YHWH as an irrevocable devotion (cherem), whether man, animal, or inherited field, shall be sold or redeemed. Every cherem is most holy (qodesh qadashim) to YHWH. Cherem (from charam = to devote irrevocably, to put under the ban) is the most absolute form of dedication in the OT — unlike the erekh-valuations of the rest of the chapter, which can be redeemed with a surcharge, cherem is irrevocable. The same word describes the ban on Canaanite cities (Josh 6:17-21) and the devoted spoil of war. Its irrevocability means that cherem-dedicated persons face death (v29) — the person cannot be redeemed because they have been absolutely given over to divine disposal.
27.29: kol-cherem asher yucherem min-haadam lo yipadeh mot yumat: no devoted person (cherem min-haadam) who is irrevocably devoted shall be redeemed — he shall surely be put to death. The cherem-death penalty applies to human cherem-dedications. This verse has been interpreted variously: as referring to criminals condemned by the community and devoted to God's judgment; as referring to enemy warriors under the ban; or as the logical extreme of the cherem principle where absolute devotion to YHWH means the person is no longer available for any earthly use. The NT transformation of cherem-logic: Paul's anathema (from ana-thema = devotion/curse) in Gal 1:8-9 invokes the same absolute-devotion language, applied to those who pervert the gospel.
27.30: vechol-maaser haaretz mizera haaretz mipri haaetz l-YHWH hu qodesh l-YHWH: all the tithe of the land — whether of the seed of the land or of the fruit of the trees — is YHWH's; it is holy to YHWH. Maaser (tithe, tenth, from eser = ten) — the tenth portion that belongs to YHWH by right of his ownership of the land (Ps 24:1). The tithe is declared qodesh l-YHWH (holy to YHWH) — set apart, belonging to God's domain. The tithe is not an act of generosity but a recognition of ownership: the land is YHWH's, and the tithe acknowledges this. Malachi 3:10 builds on this: robbing YHWH of the tithe is robbing him of what is already his.
27.31: ve-im ga'ol yig'al ish mimaaasero chamishito yosif alav: if a person redeems any of his tithe, he shall add a fifth to it. The redemption-plus-fifth rule applies to tithes as well as dedicated property: if the tithe-payer wishes to keep the actual grain or fruit and pay money instead, the 20% surcharge applies. The surcharge makes cash-commutation slightly more expensive than actual tithe-payment, preserving the integrity of the tithing system.
27.32: vechol-maaser baqar vatzon kol asher-yaavor tachat hashavet haasiri yihyeh-qodesh l-YHWH: and every tithe of cattle and flock, every tenth animal that passes under the staff (yaavor tachat hashavet) — the tenth shall be holy to YHWH. The animal tithe is calculated by counting: every animal passes under a staff or rod, and every tenth animal is automatically designated holy. The counting method (not human selection) ensures that the tithe cannot be manipulated by selecting inferior animals — the tenth passes over regardless of quality. This is the mechanical tithe, not subject to the vow-with-valuation system.
27.33: lo yevaqer bein tov lera velo yamiyrenu: he shall not select between good and bad or substitute for it (lo yamiyrenu). The non-selection, non-substitution rule for animal tithes parallels the anti-substitution rule for dedicated animals (v10). The human tendency to keep the best and give the worst is explicitly prohibited: the tenth is the tenth regardless. Ve-im hamir yamiyrenu vehayah hu utemurato yihyeh qodesh lo yigael: if he does substitute, both it and its substitute shall be holy and shall not be redeemed. The same penalty as v10: attempted manipulation results in double dedication.
27.34: eleh hamitzvot asher tzivah YHWH et-Moshe el-benei Yisrael behar Sinai: these are the commandments that YHWH commanded Moses for the people of Israel on Mount Sinai. The closing formula of the entire book of Leviticus — Sinai as the authoritative source of all the laws in the book, including the legal appendix of ch. 27. The Sinai-authority formula brackets Leviticus as a unified corpus of divine instruction delivered at the mountain of the covenant.
